book.name: Exodus
book.author: Traditionally attributed to Moses; likely compiled from multiple sources over centuries
book.dateWritten: c. 1400-400 BC (composition and compilation span centuries)
book.writtenFrom: The wilderness of Sinai / later Israel
book.writtenTo: The people of Israel, to remember their liberation and covenant identity

explainer.overview: Exodus is the liberation narrative -- the story of how God heard the cries of an enslaved people and delivered them with a mighty hand. The book moves from slavery in Egypt through the ten plagues and Passover, across the Red Sea, to Mount Sinai, where God establishes a covenant with Israel and gives them the law, including the Ten Commandments. The final section details the construction of the Tabernacle -- God's dwelling place among his people. Exodus answers the question that Genesis left hanging: what happens to Abraham's family in Egypt? They become enslaved, and God rescues them. This rescue becomes the defining event of the Old Testament, referenced more than any other event in Scripture.
explainer.historicalContext: The Exodus occurs against the backdrop of the Egyptian New Kingdom, one of the most powerful empires in the ancient world. Egypt used forced labor extensively for building projects. The Israelites' slavery fits this pattern. The ten plagues systematically target the Egyptian gods: the Nile (Hapi), the sun (Ra), cattle (Hathor), and ultimately Pharaoh's own son (Pharaoh was considered divine). The Exodus is not just a political liberation but a theological confrontation between YHWH and the gods of Egypt.
explainer.whyItMatters: Exodus defines God's character: he hears the oppressed, acts in history, defeats empires, and makes covenant with freed slaves. The Passover, the Red Sea crossing, and the Sinai covenant become the theological bedrock of both Judaism and Christianity. Jewish identity is rooted in the Exodus -- every Passover Seder recounts this story. Christian theology reads the Exodus as a pattern for salvation: Christ is the Passover lamb, baptism is the Red Sea crossing, and the new covenant fulfills Sinai. Liberation theology draws on Exodus to argue that God always sides with the oppressed against their oppressors.
keyThemes: The confrontation between YHWH and Egypt's gods
The Ten Commandments as covenant stipulations
The faithfulness of God and the faithlessness of Israel
Moses as mediator between God and the people

people.name: Moses
people.role: Deliverer, lawgiver, mediator between God and Israel
people.bio: Born to Hebrew slaves, hidden in a basket on the Nile, adopted by Pharaoh's daughter, Moses grows up in the Egyptian palace with a foot in two worlds. He kills an Egyptian, flees to Midian, encounters God in a burning bush, and is drafted to lead the most consequential liberation in human history. He confronts Pharaoh, leads Israel through the Red Sea, receives the law on Sinai, and mediates between a holy God and a rebellious people. He is described as the most humble man on earth and the only person to speak with God face to face.
people.culturalContext: Moses' Egyptian education gave him the skills to lead a nation. His forty years in Midian as a shepherd prepared him for the patience required to lead Israel through the wilderness. He bridges the worlds of Egyptian power and Hebrew faith.

people.name: Pharaoh (of the Exodus)
people.role: King of Egypt who refused to let Israel go
people.bio: The unnamed Pharaoh of the Exodus is the primary antagonist -- a ruler who enslaves an entire people and refuses to release them even as his kingdom is destroyed plague by plague. His 'hardened heart' is one of the most debated theological concepts in the Bible: does God harden Pharaoh's heart, or does Pharaoh harden his own? The text says both, suggesting a complex interplay between divine sovereignty and human choice.
people.culturalContext: Pharaoh was considered a god in Egyptian religion -- the living incarnation of Horus and the son of Ra. The Exodus story is fundamentally a contest between the God of the slaves and the god-king of the empire. Every plague is an assault on Pharaoh's claimed divinity.

people.name: Aaron
people.role: Moses' brother, spokesman, first high priest of Israel
people.bio: Three years older than Moses, Aaron becomes his brother's mouthpiece before Pharaoh and his partner in leading Israel. He performs miraculous signs, accompanies Moses on Sinai, and is appointed as the first high priest. But he also fails catastrophically by making the golden calf when Moses is on the mountain, caving to popular pressure and constructing an idol. Aaron embodies both the promise and the peril of religious leadership.
people.culturalContext: The Aaronic priesthood established in Exodus becomes the hereditary priestly line of Israel, continuing for over a thousand years until the destruction of the Temple in 70 AD.

people.name: Miriam
people.bio: Miriam appears at critical moments: she watches over baby Moses in the Nile, arranges for his mother to nurse him, and after the Red Sea crossing, leads the women in a song of triumph. She is called a prophetess -- one of the few women given this title in the Old Testament.
people.culturalContext: In a patriarchal world, Miriam's leadership role was remarkable. She, Moses, and Aaron form a leadership triad. The prophet Micah later names all three as leaders God sent to deliver Israel (Micah 6:4).

people.name: Jethro (Reuel)
people.bio: A Midianite priest who takes Moses in during his exile, gives him his daughter Zipporah in marriage, and later provides crucial organizational advice that prevents Moses from burning out. He is one of the Bible's most positive portraits of a non-Israelite who recognizes and worships YHWH.
people.culturalContext: The Midianites were descendants of Abraham through Keturah (Genesis 25:2). Jethro's priestly role suggests that knowledge of God was not limited to Abraham's immediate covenant line.

places.name: Egypt
places.description: The superpower that enslaved Israel for four hundred years. The land of the Nile, the pyramids, and a civilization that worshipped hundreds of gods. In Exodus, Egypt represents the power of empire and the system of oppression that God defeats.
places.culturalSignificance: Egypt in the Bible is always ambivalent: a place of refuge (Abraham, Joseph, Jesus' family) and a place of slavery. It becomes the symbol of every form of bondage -- political, spiritual, economic -- from which God liberates his people.
places.modernLocation: Modern Egypt, particularly the Nile Delta region and the eastern frontier.

places.name: Mount Sinai (Horeb)
places.description: The mountain where God appeared to Moses in the burning bush and later descended in fire and smoke to give the law. It is the site of the most dramatic theophany (divine appearance) in the Old Testament.
places.culturalSignificance: Sinai is where Israel's covenant identity is formed. The Ten Commandments, the civil and ceremonial laws, and the Tabernacle instructions are all given here. For Israel, Sinai is the birthplace of the nation -- not just freed slaves but a covenanted people with a constitution.
places.modernLocation: Traditionally identified with Jebel Musa in the southern Sinai Peninsula, Egypt, though other locations have been proposed.

places.name: Red Sea (Sea of Reeds)
places.description: The body of water God parted to allow Israel to cross on dry land and then closed to destroy the pursuing Egyptian army. The Hebrew yam suph means 'Sea of Reeds,' and its exact location is debated.
places.culturalSignificance: The Red Sea crossing is the most celebrated miracle in the Old Testament. It becomes the paradigmatic act of divine salvation -- the moment God proved he could defeat the mightiest empire on earth to save his people. Every subsequent deliverance is compared to this one.
places.modernLocation: Debated. Candidates include the Gulf of Suez, the Gulf of Aqaba, the Bitter Lakes, or marshy areas in the eastern Nile Delta.

places.name: Goshen
places.description: The fertile region in the eastern Nile Delta where Jacob's family settled (Genesis 47) and where the Israelites lived during their time in Egypt. During the plagues, Goshen was supernaturally protected.
places.culturalSignificance: Goshen's separation from the rest of Egypt during the plagues demonstrated God's ability to distinguish between his people and their oppressors. It also kept Israel geographically distinct, preventing total assimilation into Egyptian culture.
places.modernLocation: The eastern Nile Delta region, near modern Zagazig, Egypt.

timeline.event: Moses flees to Midian after killing an Egyptian
God calls Moses at the burning bush
The first Passover; Israel leaves Egypt
Crossing of the Red Sea; destruction of the Egyptian army
Arrival at Mount Sinai; the Ten Commandments given
Israel enters the Promised Land under Joshua

explainer.summary: Genesis ended with a coffin in Egypt; Exodus opens with a crisis. A new Pharaoh who 'did not know Joseph' rises to power and sees the growing Israelite population as a threat. He enslaves them with forced labor, building the store cities Pithom and Raamses. When slavery fails to slow their growth, Pharaoh orders the Hebrew midwives to kill every newborn boy. The midwives fear God more than Pharaoh and refuse. Pharaoh then orders all newborn Hebrew boys thrown into the Nile.
themes: The transition from favor to oppression
God blessing those who resist evil
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter answers the question Genesis raised: what happens to Abraham's family in Egypt? They grow enormously but are enslaved. The Exodus story begins not with a hero but with a crisis.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To oppress, humble, or cause to suffer
wordStudies.meaning: To oppress, humble, or cause to suffer. The same word used for the affliction God predicted in Genesis 15:13. Egypt fulfills the prophecy by imposing brutal forced labor on the Hebrews.

verses.text: Now these are the names of the sons of Israel, who came into Egypt (every man and his household came with Jacob):
verses.explainer: These are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt with Jacob. The opening connects directly to Genesis, continuing the same story.

verses.text: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah,
verses.explainer: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah.

verses.text: Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin,
verses.explainer: Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin.

verses.text: Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.
verses.explainer: Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.

verses.text: All the souls who came out of Jacob’s body were seventy souls, and Joseph was in Egypt already.
verses.explainer: Seventy persons came from Jacob's loins. Joseph was already in Egypt. The seventy recalls the Table of Nations (Genesis 10) -- Israel is a microcosm of humanity.

verses.text: Joseph died, as did all his brothers, and all that generation.
verses.explainer: Joseph died, and all his brothers, and all that generation. The generation that knew the story firsthand passes away. Memory fades.

verses.text: The children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly, and multiplied, and grew exceedingly mighty; and the land was filled with them.
verses.explainer: The Israelites are fruitful, increase abundantly, multiply, and grow exceedingly mighty. The land is filled with them. The language deliberately echoes Genesis 1:28 -- the creation mandate is being fulfilled, and it terrifies the empire.

verses.text: Now there arose a new king over Egypt, who didn’t know Joseph.
verses.explainer: A new king arises over Egypt who does not know Joseph. This single sentence marks the turning point. Political memory is short. The family that saved Egypt is forgotten.

thenVsNow.then: In Egyptian history, dynastic changes often meant complete breaks with previous policies. A new dynasty had no loyalty to arrangements made by predecessors. The Hyksos period and its aftermath may be the historical context.
thenVsNow.now: The pattern of a majority population fearing a growing minority and responding with oppression has repeated throughout history. The Exodus narrative has become a template for understanding every form of systemic oppression.

verses.text: He said to his people, “Behold, the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh tells his people: 'The Israelites have become too numerous and powerful for us.' Fear of the other drives the policy.

verses.text: Come, let us deal wisely with them, lest they multiply, and it happen that when any war breaks out, they also join themselves to our enemies, and fight against us, and escape out of the land.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh proposes dealing 'shrewdly' with them before they multiply further, join enemies in war, and escape. The language of national security masks genocidal intent.

verses.text: Therefore they set taskmasters over them to afflict them with their burdens. They built storage cities for Pharaoh: Pithom and Raamses.
verses.explainer: They set taskmasters over them to afflict them with heavy burdens. They build Pithom and Raamses as store cities for Pharaoh. Forced labor -- state-sponsored slavery -- becomes the tool of oppression.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Store cities', 'explanation': 'Pithom and Raamses were supply depots in the eastern Nile Delta, used for storing grain and military supplies. Archaeological evidence confirms extensive building projects in this region using forced labor, including mudbrick construction with and without straw.'}

verses.text: But the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and the more they spread out. They were grieved because of the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: But the more they are afflicted, the more they multiply and spread. Oppression backfires. The creation mandate cannot be stopped by human cruelty.

verses.text: The Egyptians ruthlessly made the children of Israel serve,
verses.explainer: The Egyptians ruthlessly make the Israelites serve.

verses.text: and they made their lives bitter with hard service, in mortar and in brick, and in all kinds of service in the field, all their service, in which they ruthlessly made them serve.
verses.explainer: They make their lives bitter with hard service in mortar and brick and all kinds of field work. The description is of grinding, dehumanizing labor.

verses.text: The king of Egypt spoke to the Hebrew midwives, of whom the name of the one was Shiphrah, and the name of the other Puah,
verses.explainer: Pharaoh speaks to the Hebrew midwives, Shiphrah and Puah. These two women are named; Pharaoh is not. The text values the midwives more than the king.

verses.text: and he said, “When you perform the duty of a midwife to the Hebrew women, and see them on the birth stool; if it is a son, then you shall kill him; but if it is a daughter, then she shall live.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh orders: when you deliver Hebrew women, if it is a son, kill him; if a daughter, let her live. Selective infanticide targeting males -- an attempt to control the population while preserving the female labor force.

verses.text: But the midwives feared God, and didn’t do what the king of Egypt commanded them, but saved the baby boys alive.
verses.explainer: But the midwives fear God and do not do as the king of Egypt commands them. They let the boys live. This is the first act of civil disobedience in the Bible. The midwives choose obedience to God over obedience to the state.

thenVsNow.then: Defying Pharaoh was a death sentence. The midwives' courage was extraordinary. They feared God more than they feared the most powerful ruler on earth.
thenVsNow.now: The midwives' resistance has inspired civil disobedience movements throughout history. Their example establishes the principle that there is a higher law than the state's commands, and that unjust orders must be disobeyed.

verses.text: The king of Egypt called for the midwives, and said to them, “Why have you done this thing, and have saved the boys alive?”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh summons the midwives: 'Why have you let the boys live?'

verses.text: The midwives said to Pharaoh, “Because the Hebrew women aren’t like the Egyptian women; for they are vigorous, and give birth before the midwife comes to them.”
verses.explainer: The midwives answer: 'Hebrew women are not like Egyptian women. They are vigorous and give birth before the midwife arrives.' They lie to Pharaoh. God rewards their defiance.

verses.text: God dealt well with the midwives, and the people multiplied, and grew very mighty.
verses.explainer: God deals well with the midwives. The people multiply and grow very strong.

verses.text: Because the midwives feared God, he gave them families.
verses.explainer: Because the midwives fear God, he gives them families of their own. God blesses those who resist evil, even when their methods are imperfect.

verses.text: Pharaoh commanded all his people, saying, “You shall cast every son who is born into the river, and every daughter you shall save alive.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh commands all his people: 'Every son that is born to the Hebrews you shall cast into the Nile.' The weapon of genocide is the very river that is Egypt's lifeblood. The Nile that gives life becomes an instrument of death.

explainer.summary: A Levite couple has a son and hides him for three months. When they can no longer conceal him, his mother places him in a waterproof basket on the Nile -- obeying Pharaoh's order technically (the boy goes into the Nile) while subverting its intent. Pharaoh's daughter finds the baby, names him Moses, and raises him as her own. As an adult, Moses kills an Egyptian who is beating a Hebrew, flees to Midian, marries Zipporah, and has a son. Meanwhile, God hears Israel's groaning and remembers his covenant.
themes: God hearing the cries of the oppressed
explainer.contextInBook: Moses' biography parallels Israel's story: both go down into the water and come out alive. Both are preserved by God's providence working through ordinary (and sometimes morally complex) human actions.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Pharaoh's daughter says she named him Moses 'becau
wordStudies.meaning: Pharaoh's daughter says she named him Moses 'because I drew him out of the water.' The Hebrew verb mashah means 'to draw out.' The name may also contain the Egyptian element -mose ('born of'), as in Thut-mose or Ra-meses. The deliverer's name comes from both cultures.

verses.text: A man of the house of Levi went and took a daughter of Levi as his wife.
verses.explainer: A man from the house of Levi marries a Levite woman. The parents are unnamed here (later identified as Amram and Jochebed in 6:20).

verses.text: The woman conceived, and bore a son. When she saw that he was a fine child, she hid him three months.
verses.explainer: She conceives, bears a son, sees that he is 'good,' and hides him three months. The word 'good' (tov) echoes creation: God saw that it was good. Moses' mother sees something in this child worth risking everything for.

verses.text: When she could no longer hide him, she took a papyrus basket for him, and coated it with tar and with pitch. She put the child in it, and laid it in the reeds by the river’s bank.
verses.explainer: When she can hide him no longer, she makes a papyrus basket, waterproofs it with bitumen and pitch, puts the child in it, and places it among the reeds of the Nile. The word for 'basket' (tevah) is the same word used for Noah's ark. Moses' mother builds a tiny ark to save her son from water, just as Noah's ark saved humanity.

verses.text: His sister stood far off, to see what would be done to him.
verses.explainer: His sister stations herself at a distance to see what will happen. This is likely Miriam, watching over her baby brother.

verses.text: Pharaoh’s daughter came down to bathe at the river. Her maidens walked along by the riverside. She saw the basket among the reeds, and sent her servant to get it.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh's daughter comes down to bathe at the Nile. She sees the basket among the reeds and sends her servant to get it.

thenVsNow.then: An Egyptian princess finding a Hebrew baby in the river was either an extraordinary coincidence or divine orchestration. The text implies the latter: God uses the daughter of the very king who ordered the boys killed to save the one who will liberate them.
thenVsNow.now: The irony of Pharaoh's own daughter rescuing the future deliverer from Pharaoh's own decree has captivated readers for millennia. God's providence works through the most unlikely channels.

verses.text: She opened it, and saw the child, and behold, the baby cried. She had compassion on him, and said, “This is one of the Hebrews’ children.”
verses.explainer: She opens the basket and sees the child crying. She has compassion: 'This is one of the Hebrews' children.' She knows exactly what this baby is and where it came from, yet her compassion overrides her father's decree.

verses.text: Then his sister said to Pharaoh’s daughter, “Should I go and call a nurse for you from the Hebrew women, that she may nurse the child for you?”
verses.explainer: The baby's sister asks: 'Shall I go and call a Hebrew woman to nurse the child for you?' Quick thinking under pressure.

verses.text:
Pharaoh’s daughter said to her, “Go.”
The young woman went and called the child’s mother.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh's daughter says: 'Go.' The girl goes and calls the child's mother.

verses.text:
Pharaoh’s daughter said to her, “Take this child away, and nurse him for me, and I will give you your wages.”
The woman took the child, and nursed it.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh's daughter tells the mother: 'Take this child and nurse him for me, and I will give you your wages.' Moses' mother is paid by Pharaoh's household to nurse her own son. The irony is exquisite.

verses.text: The child grew, and she brought him to Pharaoh’s daughter, and he became her son. She named him Moses, and said, “Because I drew him out of the water.”
verses.explainer: The child grows, and his mother brings him to Pharaoh's daughter, who adopts him as her son. She names him Moses: 'Because I drew him out of the water.' Moses grows up in the palace of the very empire that enslaved his people.

verses.text: In those days, when Moses had grown up, he went out to his brothers, and looked at their burdens. He saw an Egyptian striking a Hebrew, one of his brothers.
verses.explainer: When Moses has grown up, he goes out to his people and sees their hard labor. He sees an Egyptian beating a Hebrew, one of his own people. Despite his palace upbringing, Moses identifies with the Hebrews.

verses.text: He looked this way and that way, and when he saw that there was no one, he killed the Egyptian, and hid him in the sand.
verses.explainer: Moses looks around, sees no one, strikes the Egyptian, and hides him in the sand. Moses' first attempt at liberation is through violence. It fails.

verses.text: He went out the second day, and behold, two men of the Hebrews were fighting with each other. He said to him who did the wrong, “Why do you strike your fellow?”
verses.explainer: The next day, Moses sees two Hebrews fighting. He says to the one in the wrong: 'Why do you strike your companion?'

verses.text:
He said, “Who made you a prince and a judge over us? Do you plan to kill me, as you killed the Egyptian?”
Moses was afraid, and said, “Surely this thing is known.”
verses.explainer: The man replies: 'Who made you a prince and judge over us? Do you mean to kill me as you killed the Egyptian?' Moses is afraid and realizes his deed is known.

verses.text: Now when Pharaoh heard this thing, he sought to kill Moses. But Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh, and lived in the land of Midian, and he sat down by a well.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh hears and seeks to kill Moses. Moses flees to the land of Midian and sits down by a well. Wells in Genesis were places of encounter (Isaac, Jacob); Moses' encounter will come here too.

verses.text: Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters. They came and drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father’s flock.
verses.explainer: The priest of Midian has seven daughters who come to draw water and fill the troughs for their father's flock.

verses.text: The shepherds came and drove them away; but Moses stood up and helped them, and watered their flock.
verses.explainer: Shepherds come and drive the women away, but Moses stands up, rescues them, and waters their flock. Moses is still a defender of the vulnerable.

verses.text: When they came to Reuel, their father, he said, “How is it that you have returned so early today?”
verses.explainer: The daughters return to their father Reuel (also called Jethro). He asks why they have returned so soon.

verses.text: They said, “An Egyptian delivered us out of the hand of the shepherds, and moreover he drew water for us, and watered the flock.”
verses.explainer: They say: 'An Egyptian delivered us from the shepherds and even drew water for us.' They identify Moses as Egyptian -- he looks and acts like one.

verses.text: He said to his daughters, “Where is he? Why is it that you have left the man? Call him, that he may eat bread.”
verses.explainer: Reuel asks: 'Where is he? Why did you leave him? Invite him to eat.' Hospitality.

verses.text: Moses was content to dwell with the man. He gave Moses Zipporah, his daughter.
verses.explainer: Moses agrees to stay. Reuel gives him his daughter Zipporah in marriage.

verses.text: She bore a son, and he named him Gershom, for he said, “I have lived as a foreigner in a foreign land.”
verses.explainer: Zipporah bears a son, and Moses names him Gershom ('sojourner there'): 'I have been a sojourner in a foreign land.' Moses feels his exile.

verses.text: In the course of those many days, the king of Egypt died, and the children of Israel sighed because of the bondage, and they cried, and their cry came up to God because of the bondage.
verses.explainer: After a long time, the king of Egypt dies. The Israelites groan under their bondage and cry out. Their cry for help rises to God.

verses.text: God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.
verses.explainer: God hears their groaning and remembers his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. 'Remembers' (zakar) means God acts on the covenant. The groaning triggers the promise.

verses.text: God saw the children of Israel, and God was concerned about them.
verses.explainer: God sees the people of Israel, and God knows. He sees, he hears, he remembers, he knows. Four verbs of divine engagement. The God who seemed absent during the centuries of slavery has been paying attention all along.

explainer.summary: Moses, now eighty years old, is shepherding his father-in-law's flock near Mount Horeb (Sinai). He sees a bush that burns but is not consumed and turns aside to investigate. God speaks from the bush, identifies himself as the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and commissions Moses to liberate Israel. Moses asks God's name, and God reveals the most sacred name in the Bible: 'I AM WHO I AM' (YHWH). This is the call narrative that launches the Exodus.
themes: God's self-revelation in the burning bush
explainer.contextInBook: After forty years of preparation in Midian, Moses encounters the God who has been preparing him. The burning bush -- fire that does not destroy -- becomes a symbol of God's presence: powerful, purifying, and sustaining.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The most sacred name of God, revealed to Moses at
wordStudies.meaning: The most sacred name of God, revealed to Moses at the burning bush. 'I AM WHO I AM' (ehyeh asher ehyeh) is related to the verb 'to be' (hayah). It suggests self-existence, eternal presence, and dynamic faithfulness. God is not defined by anything outside himself -- he simply IS. Jews consider this name so holy that they never pronounce it, substituting 'Adonai' (Lord).

verses.text: Now Moses was keeping the flock of Jethro, his father-in-law, the priest of Midian, and he led the flock to the back of the wilderness, and came to God’s mountain, to Horeb.
verses.explainer: Moses is keeping the flock of Jethro, his father-in-law, the priest of Midian. He leads the flock to the far side of the wilderness and comes to Horeb, the mountain of God. The future deliverer is herding sheep in the middle of nowhere.

verses.text: Yahweh’s angel appeared to him in a flame of fire out of the middle of a bush. He looked, and behold, the bush burned with fire, and the bush was not consumed.
verses.explainer: The angel of the LORD appears to him in a flame of fire out of a bush. Moses looks: the bush is blazing but not consumed. Fire in the Bible represents God's presence -- purifying, powerful, and dangerous.

thenVsNow.then: Fire was associated with divine presence across the ancient Near East. But a fire that burns without consuming its fuel was unprecedented. It suggests a God whose power does not destroy but sustains.
thenVsNow.now: The burning bush has become one of the most iconic images in religious art and thought. It represents the paradox of divine presence: overwhelming holiness that does not obliterate what it touches.

verses.text: Moses said, “I will go now, and see this great sight, why the bush is not burned.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'I will turn aside and see this great sight -- why the bush is not burned.' Curiosity leads to encounter. God waits for Moses to notice and choose to investigate.

verses.text:
When Yahweh saw that he came over to see, God called to him out of the middle of the bush, and said, “Moses! Moses!”
He said, “Here I am.”
verses.explainer: When the LORD sees that Moses turns aside to see, God calls from the bush: 'Moses! Moses!' He answers: 'Here I am.' The double call signals urgency and intimacy. The hinneni (here I am) echoes Abraham and Jacob.

verses.text: He said, “Don’t come close. Take off your sandals, for the place you are standing on is holy ground.”
verses.explainer: God says: 'Do not come near; take your sandals off your feet, for the place on which you are standing is holy ground.' The ground is not inherently holy -- God's presence makes it so. Removing sandals is an act of reverence and vulnerability.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Removing sandals', 'explanation': "Taking off shoes signified reverence and humility before the divine. It also symbolized standing on ground that belonged to another -- you remove shoes when you enter someone else's space. This ordinary desert became sacred because God was there."}

verses.text:
Moreover he said, “I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.”
Moses hid his face; for he was afraid to look at God.
verses.explainer: God identifies himself: 'I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.' He connects to the covenant history. Moses hides his face because he is afraid to look at God.

verses.text: Yahweh said, “I have surely seen the affliction of my people who are in Egypt, and have heard their cry because of their taskmasters, for I know their sorrows.
verses.explainer: God says: 'I have surely seen the affliction of my people in Egypt and have heard their cry because of their taskmasters. I know their sufferings.' Three verbs: seen, heard, known. God is not distant or indifferent.

verses.text: I have come down to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians, and to bring them up out of that land to a good and large land, to a land flowing with milk and honey; to the place of the Canaanite, the Hittite, the Amorite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite.
verses.explainer: God says: 'I have come down to deliver them from the Egyptians and bring them to a good and broad land, a land flowing with milk and honey.' God comes down -- the same language used at Babel (Genesis 11:5). But this time he comes down to liberate, not to judge.

verses.text: Now, behold, the cry of the children of Israel has come to me. Moreover I have seen the oppression with which the Egyptians oppress them.
verses.explainer: God says: 'The cry of the people of Israel has come to me, and I have seen how the Egyptians oppress them.'

verses.text: Come now therefore, and I will send you to Pharaoh, that you may bring my people, the children of Israel, out of Egypt.”
verses.explainer: God says: 'Come, I will send you to Pharaoh that you may bring my people out of Egypt.' The commission is direct: you, Moses, will confront the most powerful ruler on earth and demand the release of his labor force.

verses.text: Moses said to God, “Who am I, that I should go to Pharaoh, and that I should bring the children of Israel out of Egypt?”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'Who am I that I should go to Pharaoh and bring the children of Israel out of Egypt?' The first of five objections. Moses is not being humble; he is terrified.

verses.text: He said, “Certainly I will be with you. This will be the token to you, that I have sent you: when you have brought the people out of Egypt, you shall serve God on this mountain.”
verses.explainer: God says: 'I will be with you, and this shall be the sign: when you have brought the people out of Egypt, you shall serve God on this mountain.' The sign comes after the obedience, not before. Faith precedes confirmation.

verses.text: Moses said to God, “Behold, when I come to the children of Israel, and tell them, ‘The God of your fathers has sent me to you;’ and they ask me, ‘What is his name?’ What should I tell them?”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'If I go to the Israelites and say the God of your fathers has sent me, and they ask his name, what shall I tell them?' In the ancient world, knowing a god's name meant knowing their character and having access to their power.

verses.text: God said to Moses, “I AM WHO I AM,” and he said, “You shall tell the children of Israel this: ‘I AM has sent me to you.’”
verses.explainer: God says: 'I AM WHO I AM.' Tell them 'I AM has sent me to you.' The divine name -- the most sacred utterance in the Hebrew Bible. It defies definition. God will not be captured in a name; he reveals himself through his actions.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient Near East, gods had specific names tied to specific functions and territories. The God of Israel refuses this categorization. He is not the god of one thing or one place -- he simply IS.
thenVsNow.now: The divine name YHWH has generated more theological reflection than perhaps any other word in the Bible. It grounds the entire Judeo-Christian understanding of God as self-existent, eternal, and radically free.

verses.text: God said moreover to Moses, “You shall tell the children of Israel this, ‘Yahweh, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has sent me to you.’ This is my name forever, and this is my memorial to all generations.
verses.explainer: God also says: 'Say to the people of Israel, The LORD (YHWH), the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, has sent me to you. This is my name forever.' The name YHWH is permanently linked to the covenant God of the patriarchs.

verses.text: Go and gather the elders of Israel together, and tell them, ‘Yahweh, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, of Isaac, and of Jacob, has appeared to me, saying, “I have surely visited you, and seen that which is done to you in Egypt;
verses.explainer: God instructs Moses to gather the elders of Israel and tell them: the LORD has visited them and seen what is being done to them in Egypt.

verses.text: and I have said, I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt to the land of the Canaanite, the Hittite, the Amorite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite, to a land flowing with milk and honey.”’
verses.explainer: God will bring them up from Egypt to a land flowing with milk and honey -- the land of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.

verses.text: They will listen to your voice, and you shall come, you and the elders of Israel, to the king of Egypt, and you shall tell him, ‘Yahweh, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. Now please let us go three days’ journey into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice to Yahweh, our God.’
verses.explainer: The elders will listen, and Moses and the elders will go to Pharaoh: 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. Let us go a three days' journey to sacrifice to the LORD our God.'

verses.text: I know that the king of Egypt won’t give you permission to go, no, not by a mighty hand.
verses.explainer: God tells Moses: 'I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go unless compelled by a mighty hand.' God knows the outcome before it begins.

verses.text: I will reach out my hand and strike Egypt with all my wonders which I will do among them, and after that he will let you go.
verses.explainer: God will stretch out his hand and strike Egypt with all his wonders. After that, Pharaoh will let them go.

verses.text: I will give this people favor in the sight of the Egyptians, and it will happen that when you go, you shall not go empty-handed.
verses.explainer: God will give the people favor in the Egyptians' eyes so they will not leave empty-handed.

verses.text: But every woman shall ask of her neighbor, and of her who visits her house, jewels of silver, jewels of gold, and clothing; and you shall put them on your sons, and on your daughters. You shall plunder the Egyptians.”
verses.explainer: Each woman will ask her Egyptian neighbor for silver, gold, and clothing. They will plunder the Egyptians. Centuries of unpaid slave labor will be compensated in a single night.

explainer.summary: Moses continues to resist God's call with objections: they will not believe me, I am not eloquent, please send someone else. God provides signs (a staff that becomes a serpent, a hand that becomes leprous) and assigns Aaron as Moses' spokesman. God's patience has a limit -- his anger burns at Moses' final refusal. Moses returns to Egypt with God's message. A strange incident occurs en route: God threatens to kill Moses, and Zipporah circumcises their son. Moses and Aaron gather the elders, perform the signs, and the people believe and worship.
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter completes Moses' call narrative. His reluctance is overcome not by logical argument but by God's patient provision of everything Moses says he lacks.

verses.text: Moses answered, “But, behold, they will not believe me, nor listen to my voice; for they will say, ‘Yahweh has not appeared to you.’”
verses.explainer: Moses answers: 'But they will not believe me or listen to me. They will say the LORD did not appear to you.' Second objection: credibility.

verses.text:
Yahweh said to him, “What is that in your hand?”
He said, “A rod.”
verses.explainer: The LORD asks: 'What is that in your hand?' Moses says: 'A staff.' God starts with what Moses already has.

verses.text:
He said, “Throw it on the ground.”
He threw it on the ground, and it became a snake; and Moses ran away from it.
verses.explainer: God says: 'Throw it on the ground.' Moses throws it, and it becomes a serpent. Moses runs from it. The staff of a shepherd becomes the rod of God.

verses.text:
Yahweh said to Moses, “Stretch out your hand, and take it by the tail.”
He stretched out his hand, and took hold of it, and it became a rod in his hand.
verses.explainer: God says: 'Reach out and take it by the tail.' Moses reaches out, and it becomes a staff again. Obedience in the face of fear.

verses.text: “That they may believe that Yahweh, the God of their fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has appeared to you.”
verses.explainer: This sign is so that they will believe the LORD, the God of their fathers, has appeared to Moses.

verses.text:
Yahweh said furthermore to him, “Now put your hand inside your cloak.”
He put his hand inside his cloak, and when he took it out, behold, his hand was leprous, as white as snow.
verses.explainer: God gives a second sign: 'Put your hand inside your cloak.' Moses does, and when he takes it out, it is leprous -- white as snow.

verses.text:
He said, “Put your hand inside your cloak again.”
He put his hand inside his cloak again, and when he took it out of his cloak, behold, it had turned again as his other flesh.
verses.explainer: God says: 'Put it back.' Moses does, and the hand is restored. Power over disease -- a sign of divine authority.

verses.text: “It will happen, if they will not believe you or listen to the voice of the first sign, that they will believe the voice of the latter sign.
verses.explainer: If they do not believe the first sign, they may believe the second.

verses.text: It will happen, if they will not believe even these two signs or listen to your voice, that you shall take of the water of the river, and pour it on the dry land. The water which you take out of the river will become blood on the dry land.”
verses.explainer: If they believe neither sign, Moses is to take Nile water and pour it on dry ground, where it will become blood. The first hint of what God will do to Egypt.

verses.text: Moses said to Yahweh, “O Lord, I am not eloquent, neither before now, nor since you have spoken to your servant; for I am slow of speech, and of a slow tongue.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'O Lord, I am not eloquent, neither before nor since you have spoken to your servant. I am slow of speech and slow of tongue.' Third objection: inability.

verses.text: Yahweh said to him, “Who made man’s mouth? Or who makes one mute, or deaf, or seeing, or blind? Isn’t it I, Yahweh?
verses.explainer: The LORD responds: 'Who has made man's mouth? Who makes him mute, deaf, seeing, or blind? Is it not I, the LORD?' The God who creates ability can certainly supply it.

verses.text: Now therefore go, and I will be with your mouth, and teach you what you shall speak.”
verses.explainer: God says: 'Now go, and I will be with your mouth and teach you what to speak.' Direct promise of divine assistance.

verses.text: He said, “Oh, Lord, please send someone else.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'Oh, my Lord, please send someone else.' The final objection strips away all pretense: Moses simply does not want to go.

verses.text: Yahweh’s anger burned against Moses, and he said, “What about Aaron, your brother, the Levite? I know that he can speak well. Also, behold, he comes out to meet you. When he sees you, he will be glad in his heart.
verses.explainer: The anger of the LORD burns against Moses. God has been patient through four objections; the fifth breaks through. But even in anger, God provides: 'Is there not Aaron your brother, the Levite? He can speak well. He is coming to meet you.'

verses.text: You shall speak to him, and put the words in his mouth. I will be with your mouth, and with his mouth, and will teach you what you shall do.
verses.explainer: Moses will speak to Aaron and put the words in his mouth. God will be with both of their mouths.

verses.text: He will be your spokesman to the people; and it will happen, that he will be to you a mouth, and you will be to him as God.
verses.explainer: Aaron will speak to the people for Moses. He will be Moses' mouth; Moses will be as God to him. The partnership is established.

verses.text: You shall take this rod in your hand, with which you shall do the signs.”
verses.explainer: Moses takes the staff -- 'with which you shall do the signs.' The shepherd's stick is now a divine instrument.

verses.text:
Moses went and returned to Jethro his father-in-law, and said to him, “Please let me go and return to my brothers who are in Egypt, and see whether they are still alive.”
Jethro said to Moses, “Go in peace.”
verses.explainer: Moses returns to Jethro and asks to go back to Egypt to see if his relatives are alive. Jethro says: 'Go in peace.'

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses in Midian, “Go, return into Egypt; for all the men who sought your life are dead.”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses in Midian: 'Go back to Egypt, for all the men who were seeking your life are dead.' The way is clear.

verses.text: Moses took his wife and his sons, and set them on a donkey, and he returned to the land of Egypt. Moses took God’s rod in his hand.
verses.explainer: Moses takes his wife and sons, sets them on a donkey, and returns to Egypt. He takes the staff of God in his hand.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “When you go back into Egypt, see that you do before Pharaoh all the wonders which I have put in your hand, but I will harden his heart and he will not let the people go.
verses.explainer: God tells Moses: 'When you go back, perform before Pharaoh all the wonders I have put in your power. But I will harden his heart, so that he will not let the people go.' The first mention of the hardening of Pharaoh's heart -- one of the most debated themes in Exodus.

thenVsNow.then: The text alternates between Pharaoh hardening his own heart and God hardening it. Ancient readers would not have seen the contradiction modern readers do -- divine sovereignty and human responsibility coexisted in Hebrew thought.
thenVsNow.now: The hardening of Pharaoh's heart has generated centuries of theological debate about free will, predestination, and divine justice. Paul uses it in Romans 9 to discuss God's sovereignty. The text seems to affirm both: Pharaoh chooses resistance, and God confirms that choice.

verses.text: You shall tell Pharaoh, ‘Yahweh says, Israel is my son, my firstborn,
verses.explainer: God tells Moses to say to Pharaoh: 'Israel is my firstborn son.' This is the first time God calls Israel his son. The language establishes a family relationship that will shape all subsequent theology.

verses.text: and I have said to you, “Let my son go, that he may serve me”; and you have refused to let him go. Behold, I will kill your son, your firstborn.’”
verses.explainer: God says: 'Let my son go that he may serve me. If you refuse, I will kill your firstborn son.' The stakes are established from the beginning. The tenth plague is foretold in advance.

verses.text: On the way at a lodging place, Yahweh met Moses and wanted to kill him.
verses.explainer: At a lodging place on the way, the LORD meets Moses and seeks to put him to death. This bizarre and terrifying incident is one of the most difficult passages in the Old Testament.

verses.text: Then Zipporah took a flint, and cut off the foreskin of her son, and cast it at his feet; and she said, “Surely you are a bridegroom of blood to me.”
verses.explainer: Zipporah takes a flint and circumcises her son. She touches Moses' feet with the foreskin and says: 'Surely you are a bridegroom of blood to me.' She performs the covenant sign that Moses has neglected.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Bridegroom of blood', 'explanation': "This enigmatic phrase may mean that the circumcision blood substitutes for Moses' blood -- the covenant sign saves his life. Moses, about to lead Israel out of Egypt by blood (the Passover lamb), must first be saved by blood himself. The deliverer must be delivered."}

verses.text: So he let him alone. Then she said, “You are a bridegroom of blood,” because of the circumcision.
verses.explainer: God lets Moses alone. Zipporah says: 'A bridegroom of blood, because of the circumcision.'

verses.text:
Yahweh said to Aaron, “Go into the wilderness to meet Moses.”
He went, and met him on God’s mountain, and kissed him.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Aaron: 'Go into the wilderness to meet Moses.' They meet at the mountain of God and kiss.

verses.text: Moses told Aaron all Yahweh’s words with which he had sent him, and all the signs with which he had instructed him.
verses.explainer: Moses tells Aaron everything the LORD has said and all the signs he has commanded.

verses.text: Moses and Aaron went and gathered together all the elders of the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: Moses and Aaron gather all the elders of Israel.

verses.text: Aaron spoke all the words which Yahweh had spoken to Moses, and did the signs in the sight of the people.
verses.explainer: Aaron speaks all the words the LORD has spoken to Moses and performs the signs before the people.

verses.text: The people believed, and when they heard that Yahweh had visited the children of Israel, and that he had seen their affliction, then they bowed their heads and worshiped.
verses.explainer: The people believe. They hear that the LORD has visited them and seen their affliction. They bow their heads and worship. The initial response is faith and gratitude.

explainer.summary: Moses and Aaron go to Pharaoh with God's message: 'Let my people go.' Pharaoh responds: 'Who is the LORD? I do not know the LORD, and I will not let Israel go.' He increases the Israelites' workload, requiring them to gather their own straw while maintaining the same brick quota. The Israelite foremen are beaten for failing to meet the quota. They blame Moses and Aaron. Moses cries out to God: 'Why have you done evil to this people?'
explainer.contextInBook: The initial optimism of chapter 4 crashes into Pharaoh's wall of resistance. The pattern is set: deliverance will come, but not quickly or easily. Things get worse before they get better.

verses.text: Afterward Moses and Aaron came, and said to Pharaoh, “This is what Yahweh, the God of Israel, says, ‘Let my people go, that they may hold a feast to me in the wilderness.’”
verses.explainer: Moses and Aaron go to Pharaoh: 'Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel: Let my people go, that they may hold a feast to me in the wilderness.' The demand is modest -- a religious festival, not permanent liberation.

verses.text: Pharaoh said, “Who is Yahweh, that I should listen to his voice to let Israel go? I don’t know Yahweh, and moreover I will not let Israel go.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice and let Israel go? I do not know the LORD, and moreover, I will not let Israel go.' The question is not ignorance but defiance. By the end of Exodus, Pharaoh will know exactly who the LORD is.

thenVsNow.then: Pharaoh, who considered himself a god, did not recognize the authority of a god worshipped by slaves. His refusal is both political (he needs the labor) and theological (he acknowledges no higher authority).
thenVsNow.now: Pharaoh's question 'Who is the LORD?' becomes the question the ten plagues systematically answer. Each plague reveals a different aspect of God's power and authority over the forces Pharaoh claims to control.

verses.text: They said, “The God of the Hebrews has met with us. Please let us go three days’ journey into the wilderness, and sacrifice to Yahweh, our God, lest he fall on us with pestilence, or with the sword.”
verses.explainer: Moses and Aaron persist: 'The God of the Hebrews has met with us. Please let us go a three days' journey to sacrifice, lest he fall upon us with pestilence or the sword.'

verses.text: The king of Egypt said to them, “Why do you, Moses and Aaron, take the people from their work? Get back to your burdens!”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'Moses and Aaron, why do you take the people from their work? Get back to your labors.'

verses.text: Pharaoh said, “Behold, the people of the land are now many, and you make them rest from their burdens.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'The people of the land are now many, and you make them rest from their burdens.' He sees the request as laziness.

verses.text: The same day Pharaoh commanded the taskmasters of the people, and their officers, saying,
verses.explainer: That same day, Pharaoh commands the taskmasters and foremen: no more straw for the Israelites.

verses.text: “You shall no longer give the people straw to make brick, as before. Let them go and gather straw for themselves.
verses.explainer: They must gather their own straw.

verses.text: The number of the bricks, which they made before, you require from them. You shall not diminish anything of it, for they are idle; therefore they cry, saying, ‘Let us go and sacrifice to our God.’
verses.explainer: But the brick quota stays the same. 'They are idle; that is why they cry, Let us go and sacrifice to our God.'

verses.text: Let heavier work be laid on the men, that they may labor in it; and don’t let them pay any attention to lying words.”
verses.explainer: Let heavier work be laid on them. Let them not pay attention to lying words.

verses.text: The taskmasters of the people went out, and their officers, and they spoke to the people, saying, “This is what Pharaoh says: ‘I will not give you straw.
verses.explainer: The taskmasters and foremen go out and tell the people: Pharaoh says no more straw.

verses.text: Go yourselves, get straw where you can find it, for nothing of your work shall be diminished.’”
verses.explainer: Go find your own straw wherever you can. But your work output will not decrease.

verses.text: So the people were scattered abroad throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble for straw.
verses.explainer: The people scatter throughout Egypt to gather stubble for straw.

verses.text: The taskmasters were urgent saying, “Fulfill your work quota daily, as when there was straw!”
verses.explainer: The taskmasters press them: 'Complete your work quota each day, just as when straw was provided.'

verses.text: The officers of the children of Israel, whom Pharaoh’s taskmasters had set over them, were beaten, and demanded, “Why haven’t you fulfilled your quota both yesterday and today, in making brick as before?”
verses.explainer: The Israelite foremen are beaten: 'Why have you not met your quota of bricks yesterday and today?'

verses.text: Then the officers of the children of Israel came and cried to Pharaoh, saying, “Why do you deal this way with your servants?
verses.explainer: The Israelite foremen appeal to Pharaoh: 'Why do you treat your servants this way?'

verses.text: No straw is given to your servants, and they tell us, ‘Make brick!’ and behold, your servants are beaten; but the fault is in your own people.”
verses.explainer: They say: 'No straw is given, yet they say make bricks! Your servants are beaten, but the fault is with your own people.'

verses.text: But he said, “You are idle! You are idle! Therefore you say, ‘Let us go and sacrifice to Yahweh.’
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'You are idle! That is why you say let us go sacrifice to the LORD.'

verses.text: Go therefore now, and work, for no straw shall be given to you, yet you shall deliver the same number of bricks!”
verses.explainer: Go work. No straw will be given, but you will deliver the same number of bricks.

verses.text: The officers of the children of Israel saw that they were in trouble, when it was said, “You shall not diminish anything from your daily quota of bricks!”
verses.explainer: The foremen see that they are in trouble when they are told: 'You shall not reduce your daily quota of bricks.'

verses.text: They met Moses and Aaron, who stood in the way, as they came out from Pharaoh:
verses.explainer: They meet Moses and Aaron, who are waiting for them after their audience with Pharaoh.

verses.text: and they said to them, “May Yahweh look at you, and judge, because you have made us a stench to be abhorred in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of his servants, to put a sword in their hand to kill us.”
verses.explainer: The foremen say: 'The LORD look on you and judge, because you have made us stink in the sight of Pharaoh and his servants. You have put a sword in their hand to kill us.' Moses' intervention has made everything worse.

verses.text: Moses returned to Yahweh, and said, “Lord, why have you brought trouble on this people? Why is it that you have sent me?
verses.explainer: Moses returns to the LORD: 'O Lord, why have you done evil to this people? Why did you ever send me?'

verses.text: For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has brought trouble on this people; and you have not rescued your people at all.”
verses.explainer: Since I came to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has done evil to this people, and you have not delivered your people at all. Moses' complaint is raw and honest. He has obeyed God, and the result is more suffering.

explainer.summary: God responds to Moses' complaint by reaffirming his identity and his covenant promises. He reveals that the patriarchs knew him as El Shaddai but not by the name YHWH -- the name that carries the full weight of his redemptive action. God promises to free Israel, adopt them as his people, and bring them to the Promised Land. Moses tells the Israelites, but they do not listen because of their crushed spirits. A genealogy places Moses and Aaron in their tribal context.
themes: The people too broken to hear
explainer.contextInBook: God meets Moses' despair with a deeper revelation of himself. The name YHWH is not just an identification but a commitment: I AM the one who will do what I have promised.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Now you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh, for by a strong hand he shall let them go, and by a strong hand he shall drive them out of his land.”
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: 'Now you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh. With a strong hand he will let them go; with a strong hand he will drive them out of his land.' God does not rebuke Moses' complaint; he answers it with a promise of action.

verses.text: God spoke to Moses, and said to him, “I am Yahweh;
verses.explainer: God speaks to Moses: 'I am the LORD (YHWH).'

verses.text: and I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, as God Almighty; but by my name Yahweh I was not known to them.
verses.explainer: God says: 'I appeared to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob as God Almighty (El Shaddai), but by my name the LORD (YHWH) I did not make myself known to them.' The patriarchs knew God by power; Moses will know God by covenant action.

verses.text: I have also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan, the land of their travels, in which they lived as aliens.
verses.explainer: God established his covenant with the patriarchs, promising them Canaan.

verses.text: Moreover I have heard the groaning of the children of Israel, whom the Egyptians keep in bondage, and I have remembered my covenant.
verses.explainer: God has heard the groaning of the Israelites, enslaved by the Egyptians, and he remembers his covenant.

verses.text: Therefore tell the children of Israel, ‘I am Yahweh, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will rid you out of their bondage, and I will redeem you with an outstretched arm, and with great judgments:
verses.explainer: God says to tell Israel: 'I am the LORD. I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians. I will deliver you from slavery. I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and great acts of judgment.' Three 'I will' statements of liberation.

verses.text: and I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God; and you shall know that I am Yahweh your God, who brings you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians.
verses.explainer: 'I will take you to be my people, and I will be your God.' The fourth and climactic 'I will.' This is the covenant formula: you will be my people, I will be your God. It defines the relationship established at Sinai.

verses.text: I will bring you into the land which I swore to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob; and I will give it to you for a heritage: I am Yahweh.’”
verses.explainer: 'I will bring you into the land that I swore to give to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. I will give it to you for a possession. I am the LORD.' The land promise is reaffirmed. The section opens and closes with 'I am the LORD' -- bookending the promises with God's identity.

verses.text: Moses spoke so to the children of Israel, but they didn’t listen to Moses for anguish of spirit, and for cruel bondage.
verses.explainer: Moses tells the people, but they do not listen because of their broken spirit and harsh slavery. Their suffering is so deep that they cannot receive good news. Oppression can crush the capacity for hope.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to Moses: Go tell Pharaoh to let the Israelites go.

verses.text: “Go in, speak to Pharaoh king of Egypt, that he let the children of Israel go out of his land.”
verses.explainer: Moses objects again: 'The Israelites have not listened to me. How will Pharaoh listen, especially since I have uncircumcised lips?'

verses.text: Moses spoke before Yahweh, saying, “Behold, the children of Israel haven’t listened to me. How then shall Pharaoh listen to me, who am of uncircumcised lips?”
verses.explainer: Moses calls his speech deficiency 'uncircumcised lips' -- using covenant language to describe a personal limitation.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses and to Aaron, and gave them a command to the children of Israel, and to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to both Moses and Aaron and gives them a charge concerning both Israel and Pharaoh.

verses.text: These are the heads of their fathers’ houses. The sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel: Hanoch, and Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi; these are the families of Reuben.
verses.explainer: Genealogy begins: the heads of their fathers' houses. Reuben's sons: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.

verses.text: The sons of Simeon: Jemuel, and Jamin, and Ohad, and Jachin, and Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman; these are the families of Simeon.
verses.explainer: Simeon's sons: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul.

verses.text: These are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations: Gershon, and Kohath, and Merari; and the years of the life of Levi were one hundred thirty-seven years.
verses.explainer: Levi's sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Levi lives 137 years. The genealogy focuses on Levi's line because Moses and Aaron are Levites.

verses.text: The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei, according to their families.
verses.explainer: Gershon's sons: Libni and Shimei.

verses.text: The sons of Kohath: Amram, and Izhar, and Hebron, and Uzziel; and the years of the life of Kohath were one hundred thirty-three years.
verses.explainer: Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Kohath lives 133 years.

verses.text: The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the families of the Levites according to their generations.
verses.explainer: Merari's sons: Mahli and Mushi. These are the Levite clans.

verses.text: Amram took Jochebed his father’s sister to himself as wife; and she bore him Aaron and Moses: and the years of the life of Amram were a hundred and thirty-seven years.
verses.explainer: Amram marries Jochebed his father's sister. She bears Aaron and Moses. Amram lives 137 years.

verses.text: The sons of Izhar: Korah, and Nepheg, and Zichri.
verses.explainer: Izhar's sons: Korah, Nepheg, and Zichri.

verses.text: The sons of Uzziel: Mishael, and Elzaphan, and Sithri.
verses.explainer: Uzziel's sons: Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri.

verses.text: Aaron took Elisheba, the daughter of Amminadab, the sister of Nahshon, as his wife; and she bore him Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar.
verses.explainer: Aaron marries Elisheba. She bears Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.

verses.text: The sons of Korah: Assir, and Elkanah, and Abiasaph; these are the families of the Korahites.
verses.explainer: Korah's sons: Assir, Elkanah, and Abiasaph.

verses.text: Eleazar Aaron’s son took one of the daughters of Putiel as his wife; and she bore him Phinehas. These are the heads of the fathers’ houses of the Levites according to their families.
verses.explainer: Eleazar (Aaron's son) marries a daughter of Putiel. She bears Phinehas. These are the heads of the Levitical fathers' houses.

verses.text: These are that Aaron and Moses, to whom Yahweh said, “Bring out the children of Israel from the land of Egypt according to their armies.”
verses.explainer: These are the Aaron and Moses to whom the LORD said: bring the Israelites out of Egypt by their hosts.

verses.text: These are those who spoke to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring out the children of Israel from Egypt. These are that Moses and Aaron.
verses.explainer: These are the ones who spoke to Pharaoh about bringing Israel out. These are Moses and Aaron.

verses.text: On the day when Yahweh spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt,
verses.explainer: On the day the LORD speaks to Moses in Egypt, he says:

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying, “I am Yahweh. Speak to Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I speak to you.”
verses.explainer: 'I am the LORD. Tell Pharaoh everything I say to you.'

verses.text: Moses said before Yahweh, “Behold, I am of uncircumcised lips, and how shall Pharaoh listen to me?”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'I have uncircumcised lips. How will Pharaoh listen to me?' The objection repeats, setting up the next phase.

explainer.summary: God tells Moses he has made him like God to Pharaoh, with Aaron as his prophet. God warns that he will harden Pharaoh's heart and multiply signs and wonders. When Moses and Aaron confront Pharaoh, Aaron's staff becomes a serpent. Pharaoh's magicians replicate the feat, but Aaron's staff swallows theirs. Pharaoh's heart is hardened. The first plague begins: Aaron strikes the Nile with his staff, and all the water in Egypt turns to blood. The fish die, the river stinks, and the Egyptians cannot drink.
themes: Signs and the hardening of Pharaoh
The first plague: the Nile turns to blood
explainer.contextInBook: The plague narrative begins. Each plague escalates in severity and targets different aspects of Egyptian life and religion. The Nile, worshipped as a god, becomes the first casualty.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Behold, I have made you as God to Pharaoh; and Aaron your brother shall be your prophet.
verses.explainer: The LORD says: 'I have made you like God to Pharaoh, and Aaron shall be your prophet.' The dynamic is clear: Moses speaks for God; Aaron speaks for Moses.

verses.text: You shall speak all that I command you; and Aaron your brother shall speak to Pharaoh, that he let the children of Israel go out of his land.
verses.explainer: Moses will speak everything God commands; Aaron will tell Pharaoh to let Israel go.

verses.text: I will harden Pharaoh’s heart, and multiply my signs and my wonders in the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: God says: 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart and multiply my signs and wonders in Egypt.' The hardening serves a purpose: more plagues mean a more complete demonstration of God's power.

verses.text: But Pharaoh will not listen to you, and I will lay my hand on Egypt, and bring out my armies, my people the children of Israel, out of the land of Egypt by great judgments.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh will not listen. God will lay his hand on Egypt and bring Israel out by great acts of judgment.

verses.text: The Egyptians shall know that I am Yahweh, when I stretch out my hand on Egypt, and bring out the children of Israel from among them.”
verses.explainer: 'The Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I stretch out my hand against Egypt.' The plagues are revelatory: they answer Pharaoh's question 'Who is the LORD?' from 5:2.

verses.text: Moses and Aaron did so. As Yahweh commanded them, so they did.
verses.explainer: Moses and Aaron do as the LORD commands them.

verses.text: Moses was eighty years old, and Aaron eighty-three years old, when they spoke to Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: Moses is eighty and Aaron eighty-three when they speak to Pharaoh. The deliverers are not young men.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses and to Aaron, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD instructs Moses and Aaron: when Pharaoh asks for a miracle, Aaron will throw his staff down, and it will become a serpent.

verses.text: “When Pharaoh speaks to you, saying, ‘Perform a miracle!’ then you shall tell Aaron, ‘Take your rod, and cast it down before Pharaoh, that it become a serpent.’”
verses.explainer: They go before Pharaoh. Aaron throws down his staff, and it becomes a serpent.

verses.text: Moses and Aaron went in to Pharaoh, and they did so, as Yahweh had commanded: and Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh and before his servants, and it became a serpent.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh summons his wise men and sorcerers. The magicians of Egypt do the same with their secret arts.

verses.text: Then Pharaoh also called for the wise men and the sorcerers. They also, the magicians of Egypt, did the same thing with their enchantments.
verses.explainer: Each man throws down his staff, and they become serpents. The implication is that Egyptian magic has real power -- but limited power.

verses.text: For they each cast down their rods, and they became serpents: but Aaron’s rod swallowed up their rods.
verses.explainer: But Aaron's staff swallows their staffs. God's power consumes the competition. Pharaoh's heart is hardened, and he does not listen, as the LORD predicted.

thenVsNow.then: Egyptian magicians were highly trained practitioners of ritual magic, believed to access the power of the gods. The contest between Aaron's staff and theirs is a contest between YHWH and the gods of Egypt.
thenVsNow.now: The swallowing of the magicians' serpents establishes the pattern: Egyptian power can imitate but not match God's power. What Egypt can do, God can undo and surpass.

verses.text: Pharaoh’s heart was hardened, and he didn’t listen to them; as Yahweh had spoken.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh's heart is hardened. He does not listen. Everything proceeds as God said.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Pharaoh’s heart is stubborn. He refuses to let the people go.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: 'Pharaoh's heart is hardened; he refuses to let the people go.'

verses.text: Go to Pharaoh in the morning. Behold, he goes out to the water; and you shall stand by the river’s bank to meet him; and the rod which was turned to a serpent you shall take in your hand.
verses.explainer: God instructs Moses to meet Pharaoh at the Nile in the morning with his staff.

verses.text: You shall tell him, ‘Yahweh, the God of the Hebrews, has sent me to you, saying, “Let my people go, that they may serve me in the wilderness:” and behold, until now you haven’t listened.
verses.explainer: Moses will say: 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, sent me to you: Let my people go to serve me in the wilderness. You have not listened.'

verses.text: Yahweh says, “In this you shall know that I am Yahweh. Behold, I will strike with the rod that is in my hand on the waters which are in the river, and they shall be turned to blood.
verses.explainer: 'Thus says the LORD: By this you shall know that I am the LORD. I will strike the water of the Nile with this staff, and it shall turn into blood.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The Nile as a god', 'explanation': "The Nile was the lifeblood of Egypt -- without it, civilization was impossible. The Egyptians worshipped the Nile inundation as the god Hapi. Turning the Nile to blood was a direct assault on Egypt's most essential deity and resource."}

verses.text: The fish that are in the river shall die, and the river shall become foul; and the Egyptians shall loathe to drink water from the river.”’”
verses.explainer: The fish will die, the river will stink, and the Egyptians will loathe drinking from the Nile.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Tell Aaron, ‘Take your rod, and stretch out your hand over the waters of Egypt, over their rivers, over their streams, and over their pools, and over all their ponds of water, that they may become blood; and there shall be blood throughout all the land of Egypt, both in vessels of wood and in vessels of stone.’”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: tell Aaron to stretch out his staff over all the waters of Egypt -- rivers, canals, ponds, and pools. They will become blood. There will be blood throughout all Egypt, even in wood and stone vessels.

verses.text: Moses and Aaron did so, as Yahweh commanded; and he lifted up the rod, and struck the waters that were in the river, in the sight of Pharaoh, and in the sight of his servants; and all the waters that were in the river were turned to blood.
verses.explainer: Moses and Aaron do as the LORD commands. Aaron lifts his staff and strikes the Nile in the sight of Pharaoh and his servants. All the water turns to blood.

verses.text: The fish that were in the river died; and the river became foul, and the Egyptians couldn’t drink water from the river; and the blood was throughout all the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: The fish die, the river stinks, and the Egyptians cannot drink. Blood is throughout all Egypt.

verses.text: The magicians of Egypt did the same thing with their enchantments; and Pharaoh’s heart was hardened, and he didn’t listen to them; as Yahweh had spoken.
verses.explainer: But the magicians of Egypt do the same with their secret arts. Pharaoh's heart is hardened. The magicians can replicate the plague but cannot reverse it -- making the problem worse, not better.

verses.text: Pharaoh turned and went into his house, and he didn’t even take this to heart.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh turns and goes into his house. He does not even take this to heart.

verses.text: All the Egyptians dug around the river for water to drink; for they couldn’t drink the river water.
verses.explainer: All the Egyptians dig along the Nile for water to drink. They cannot drink the river water.

verses.text: Seven days were fulfilled, after Yahweh had struck the river.
verses.explainer: Seven full days pass after the LORD strikes the Nile. The first plague has run its course.

explainer.summary: Three more plagues strike Egypt in rapid succession. Frogs swarm the land -- in houses, beds, and ovens. Pharaoh begs Moses to pray, and Moses asks when he wants the frogs removed. Pharaoh says 'tomorrow,' and the frogs die, leaving massive piles of rotting frogs. Then gnats (or lice) come, and even the magicians admit: 'This is the finger of God.' Next, swarms of flies overwhelm Egypt, but Goshen is protected. Pharaoh begins to negotiate but will not fully comply.
themes: The distinction between Egypt and Goshen
explainer.contextInBook: The plagues intensify. The magicians who replicated the first two plagues now fail at the third and confess divine power. Pharaoh begins to negotiate but always reneges once the pressure is removed.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, Go in to Pharaoh, and tell him, “This is what Yahweh says, ‘Let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses to go to Pharaoh: 'Let my people go to serve me.'

verses.text: If you refuse to let them go, behold, I will plague all your borders with frogs:
verses.explainer: If Pharaoh refuses, God will plague Egypt with frogs.

verses.text: and the river shall swarm with frogs, which shall go up and come into your house, and into your bedroom, and on your bed, and into the house of your servants, and on your people, and into your ovens, and into your kneading troughs:
verses.explainer: The Nile will teem with frogs. They will come into Pharaoh's house, bedroom, bed, servants' houses, ovens, and kneading bowls. Frogs everywhere -- no space is private or sacred.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Frogs and Heqet', 'explanation': "The Egyptian goddess Heqet was depicted with a frog's head and was associated with fertility and childbirth. A plague of frogs turned an object of worship into an instrument of torment. Killing a frog sacred to Heqet would have been taboo, yet the frogs were making life unbearable."}

verses.text: and the frogs shall come up both on you, and on your people, and on all your servants.’”
verses.explainer: Frogs will come on Pharaoh himself, on his people, and on all his servants.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Tell Aaron, ‘Stretch out your hand with your rod over the rivers, over the streams, and over the pools, and cause frogs to come up on the land of Egypt.’”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses to tell Aaron to stretch out his hand with the staff over the rivers, canals, and ponds, and bring up frogs.

verses.text: Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt; and the frogs came up, and covered the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Aaron stretches out his hand, and frogs come up and cover the land of Egypt.

verses.text: The magicians did the same thing with their enchantments, and brought up frogs on the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: The magicians do the same -- they produce more frogs. They can make the problem worse but cannot solve it.

verses.text: Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron, and said, “Entreat Yahweh, that he take away the frogs from me, and from my people; and I will let the people go, that they may sacrifice to Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh calls Moses and Aaron: 'Plead with the LORD to take away the frogs, and I will let the people go to sacrifice.' First concession.

verses.text: Moses said to Pharaoh, “I give you the honor of setting the time that I should pray for you, and for your servants, and for your people, that the frogs be destroyed from you and your houses, and remain in the river only.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'You set the time. When shall I plead for you, your servants, and your people, that the frogs be destroyed?'

verses.text:
He said, “Tomorrow.”
He said, “Be it according to your word, that you may know that there is no one like Yahweh our God.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'Tomorrow.' He could say 'now' but chooses to spend one more night with the frogs. The irrationality of resistance.

verses.text: The frogs shall depart from you, and from your houses, and from your servants, and from your people. They shall remain in the river only.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'Be it as you say, so that you may know that there is no one like the LORD our God.'

verses.text: Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh, and Moses cried to Yahweh concerning the frogs which he had brought on Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: The frogs will depart from everywhere except the Nile.

verses.text: Yahweh did according to the word of Moses, and the frogs died out of the houses, out of the courts, and out of the fields.
verses.explainer: Moses and Aaron leave Pharaoh. Moses cries to the LORD about the frogs.

verses.text: They gathered them together in heaps, and the land stank.
verses.explainer: The LORD does as Moses asks. The frogs die in the houses, courtyards, and fields.

verses.text: But when Pharaoh saw that there was a respite, he hardened his heart, and didn’t listen to them, as Yahweh had spoken.
verses.explainer: They gather the frogs into heaps, and the land stinks. But when Pharaoh sees there is relief, he hardens his heart and does not listen. The pattern: suffering produces promises; relief produces broken promises.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Tell Aaron, ‘Stretch out your rod, and strike the dust of the earth, that it may become lice throughout all the land of Egypt.’”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: tell Aaron to stretch out his staff and strike the dust of the earth, and it will become gnats (or lice) throughout Egypt.

verses.text: They did so; and Aaron stretched out his hand with his rod, and struck the dust of the earth, and there were lice on man, and on animal; all the dust of the earth became lice throughout all the land of Egypt.
