verses.explainer: Aaron strikes the dust, and gnats come on man and beast. All the dust of the earth becomes gnats throughout Egypt.

verses.text: The magicians tried with their enchantments to produce lice, but they couldn’t. There were lice on man, and on animal.
verses.explainer: The magicians try to produce gnats by their secret arts but cannot. The gnats remain on man and beast. The magicians have reached their limit.

verses.text: Then the magicians said to Pharaoh, “This is God’s finger:” and Pharaoh’s heart was hardened, and he didn’t listen to them; as Yahweh had spoken.
verses.explainer: The magicians tell Pharaoh: 'This is the finger of God.' Pharaoh's heart is hardened, and he does not listen. Even his own experts recognize divine power, but Pharaoh is unmoved.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh; behold, he comes out to the water; and tell him, ‘This is what Yahweh says, “Let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses to rise early and confront Pharaoh at the water: 'Let my people go to serve me.'

verses.text: Else, if you will not let my people go, behold, I will send swarms of flies on you, and on your servants, and on your people, and into your houses: and the houses of the Egyptians shall be full of swarms of flies, and also the ground whereon they are.
verses.explainer: If Pharaoh refuses, God will send swarms of flies on Pharaoh, his servants, his people, and their houses. The land will be full of flies.

verses.text: I will set apart in that day the land of Goshen, in which my people dwell, that no swarms of flies shall be there; to the end you may know that I am Yahweh on the earth.
verses.explainer: But on that day, God will set apart the land of Goshen where his people dwell. No swarms of flies will be there, 'so that you may know that I am the LORD in the midst of the earth.' The distinction begins -- God protects his own.

thenVsNow.then: The separation of Goshen from the rest of Egypt during the plagues was a dramatic demonstration of God's power to discriminate -- to target oppressors while protecting the oppressed. It proved the plagues were not random natural phenomena.
thenVsNow.now: The Goshen distinction has become a theological principle: God knows and protects his people even in the midst of general catastrophe. It does not guarantee comfort but it demonstrates care.

verses.text: I will put a division between my people and your people: by tomorrow shall this sign be.”’”
verses.explainer: God will put a distinction between his people and Pharaoh's people. This sign will appear tomorrow.

verses.text: Yahweh did so; and there came grievous swarms of flies into the house of Pharaoh, and into his servants’ houses: and in all the land of Egypt the land was corrupted by reason of the swarms of flies.
verses.explainer: The LORD does this. Dense swarms of flies come into Pharaoh's house and his servants' houses. All of Egypt is ruined by the flies.

verses.text: Pharaoh called for Moses and for Aaron, and said, “Go, sacrifice to your God in the land!”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh calls Moses and Aaron: 'Go, sacrifice to your God within the land.' A compromise: worship, but stay in Egypt.

verses.text: Moses said, “It isn’t appropriate to do so; for we shall sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians to Yahweh our God. Behold, shall we sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians before their eyes, and won’t they stone us?
verses.explainer: Moses refuses: 'It would not be right. Our sacrifices would be an abomination to the Egyptians. Would they not stone us?'

verses.text: We will go three days’ journey into the wilderness, and sacrifice to Yahweh our God, as he shall command us.”
verses.explainer: Moses insists: 'We must go three days into the wilderness and sacrifice as the LORD tells us.'

verses.text: Pharaoh said, “I will let you go, that you may sacrifice to Yahweh your God in the wilderness, only you shall not go very far away. Pray for me.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'I will let you go to sacrifice, but do not go very far. Plead for me.' Each concession comes with conditions.

verses.text: Moses said, “Behold, I go out from you, and I will pray to Yahweh that the swarms of flies may depart from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people, tomorrow; only don’t let Pharaoh deal deceitfully any more in not letting the people go to sacrifice to Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'I will plead with the LORD. The swarms will depart tomorrow. But let not Pharaoh cheat us again.'

verses.text: Moses went out from Pharaoh, and prayed to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Moses leaves and pleads with the LORD.

verses.text: Yahweh did according to the word of Moses, and he removed the swarms of flies from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people. There remained not one.
verses.explainer: The LORD removes the flies. Not one remains.

verses.text: Pharaoh hardened his heart this time also, and he didn’t let the people go.
verses.explainer: But Pharaoh hardens his heart again and does not let the people go. Relief always leads to regression.

explainer.summary: Three more plagues devastate Egypt. A deadly plague kills all Egyptian livestock, but not a single Israelite animal dies. Then boils break out on all Egyptians and their animals -- even the magicians cannot stand before Moses. Finally, the worst hailstorm in Egyptian history strikes everything in the open: people, animals, and plants. Only Goshen is spared. Pharaoh confesses sin for the first time but hardens his heart again when the hail stops.
themes: Plagues targeting Egyptian economy and health
The complete distinction between Egypt and Israel
explainer.contextInBook: The plagues are now causing severe economic damage and physical suffering. The magicians are personally afflicted. Pharaoh confesses but does not change. The final three plagues will be even worse.

verses.text: Then Yahweh said to Moses, “Go in to Pharaoh, and tell him, ‘This is what Yahweh, the God of the Hebrews, says: “Let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses to go to Pharaoh: 'Let my people go to serve me.'

verses.text: For if you refuse to let them go, and hold them still,
verses.explainer: If Pharaoh refuses and continues to hold them...

verses.text: behold, Yahweh’s hand is on your livestock which are in the field, on the horses, on the donkeys, on the camels, on the herds, and on the flocks with a very grievous pestilence.
verses.explainer: The hand of the LORD will bring a severe plague on Egyptian livestock -- horses, donkeys, camels, herds, and flocks. Cattle were sacred in Egypt; Hathor was a cow goddess, and the Apis bull was worshipped at Memphis.

verses.text: Yahweh will make a distinction between the livestock of Israel and the livestock of Egypt; and nothing shall die of all that belongs to the children of Israel.”’”
verses.explainer: The LORD will make a distinction between Israelite livestock and Egyptian livestock. Nothing belonging to Israel will die.

verses.text: Yahweh appointed a set time, saying, “Tomorrow Yahweh shall do this thing in the land.”
verses.explainer: The LORD sets a time: tomorrow.

verses.text: Yahweh did that thing on the next day; and all the livestock of Egypt died, but of the livestock of the children of Israel, not one died.
verses.explainer: The next day, the LORD does this. All Egyptian livestock die. Not one Israelite animal dies. Total distinction.

verses.text: Pharaoh sent, and, behold, there was not so much as one of the livestock of the Israelites dead. But the heart of Pharaoh was stubborn, and he didn’t let the people go.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh investigates and confirms that not a single Israelite animal has died. His heart is still hardened.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses and to Aaron, “Take to you handfuls of ashes of the furnace, and let Moses sprinkle it toward the sky in the sight of Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses and Aaron to take handfuls of furnace soot and throw it in the air before Pharaoh.

verses.text: It shall become small dust over all the land of Egypt, and shall be a boil breaking out with boils on man and on animal, throughout all the land of Egypt.”
verses.explainer: It will become fine dust over all Egypt, producing boils that break out in sores on people and animals.

verses.text: They took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh; and Moses sprinkled it up toward the sky; and it became a boil breaking out with boils on man and on animal.
verses.explainer: They take soot and stand before Pharaoh. Moses throws it in the air, and boils break out on all Egyptians and their animals.

verses.text: The magicians couldn’t stand before Moses because of the boils; for the boils were on the magicians, and on all the Egyptians.
verses.explainer: The magicians cannot stand before Moses because of the boils. The boils are on the magicians themselves. They are not just defeated; they are afflicted.

verses.text: Yahweh hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he didn’t listen to them, as Yahweh had spoken to Moses.
verses.explainer: The LORD hardens Pharaoh's heart. He does not listen to Moses and Aaron, just as the LORD predicted.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh, and tell him, ‘This is what Yahweh, the God of the Hebrews, says: “Let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses to rise early and confront Pharaoh: 'Let my people go to serve me.'

verses.text: For this time I will send all my plagues against your heart, against your officials, and against your people; that you may know that there is no one like me in all the earth.
verses.explainer: God says: 'This time I will send all my plagues on you personally, on your servants, and on your people, so that you may know there is none like me in all the earth.'

verses.text: For now I would have stretched out my hand, and struck you and your people with pestilence, and you would have been cut off from the earth;
verses.explainer: God says he could have stretched out his hand and struck Pharaoh with pestilence, wiping him off the earth.

verses.text: but indeed for this cause I have made you stand: to show you my power, and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth;
verses.explainer: 'But for this purpose I have raised you up, to show you my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth.' Pharaoh's resistance serves God's global purpose -- the Exodus will be told and retold across all nations.

verses.text: as you still exalt yourself against my people, that you won’t let them go.
verses.explainer: God tells Pharaoh: 'You are still exalting yourself against my people and will not let them go.'

verses.text: Behold, tomorrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail, such as has not been in Egypt since the day it was founded even until now.
verses.explainer: Tomorrow God will send a hailstorm worse than any since Egypt became a nation.

verses.text: Now therefore command that all of your livestock and all that you have in the field be brought into shelter. Every man and animal that is found in the field, and isn’t brought home, the hail shall come down on them, and they shall die.”’”
verses.explainer: God warns: bring your livestock and everything in the field into shelter. Anything left outside will die. God gives a warning -- even in judgment, there is mercy.

verses.text: Those who feared Yahweh’s word among the servants of Pharaoh made their servants and their livestock flee into the houses.
verses.explainer: Some of Pharaoh's servants fear the word of the LORD and bring their servants and livestock inside.

verses.text: Whoever didn’t respect Yahweh’s word left his servants and his livestock in the field.
verses.explainer: Those who do not regard the LORD's word leave their servants and livestock in the field. Even among Egyptians, some believe and some do not.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Stretch out your hand toward the sky, that there may be hail in all the land of Egypt, on man, and on animal, and on every herb of the field, throughout the land of Egypt.”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses to stretch out his hand toward heaven, and hail will fall.

verses.text: Moses stretched out his rod toward the heavens, and Yahweh sent thunder, hail, and lightning flashed down to the earth. Yahweh rained hail on the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Moses stretches out his staff. The LORD sends thunder, hail, and lightning running along the ground. The worst storm in Egyptian history.

verses.text: So there was very severe hail, and lightning mixed with the hail, such as had not been in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation.
verses.explainer: Hail mixed with fire -- very heavy. Nothing like it has been in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation.

verses.text: The hail struck throughout all the land of Egypt all that was in the field, both man and animal; and the hail struck every herb of the field, and broke every tree of the field.
verses.explainer: The hail strikes everything in the field -- people, animals, plants. It shatters every tree.

verses.text: Only in the land of Goshen, where the children of Israel were, there was no hail.
verses.explainer: Only in Goshen, where the Israelites are, there is no hail.

verses.text: Pharaoh sent, and called for Moses and Aaron, and said to them, “I have sinned this time. Yahweh is righteous, and I and my people are wicked.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh sends for Moses and Aaron: 'This time I have sinned. The LORD is in the right, and I and my people are in the wrong.' The first confession.

verses.text: Pray to Yahweh; for there has been enough of mighty thunderings and hail. I will let you go, and you shall stay no longer.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'Plead with the LORD. Enough of this thunder and hail! I will let you go.'

verses.text: Moses said to him, “As soon as I have gone out of the city, I will spread abroad my hands to Yahweh. The thunders shall cease, and there will not be any more hail; that you may know that the earth is Yahweh’s.
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'When I have gone out of the city, I will stretch out my hands to the LORD. The thunder will cease and the hail will stop, so that you may know the earth is the LORD's.'

verses.text: But as for you and your servants, I know that you don’t yet fear Yahweh God.”
verses.explainer: Moses adds: 'But as for you and your servants, I know you do not yet fear the LORD God.' He sees through the confession.

verses.text: The flax and the barley were struck, for the barley was in the ear, and the flax was in bloom.
verses.explainer: The flax and barley are destroyed because they were in bloom. These are early crops.

verses.text: But the wheat and the spelt were not struck, for they had not grown up.
verses.explainer: The wheat and emmer are not destroyed because they ripen later. This detail explains why there is still food to be destroyed by the locusts in chapter 10.

verses.text: Moses went out of the city from Pharaoh, and spread abroad his hands to Yahweh; and the thunders and hail ceased, and the rain was not poured on the earth.
verses.explainer: Moses leaves the city, stretches his hands to the LORD. The thunder, hail, and rain stop.

verses.text: When Pharaoh saw that the rain and the hail and the thunders had ceased, he sinned yet more, and hardened his heart, he and his servants.
verses.explainer: When Pharaoh sees the rain, hail, and thunder have ceased, he sins again and hardens his heart.

verses.text: The heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he didn’t let the children of Israel go, just as Yahweh had spoken through Moses.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh's heart is hardened, and he does not let the Israelites go, just as the LORD said through Moses.

explainer.summary: The eighth plague brings locusts that devour everything the hail left standing. Pharaoh's own servants beg him to let Israel go before Egypt is completely destroyed. Pharaoh attempts to negotiate -- only the men can go -- but Moses refuses partial obedience. The ninth plague brings three days of total darkness over Egypt, so thick it can be felt. Only the Israelites have light. Pharaoh offers to let them go but without their livestock. Moses refuses. Pharaoh threatens to kill Moses if he returns.
explainer.contextInBook: The ninth plague -- darkness -- targets Ra, Egypt's supreme deity, the sun god. When the sun goes dark, Pharaoh's own divinity is under assault. After nine plagues, Pharaoh is left with nothing except his stubbornness.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Go in to Pharaoh, for I have hardened his heart, and the heart of his servants, that I may show these my signs among them,
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses to go to Pharaoh: 'I have hardened his heart and his servants' hearts, that I may show these signs among them.'

verses.text: and that you may tell in the hearing of your son, and of your son’s son, what things I have done to Egypt, and my signs which I have done among them; that you may know that I am Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: 'And that you may tell your children and grandchildren how I dealt with the Egyptians and what signs I did among them, that you may know that I am the LORD.' The plagues are designed to be remembered and retold across generations.

thenVsNow.then: The Exodus was to become Israel's foundational story -- told at every Passover, referenced by every prophet, sung in every psalm of praise. The command to tell the story is embedded in the event itself.
thenVsNow.now: The Passover Seder remains the oldest continuously observed religious ceremony in the world. Jewish families have told this story at their tables for over three thousand years.

verses.text: Moses and Aaron went in to Pharaoh, and said to him, “This is what Yahweh, the God of the Hebrews, says: ‘How long will you refuse to humble yourself before me? Let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.explainer: Moses and Aaron go to Pharaoh: 'How long will you refuse to humble yourself before me? Let my people go.'

verses.text: Or else, if you refuse to let my people go, behold, tomorrow I will bring locusts into your country,
verses.explainer: If Pharaoh refuses, tomorrow God will bring locusts into Egypt.

verses.text: and they shall cover the surface of the earth, so that one won’t be able to see the earth. They shall eat the residue of that which has escaped, which remains to you from the hail, and shall eat every tree which grows for you out of the field.
verses.explainer: They will cover the face of the land. They will eat what is left after the hail and strip every tree.

verses.text: Your houses shall be filled, and the houses of all your servants, and the houses of all the Egyptians; as neither your fathers nor your fathers’ fathers have seen, since the day that they were on the earth to this day.’” He turned, and went out from Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: They will fill houses -- something neither your fathers nor grandfathers have ever seen. Moses turns and leaves.

verses.text: Pharaoh’s servants said to him, “How long will this man be a snare to us? Let the men go, that they may serve Yahweh, their God. Don’t you yet know that Egypt is destroyed?”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh's servants say to him: 'How long shall this man be a snare to us? Let the men go to serve the LORD their God. Do you not yet understand that Egypt is destroyed?' Pharaoh's own court rebels against his stubbornness.

verses.text: Moses and Aaron were brought again to Pharaoh, and he said to them, “Go, serve Yahweh your God; but who are those who will go?”
verses.explainer: Moses and Aaron are brought back to Pharaoh. He says: 'Go, serve the LORD your God. But who exactly is going?'

verses.text: Moses said, “We will go with our young and with our old; with our sons and with our daughters, with our flocks and with our herds will we go; for we must hold a feast to Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'We will go with our young and our old, our sons and daughters, our flocks and herds. We must celebrate a feast to the LORD.'

verses.text: He said to them, “Yahweh be with you if I will let you go with your little ones! See, evil is clearly before your faces.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says sarcastically: 'The LORD be with you if I ever let you and your little ones go! Look, you have evil intent.' He accuses them of using religion as cover for escape.

verses.text: Not so! Go now you who are men, and serve Yahweh; for that is what you desire!” They were driven out from Pharaoh’s presence.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'No! Only the men may go and serve the LORD, since that is what you desire.' They are driven out from Pharaoh's presence. He will allow worship but holds families hostage.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts, that they may come up on the land of Egypt, and eat every herb of the land, even all that the hail has left.”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses to stretch out his hand over Egypt for the locusts.

verses.text: Moses stretched out his rod over the land of Egypt, and Yahweh brought an east wind on the land all that day, and all night; and when it was morning, the east wind brought the locusts.
verses.explainer: Moses stretches out his staff. The LORD brings an east wind all day and night. By morning the wind has brought the locusts.

verses.text: The locusts went up over all the land of Egypt, and rested in all the borders of Egypt. They were very grievous. Before them there were no such locusts as they, nor will there ever be again.
verses.explainer: The locusts come over all Egypt and settle on the whole country in a devastating swarm. Never before has there been such a plague of locusts, and never will there be again.

verses.text: For they covered the surface of the whole earth, so that the land was darkened, and they ate every herb of the land, and all the fruit of the trees which the hail had left. There remained nothing green, either tree or herb of the field, through all the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: They cover the surface of the whole land until the ground is black. They eat all the plants and fruit that the hail left. Not a green thing remains in all Egypt.

verses.text: Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron in haste, and he said, “I have sinned against Yahweh your God, and against you.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh hastily summons Moses and Aaron: 'I have sinned against the LORD your God and against you.' Second confession.

verses.text: Now therefore please forgive my sin again, and pray to Yahweh your God, that he may also take away from me this death.”
verses.explainer: He says: 'Please forgive my sin just this once. Plead with the LORD to remove this deadly plague from me.'

verses.text: He went out from Pharaoh, and prayed to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Moses goes out and pleads with the LORD.

verses.text: Yahweh turned an exceeding strong west wind, which took up the locusts, and drove them into the Red Sea. There remained not one locust in all the borders of Egypt.
verses.explainer: The LORD turns a very strong west wind that lifts the locusts and drives them into the Red Sea. Not a single locust remains in Egypt.

verses.text: But Yahweh hardened Pharaoh’s heart, and he didn’t let the children of Israel go.
verses.explainer: But the LORD hardens Pharaoh's heart, and he does not let Israel go.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Stretch out your hand toward the sky, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, even darkness which may be felt.”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: 'Stretch out your hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over Egypt, a darkness to be felt.'

verses.text: Moses stretched out his hand toward the sky, and there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt for three days.
verses.explainer: Moses stretches out his hand, and thick darkness covers all Egypt for three days. No one can see anyone else or move from their place for three days.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Darkness and Ra', 'explanation': "Ra was the supreme deity of Egypt -- the sun god whose daily journey across the sky sustained all life. Three days of impenetrable darkness was a direct assault on Ra's power. If Ra cannot shine, Pharaoh's claim to be the son of Ra is worthless. The darkness declares that YHWH controls even the sun."}

verses.text: They didn’t see one another, and nobody rose from his place for three days; but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings.
verses.explainer: But all the Israelites have light in their dwellings. God makes a distinction even with light and darkness -- echoing creation itself, when God separated light from darkness.

verses.text: Pharaoh called to Moses, and said, “Go, serve Yahweh. Only let your flocks and your herds stay behind. Let your little ones also go with you.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh calls Moses: 'Go, serve the LORD. Even your little ones may go with you. Only your flocks and herds must remain.' He tries one more compromise.

verses.text: Moses said, “You must also give into our hand sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may sacrifice to Yahweh our God.
verses.explainer: Moses refuses: 'You must also let us have sacrifices and burnt offerings. Our livestock also must go with us. Not a hoof shall be left behind.'

verses.text: Our livestock also shall go with us. Not a hoof shall be left behind, for of it we must take to serve Yahweh our God; and we don’t know with what we must serve Yahweh, until we come there.”
verses.explainer: Moses says they will not know what they need to sacrifice until they arrive. The demand is total: all the people, all the animals.

verses.text: But Yahweh hardened Pharaoh’s heart, and he wouldn’t let them go.
verses.explainer: The LORD hardens Pharaoh's heart, and he will not let them go.

verses.text: Pharaoh said to him, “Get away from me! Be careful to see my face no more; for in the day you see my face you shall die!”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'Get away from me! Take care never to see my face again, for on the day you see my face you shall die.'

verses.text: Moses said, “You have spoken well. I will see your face again no more.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: 'As you say. I will not see your face again.' The negotiations are over. The tenth and final plague is about to fall.

explainer.summary: God tells Moses that one final plague remains. At midnight, every firstborn in Egypt will die -- from Pharaoh's son to the firstborn of the slave girl behind the mill. A great cry will rise throughout Egypt like nothing before and nothing since. But not a dog will bark against the Israelites. God will make a clear distinction between Egypt and Israel. The Egyptians will beg the Israelites to leave.
themes: The ultimate plague targeting Pharaoh's divine claims
The distinction between Egypt and Israel
God's judgment on the gods of Egypt
explainer.contextInBook: Nine plagues have failed to break Pharaoh. The tenth strikes at the heart of Egyptian religion: Pharaoh is considered a god, and his firstborn son is the continuation of divine rule. God strikes the thing Pharaoh values most.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The firstborn son held the primary inheritance rig
wordStudies.meaning: The firstborn son held the primary inheritance rights, the leadership role, and in Egypt, represented the continuity of the dynasty. The death of the firstborn was the death of the future itself.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Yet one plague more will I bring on Pharaoh, and on Egypt; afterwards he will let you go. When he lets you go, he will surely thrust you out altogether.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: one more plague remains. After it, Pharaoh will not merely let them go -- he will drive them out completely.

verses.text: Speak now in the ears of the people, and let every man ask of his neighbor, and every woman of her neighbor, jewels of silver, and jewels of gold.”
verses.explainer: God instructs the people to ask their Egyptian neighbors for silver and gold articles. The slaves will not leave empty-handed.

verses.text: Yahweh gave the people favor in the sight of the Egyptians. Moreover the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt, in the sight of Pharaoh’s servants, and in the sight of the people.
verses.explainer: The LORD gives the people favor in the sight of the Egyptians. Moses himself is highly regarded by Pharaoh's servants and the people. Even Egypt recognizes Moses' greatness.

thenVsNow.then: The plagues have demonstrated that the God of the slaves is more powerful than all the gods of Egypt. Even the Egyptians now respect Moses and the Israelites, recognizing divine power at work.
thenVsNow.now: The transfer of wealth from oppressor to oppressed is a theme that resonates in every liberation movement. Reparations for generations of unpaid labor are not charity but justice.

verses.text: Moses said, “This is what Yahweh says: ‘About midnight I will go out into the middle of Egypt,
verses.explainer: Moses announces: 'About midnight the LORD will go out through the midst of Egypt.'

verses.text: and all the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sits on his throne, even to the firstborn of the female servant who is behind the mill; and all the firstborn of livestock.
verses.explainer: Every firstborn in Egypt will die -- from Pharaoh's firstborn who sits on his throne to the firstborn of the slave girl behind the millstones, and all the firstborn of the livestock.

verses.text: There shall be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there has not been, nor shall be any more.
verses.explainer: There will be a great cry throughout all Egypt, such as has never been and never will be again.

verses.text: But against any of the children of Israel a dog won’t even bark or move its tongue, against man or animal; that you may know that Yahweh makes a distinction between the Egyptians and Israel.
verses.explainer: But against the Israelites, not a dog will growl. The LORD makes a distinction between Egypt and Israel. This separation is the essence of the Exodus: God knows who belongs to him.

verses.text: All these servants of yours will come down to me, and bow down themselves to me, saying, “Get out, with all the people who follow you”; and after that I will go out.’” He went out from Pharaoh in hot anger.
verses.explainer: Moses tells Pharaoh: all your servants will come bowing to me, saying 'Get out!' Then I will leave. Moses goes out from Pharaoh in hot anger.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Pharaoh won’t listen to you, that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt.”
verses.explainer: The LORD had told Moses: Pharaoh will not listen, so that my wonders may be multiplied. The plagues serve a revelatory purpose -- they show who God is.

verses.text: Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh, and Yahweh hardened Pharaoh’s heart, and he didn’t let the children of Israel go out of his land.
verses.explainer: Moses and Aaron performed all these wonders before Pharaoh, but the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart and he did not let the Israelites go. The narrative summary before the final act.

explainer.summary: The pivot of the entire Old Testament. God institutes the Passover: each household must take a lamb, kill it at twilight, and put its blood on the doorposts. When the destroyer passes through Egypt at midnight, he will 'pass over' the houses marked with blood. That night, every firstborn in Egypt dies. Pharaoh finally relents and drives Israel out. The Israelites leave after 430 years, carrying Egypt's wealth. God commands that Passover be observed forever as a memorial.
themes: Substitutionary sacrifice -- the lamb dies instead of the firstborn
Blood as the sign of deliverance
The institution of Israel's most sacred ritual
explainer.contextInBook: This is the central chapter of Exodus and arguably of the entire Old Testament. Everything before it builds to this moment; everything after it refers back to it. The Passover becomes the defining event of Israelite identity.

wordStudies.translatedAs: From the verb pasach, meaning 'to pass over' or 't
wordStudies.meaning: From the verb pasach, meaning 'to pass over' or 'to spare.' God passes over the houses marked with lamb's blood, sparing the firstborn inside. The concept of substitution -- innocent life given in place of guilty life -- is embedded in the word itself.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Bread made without yeast
wordStudies.meaning: Bread made without yeast. The Israelites left Egypt so quickly they could not wait for their dough to rise. Leaven later becomes a symbol of corruption and sin. Eating unleavened bread remembers the urgency of liberation.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses and Aaron in the land of Egypt, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to Moses and Aaron in the land of Egypt.

verses.text: “This month shall be to you the beginning of months. It shall be the first month of the year to you.
verses.explainer: This month shall be the beginning of months for you -- the first month of the year. Liberation resets the calendar. Freedom creates a new beginning for time itself.

thenVsNow.then: God literally resets Israel's calendar so that their year begins with liberation, not with agricultural cycles or royal accessions. Their identity starts with rescue.
thenVsNow.now: The Jewish calendar still begins with Nisan, the month of the Exodus. Christians see a parallel: new life in Christ resets everything. Both traditions organize time around liberation.

verses.text: Speak to all the congregation of Israel, saying, ‘On the tenth day of this month, they shall take to them every man a lamb, according to their fathers’ houses, a lamb for a household;
verses.explainer: Tell all the congregation of Israel: on the tenth day of this month, every household shall take a lamb.

verses.text: and if the household is too little for a lamb, then he and his neighbor next to his house shall take one according to the number of the souls; according to what everyone can eat you shall make your count for the lamb.
verses.explainer: If the household is too small for a lamb, they shall share with a neighbor. The lamb is portioned according to what each person will eat. Community is built into the meal.

verses.text: Your lamb shall be without defect, a male a year old. You shall take it from the sheep, or from the goats:
verses.explainer: The lamb shall be without blemish, a year-old male. You may take it from the sheep or the goats.

verses.text: and you shall keep it until the fourteenth day of the same month; and the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel shall kill it at evening.
verses.explainer: You shall keep it until the fourteenth day of the month, and the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel shall kill their lambs at twilight. Four days of living with the lamb before it dies -- the family bonds with the animal, making the sacrifice personal.

verses.text: They shall take some of the blood, and put it on the two door posts and on the lintel, on the houses in which they shall eat it.
verses.explainer: They shall take the blood and put it on the two doorposts and the lintel of the houses where they eat it. The blood marks the house. It is a sign not for God (who knows who lives where) but for the family -- an active declaration of faith.

verses.text: They shall eat the meat in that night, roasted with fire, and unleavened bread. They shall eat it with bitter herbs.
verses.explainer: They shall eat the flesh that night, roasted with fire. With unleavened bread and bitter herbs. Three elements: the lamb (sacrifice), unleavened bread (haste), bitter herbs (the bitterness of slavery).

verses.text: Don’t eat it raw, nor boiled at all with water, but roasted with fire; with its head, its legs and its inner parts.
verses.explainer: Do not eat it raw or boiled, but roasted with fire -- its head with its legs and inner parts. The lamb is consumed whole; nothing is wasted.

verses.text: You shall let nothing of it remain until the morning; but that which remains of it until the morning you shall burn with fire.
verses.explainer: Nothing shall remain until morning. Whatever is left must be burned. The meal is complete and final.

verses.text: This is how you shall eat it: with your belt on your waist, your shoes on your feet, and your staff in your hand; and you shall eat it in haste: it is Yahweh’s Passover.
verses.explainer: You shall eat it with your belt fastened, your sandals on, and your staff in hand. Eat in haste -- it is the LORD's Passover. This is the meal of people about to move. They eat standing, dressed for travel.

verses.text: For I will go through the land of Egypt in that night, and will strike all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both man and animal. Against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments: I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: I will pass through the land of Egypt that night and strike all the firstborn. On all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments. I am the LORD. The plagues have been a systematic dismantling of Egypt's pantheon. This is the final verdict.

verses.text: The blood shall be to you for a token on the houses where you are: and when I see the blood, I will pass over you, and there shall no plague be on you to destroy you, when I strike the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: The blood shall be a sign for you on your houses. When I see the blood, I will pass over you, and no plague shall fall on you to destroy you. The blood is not magical -- it is the sign of obedient faith. Those who trust God's instruction are spared.

verses.text: This day shall be to you for a memorial, and you shall keep it a feast to Yahweh: throughout your generations you shall keep it a feast by an ordinance forever.
verses.explainer: This day shall be a memorial day for you. You shall keep it as a feast to the LORD throughout your generations, as a statute forever.

verses.text: “‘Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread; even the first day you shall put away yeast out of your houses, for whoever eats leavened bread from the first day until the seventh day, that soul shall be cut off from Israel.
verses.explainer: Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread. On the first day you shall remove leaven from your houses. Whoever eats leavened bread from the first to the seventh day shall be cut off from Israel.

verses.text: In the first day there shall be to you a holy convocation, and in the seventh day a holy convocation; no kind of work shall be done in them, except that which every man must eat, that only may be done by you.
verses.explainer: On the first day and the seventh day, there shall be a holy assembly. No work shall be done except for food preparation.

verses.text: You shall observe the feast of unleavened bread; for in this same day I have brought your armies out of the land of Egypt: therefore you shall observe this day throughout your generations by an ordinance forever.
verses.explainer: You shall observe the Feast of Unleavened Bread, for on this very day I brought your hosts out of Egypt. It is a statute forever.

verses.text: In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month at evening, you shall eat unleavened bread, until the twenty first day of the month at evening.
verses.explainer: In the first month, from the fourteenth day at evening to the twenty-first day at evening, you shall eat unleavened bread.

verses.text: There shall be no yeast found in your houses for seven days, for whoever eats that which is leavened, that soul shall be cut off from the congregation of Israel, whether he is a foreigner, or one who is born in the land.
verses.explainer: For seven days, no leaven shall be found in your houses. Whoever eats what is leavened shall be cut off, whether sojourner or native.

verses.text: You shall eat nothing leavened. In all your habitations you shall eat unleavened bread.’”
verses.explainer: You shall eat nothing leavened. In all your dwelling places you shall eat unleavened bread.

verses.text: Then Moses called for all the elders of Israel, and said to them, “Draw out, and take lambs according to your families, and kill the Passover.
verses.explainer: Moses calls the elders: select lambs according to your families and kill the Passover lamb. The instructions move from God to Moses to the elders to the people.

verses.text: You shall take a bunch of hyssop, and dip it in the blood that is in the basin, and strike the lintel and the two door posts with the blood that is in the basin; and none of you shall go out of the door of his house until the morning.
verses.explainer: Take a bunch of hyssop, dip it in the blood, and touch the lintel and doorposts. None of you shall go out of the door of your house until morning.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Hyssop', 'explanation': 'A small bushy plant used for sprinkling in purification rituals. Hyssop appears again when Jesus is offered sour wine on a hyssop branch at the crucifixion (John 19:29). The plant that applied the first Passover blood appears at the death of the final Passover Lamb.'}

verses.text: For Yahweh will pass through to strike the Egyptians; and when he sees the blood on the lintel, and on the two door posts, Yahweh will pass over the door, and will not allow the destroyer to come in to your houses to strike you.
verses.explainer: The LORD will pass through to strike the Egyptians, and when he sees the blood on the lintel and doorposts, he will pass over the door and not allow the destroyer to enter. The blood is protective.

verses.text: You shall observe this thing for an ordinance to you and to your sons forever.
verses.explainer: You shall observe this as a statute for you and your children forever.

verses.text: It shall happen when you have come to the land which Yahweh will give you, according as he has promised, that you shall keep this service.
verses.explainer: When you enter the land the LORD will give you, you shall keep this service.

verses.text: It will happen, when your children ask you, ‘What do you mean by this service?’
verses.explainer: When your children ask, 'What do you mean by this service?' -- the ritual is designed to provoke questions from the next generation.

thenVsNow.then: The Passover Seder is structured around the questions of the youngest child. The ritual is pedagogical -- it exists to transmit the story from generation to generation.
thenVsNow.now: Jewish families still celebrate Passover today, making it the longest continuously observed religious ritual in the world. The youngest child asks the Four Questions, and the family retells the story.

verses.text:
that you shall say, ‘It is the sacrifice of Yahweh’s Passover, who passed over the houses of the children of Israel in Egypt, when he struck the Egyptians, and spared our houses.’”
The people bowed their heads and worshiped.
verses.explainer: You shall say: 'It is the sacrifice of the LORD's Passover, for he passed over the houses of the Israelites in Egypt when he struck the Egyptians but spared our houses.' The people bow and worship.

verses.text: The children of Israel went and did so; as Yahweh had commanded Moses and Aaron, so they did.
verses.explainer: The Israelites do exactly as the LORD commanded Moses and Aaron.

verses.text: At midnight, Yahweh struck all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sat on his throne to the firstborn of the captive who was in the dungeon; and all the firstborn of livestock.
verses.explainer: At midnight the LORD strikes all the firstborn in Egypt -- from Pharaoh's firstborn on the throne to the firstborn of the captive in the dungeon, and all the firstborn of the livestock. The moment arrives.

verses.text: Pharaoh rose up in the night, he, and all his servants, and all the Egyptians; and there was a great cry in Egypt, for there was not a house where there was not one dead.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh rises in the night -- he, his servants, all Egypt. There is a great cry, for there is not a house without someone dead.

verses.text: He called for Moses and Aaron by night, and said, “Rise up, get out from among my people, both you and the children of Israel; and go, serve Yahweh, as you have said!
verses.explainer: Pharaoh summons Moses and Aaron by night: 'Up, get out from among my people, both you and the Israelites! Go, serve the LORD as you said!' After months of resistance, Pharaoh breaks completely.

verses.text: Take both your flocks and your herds, as you have said, and be gone; and bless me also!”
verses.explainer: Take your flocks and herds and go -- and bless me also. The oppressor now asks the slaves for a blessing. The reversal is total.

verses.text: The Egyptians were urgent with the people, to send them out of the land in haste, for they said, “We are all dead men.”
verses.explainer: The Egyptians are urgent with the people to send them away, saying, 'We shall all be dead.'

verses.text: The people took their dough before it was leavened, their kneading troughs being bound up in their clothes on their shoulders.
verses.explainer: The people take their dough before it is leavened, their kneading bowls wrapped in cloaks on their shoulders. They leave so fast the bread cannot rise.

verses.text: The children of Israel did according to the word of Moses; and they asked of the Egyptians jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and clothing.
verses.explainer: The Israelites do as Moses told them: they ask the Egyptians for silver, gold, and clothing.

verses.text: Yahweh gave the people favor in the sight of the Egyptians, so that they let them have what they asked. They plundered the Egyptians.
verses.explainer: The LORD gives the people favor in the Egyptians' sight, and they let them have what they ask. So they plundered the Egyptians. Wages for 430 years of unpaid labor.

verses.text: The children of Israel traveled from Rameses to Succoth, about six hundred thousand on foot who were men, besides children.
verses.explainer: The Israelites journey from Rameses to Succoth -- about 600,000 men on foot, besides women and children.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': '600,000 men', 'explanation': "This number, implying a total population of 2-3 million, is one of the most debated figures in the Bible. Some scholars take it literally; others note that the Hebrew word for 'thousand' (eleph) can also mean 'clan' or 'military unit,' which would yield a much smaller but still significant number."}

verses.text: A mixed multitude went up also with them, with flocks, herds, and even very much livestock.
verses.explainer: A mixed multitude also goes up with them, along with very much livestock. The Exodus is not ethnically pure -- non-Israelites join the liberation.

verses.text: They baked unleavened cakes of the dough which they brought out of Egypt; for it wasn’t leavened, because they were thrust out of Egypt, and couldn’t wait, and they had not prepared any food for themselves.
verses.explainer: They bake unleavened cakes from the dough they brought from Egypt. It was not leavened because they were driven out and could not wait.

verses.text: Now the time that the children of Israel lived in Egypt was four hundred thirty years.
verses.explainer: The time the Israelites lived in Egypt was 430 years.

verses.text: At the end of four hundred thirty years, to the day, all of Yahweh’s armies went out from the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: At the end of 430 years, on that very day, all the hosts of the LORD went out. God keeps exact time.

verses.text: It is a night to be much observed to Yahweh for bringing them out from the land of Egypt. This is that night of Yahweh, to be much observed of all the children of Israel throughout their generations.
verses.explainer: It was a night of watching by the LORD, to bring them out of Egypt. This same night is a night of watching kept to the LORD by all Israelites throughout their generations.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses and Aaron, “This is the ordinance of the Passover. No foreigner shall eat of it,
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses and Aaron: this is the statute of the Passover. No foreigner shall eat it.

verses.text: but every man’s servant who is bought for money, when you have circumcised him, then shall he eat of it.
verses.explainer: But every slave bought with money may eat it after being circumcised. Participation in the covenant community is possible for anyone.

verses.text: A foreigner and a hired servant shall not eat of it.
verses.explainer: No temporary resident or hired worker may eat it.

verses.text: It must be eaten in one house. You shall not carry any of the meat outside of the house. Do not break any of its bones.
verses.explainer: It shall be eaten in one house. You shall not break any of its bones.

verses.text: All the congregation of Israel shall keep it.
verses.explainer: All the congregation of Israel shall keep it.

verses.text: When a stranger shall live as a foreigner with you, and will keep the Passover to Yahweh, let all his males be circumcised, and then let him come near and keep it; and he shall be as one who is born in the land: but no uncircumcised person shall eat of it.
verses.explainer: If a stranger wants to keep the Passover, all his males must be circumcised. Then he may observe it as a native. There is one law for native and stranger.

verses.text: One law shall be to him who is born at home, and to the stranger who lives as a foreigner among you.”
verses.explainer: There shall be one law for the native and for the stranger who sojourns among you. Equality before God's law.

verses.text: All the children of Israel did so. As Yahweh commanded Moses and Aaron, so they did.
verses.explainer: All the Israelites did just as the LORD commanded Moses and Aaron.

verses.text: That same day, Yahweh brought the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt by their armies.
verses.explainer: On that very day the LORD brought the Israelites out of Egypt by their hosts. The Exodus happens.

explainer.summary: God commands that every firstborn -- human and animal -- be consecrated to him. The firstborn belongs to God because God spared Israel's firstborn in Egypt. Moses tells the people to remember this day and observe the Feast of Unleavened Bread when they enter the Promised Land. God does not lead them by the short coastal route (the Way of the Philistines) but by the longer wilderness road, because they are not ready for war. God leads them with a pillar of cloud by day and a pillar of fire by night.
themes: The pillar of cloud and fire -- visible divine presence
explainer.contextInBook: The Exodus has happened, but the journey has just begun. God establishes rituals of remembrance and provides visible guidance. The pillar of cloud and fire becomes the central symbol of God's presence throughout the wilderness period.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to Moses.

verses.text: “Sanctify to me all the firstborn, whatever opens the womb among the children of Israel, both of man and of animal. It is mine.”
verses.explainer: Consecrate to me every firstborn. Whatever is the first to open the womb among the Israelites, both of man and beast, is mine. The firstborn belongs to God because God spared them.

verses.text: Moses said to the people, “Remember this day, in which you came out of Egypt, out of the house of bondage; for by strength of hand Yahweh brought you out from this place. No leavened bread shall be eaten.
verses.explainer: Moses tells the people: Remember this day in which you came out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery, for by a strong hand the LORD brought you out. No leavened bread shall be eaten.

verses.text: Today you go out in the month Abib.
verses.explainer: Today, in the month of Abib, you are going out.

verses.text: It shall be, when Yahweh shall bring you into the land of the Canaanite, and the Hittite, and the Amorite, and the Hivite, and the Jebusite, which he swore to your fathers to give you, a land flowing with milk and honey, that you shall keep this service in this month.
verses.explainer: When the LORD brings you into the land of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Hivites, and Jebusites -- a land flowing with milk and honey -- you shall keep this service in this month.

verses.text: Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread, and in the seventh day shall be a feast to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread. On the seventh day there shall be a feast to the LORD.

verses.text: Unleavened bread shall be eaten throughout the seven days; and no leavened bread shall be seen with you. No yeast shall be seen with you, within all your borders.
verses.explainer: Unleavened bread shall be eaten for seven days. No leavened bread shall be seen with you, and no leaven shall be seen in all your territory.

verses.text: You shall tell your son in that day, saying, ‘It is because of that which Yahweh did for me when I came out of Egypt.’
verses.explainer: You shall tell your son on that day: 'It is because of what the LORD did for me when I came out of Egypt.' The story is told in first person -- each generation identifies with the original liberation.

verses.text: It shall be for a sign to you on your hand, and for a memorial between your eyes, that Yahweh’s law may be in your mouth; for with a strong hand Yahweh has brought you out of Egypt.
verses.explainer: It shall be as a sign on your hand and a memorial between your eyes, that the law of the LORD may be in your mouth. The memory should be so close it is like something worn on the body.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Sign on your hand and between your eyes', 'explanation': 'This instruction was later taken literally in Judaism through the practice of wearing tefillin (phylacteries) -- small leather boxes containing Torah passages strapped to the arm and forehead during morning prayers. Whether the original intent was literal or metaphorical, the practice has been maintained for millennia.'}

verses.text: You shall therefore keep this ordinance in its season from year to year.
verses.explainer: You shall keep this statute at its appointed time from year to year.

verses.text: “It shall be, when Yahweh shall bring you into the land of the Canaanite, as he swore to you and to your fathers, and shall give it you,
verses.explainer: When the LORD brings you into the land of the Canaanites, as he swore to you and your fathers, and gives it to you --

verses.text: that you shall set apart to Yahweh all that opens the womb, and every firstborn that comes from an animal which you have. The males shall be Yahweh’s.
verses.explainer: You shall set apart to the LORD all that first opens the womb. Every firstborn of your livestock that is male shall be the LORD's.

verses.text: Every firstborn of a donkey you shall redeem with a lamb; and if you will not redeem it, then you shall break its neck; and you shall redeem all the firstborn of man among your sons.
verses.explainer: Every firstborn donkey you shall redeem with a lamb, or break its neck. Every firstborn of man among your sons you shall redeem. Human firstborn are redeemed, not sacrificed -- a crucial distinction from surrounding cultures.

verses.text: It shall be, when your son asks you in time to come, saying, ‘What is this?’ that you shall tell him, ‘By strength of hand Yahweh brought us out from Egypt, from the house of bondage.
verses.explainer: When your son asks in time to come, 'What does this mean?' you shall say, 'By a strong hand the LORD brought us out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.' The ritual exists to provoke questions.

verses.text: When Pharaoh stubbornly refused to let us go, Yahweh killed all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both the firstborn of man, and the firstborn of livestock. Therefore I sacrifice to Yahweh all that opens the womb, being males; but all the firstborn of my sons I redeem.’
verses.explainer: When Pharaoh stubbornly refused to let us go, the LORD killed all the firstborn in Egypt, both of man and beast. Therefore I sacrifice to the LORD every male that first opens the womb, but every firstborn of my sons I redeem.

verses.text: It shall be for a sign on your hand, and for symbols between your eyes: for by strength of hand Yahweh brought us out of Egypt.”
verses.explainer: It shall be as a mark on your hand and frontlets between your eyes. By a strong hand the LORD brought us out of Egypt.

verses.text: When Pharaoh had let the people go, God didn’t lead them by the way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near; for God said, “Lest perhaps the people change their minds when they see war, and they return to Egypt”;
verses.explainer: When Pharaoh let the people go, God did not lead them by the way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near. God said, 'Lest the people change their minds when they see war and return to Egypt.' God knows their capacity and does not push them beyond it.

thenVsNow.then: The coastal road (the Way of Horus) was the most direct route to Canaan but was heavily fortified by Egyptian military outposts. Newly freed slaves were in no condition to fight through these garrisons.
thenVsNow.now: God's indirect route teaches that the shortest path is not always the wisest. Sometimes God leads through the wilderness because we are not ready for what the direct route demands.

verses.text: but God led the people around by the way of the wilderness by the Red Sea; and the children of Israel went up armed out of the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: God leads the people by the roundabout way of the wilderness toward the Red Sea. The Israelites go up out of Egypt equipped for battle.

verses.text: Moses took the bones of Joseph with him, for he had made the children of Israel swear, saying, “God will surely visit you, and you shall carry up my bones away from here with you.”
verses.explainer: Moses takes the bones of Joseph with him, for Joseph had made the sons of Israel solemnly swear: 'God will surely visit you, and you shall carry my bones up from here.' Joseph's faith reaches across four centuries.

verses.text: They took their journey from Succoth, and encamped in Etham, in the edge of the wilderness.
verses.explainer: They set out from Succoth and encamp at Etham, on the edge of the wilderness.

verses.text: Yahweh went before them by day in a pillar of cloud, to lead them on their way, and by night in a pillar of fire, to give them light, that they might go by day and by night:
verses.explainer: The LORD goes before them by day in a pillar of cloud to lead the way, and by night in a pillar of fire to give them light, that they might travel by day and by night. God's presence is visible, constant, and practical.

verses.text: the pillar of cloud by day, and the pillar of fire by night, didn’t depart from before the people.
verses.explainer: The pillar of cloud by day and pillar of fire by night did not depart from before the people. God's presence is constant and unwavering.

explainer.summary: The most celebrated miracle in the Old Testament. God tells Moses to camp by the sea, knowing Pharaoh will pursue them. Pharaoh changes his mind and sends his entire army after the Israelites. Trapped between the sea and the chariots, the people panic. Moses says: 'Fear not, stand firm, and see the salvation of the LORD.' God parts the sea, Israel walks through on dry ground, and the waters close over the pursuing Egyptian army. Israel sees the great power of God and believes.
explainer.contextInBook: This is the climax of the Exodus narrative. Everything from chapter 1 has been building to this moment. The Red Sea crossing becomes the defining act of God in the Old Testament -- the event referenced more than any other.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Deliverance, rescue, victory
wordStudies.meaning: Deliverance, rescue, victory. The Hebrew root is the same as the name Joshua (and later, Jesus). When Moses says 'see the salvation of the LORD,' he uses the word that will become the name of the Messiah.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses to turn back and camp before Pi-hahiroth, between Migdol and the sea.

verses.text: “Speak to the children of Israel, that they turn back and encamp before Pihahiroth, between Migdol and the sea, before Baal Zephon. You shall encamp opposite it by the sea.
verses.explainer: Camp in front of Baal-zephon, by the sea. God positions them in what appears to be a trap.

verses.text: Pharaoh will say of the children of Israel, ‘They are entangled in the land. The wilderness has shut them in.’
verses.explainer: Pharaoh will say: 'They are wandering aimlessly. The wilderness has shut them in.' God engineers the situation to draw Pharaoh into pursuit.

verses.text: I will harden Pharaoh’s heart, and he will follow after them; and I will get honor over Pharaoh, and over all his armies; and the Egyptians shall know that I am Yahweh.” They did so.
verses.explainer: I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and he will pursue them. I will get glory over Pharaoh and all his host, and the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD. The final confrontation is by divine design.

verses.text: The king of Egypt was told that the people had fled; and the heart of Pharaoh and of his servants was changed towards the people, and they said, “What is this we have done, that we have let Israel go from serving us?”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh is told the people have fled. His heart changes: 'What have we done, letting Israel go from serving us?'

verses.text: He prepared his chariot, and took his army with him;
verses.explainer: He makes ready his chariot and takes his army with him.

verses.text: and he took six hundred chosen chariots, and all the chariots of Egypt, and captains over all of them.
verses.explainer: He takes 600 chosen chariots and all the other chariots of Egypt with officers over all of them. The full military might of the ancient world's superpower.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': '600 chosen chariots', 'explanation': "Chariots were the most advanced military technology of the ancient world -- the equivalent of tanks. Egypt's chariot corps was legendary. Sending 600 elite chariots plus additional forces against unarmed former slaves was overwhelming force designed to crush any resistance."}

verses.text: Yahweh hardened the heart of Pharaoh king of Egypt, and he pursued the children of Israel; for the children of Israel went out with a high hand.
verses.explainer: The LORD hardens Pharaoh's heart, and he pursues the Israelites. The Israelites had left boldly.

verses.text: The Egyptians pursued them. All the horses and chariots of Pharaoh, his horsemen, and his army overtook them encamping by the sea, beside Pihahiroth, before Baal Zephon.
verses.explainer: The Egyptians pursue them -- all Pharaoh's horses, chariots, horsemen, and army -- and overtake them camped by the sea.

verses.text: When Pharaoh came near, the children of Israel lifted up their eyes, and behold, the Egyptians were marching after them; and they were very afraid. The children of Israel cried out to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: When Pharaoh draws near, the Israelites look up and see the Egyptians marching after them. They are terrified and cry out to the LORD.

thenVsNow.then: The Israelites were hemmed in: sea in front, mountains on the sides, and the most powerful army in the world behind them. From a military perspective, their situation was hopeless.
thenVsNow.now: The Red Sea moment -- trapped with no human solution -- is a universal experience. The text teaches that the moment of apparent impossibility is often the moment just before God acts.

verses.text: They said to Moses, “Because there were no graves in Egypt, have you taken us away to die in the wilderness? Why have you treated us this way, to bring us out of Egypt?
verses.explainer: They say to Moses: 'Was it because there were no graves in Egypt that you brought us to the wilderness to die?' The sarcasm is sharp -- Egypt was famous for its elaborate tombs.

verses.text: Isn’t this the word that we spoke to you in Egypt, saying, ‘Leave us alone, that we may serve the Egyptians?’ For it would have been better for us to serve the Egyptians than to die in the wilderness.”
verses.explainer: 'Didn't we say in Egypt, Leave us alone and let us serve the Egyptians? It would have been better to serve the Egyptians than to die in the wilderness.' Fear makes slavery look preferable to freedom.

verses.text: Moses said to the people, “Don’t be afraid. Stand still, and see the salvation of Yahweh, which he will work for you today: for the Egyptians whom you have seen today, you shall never see them again.
verses.explainer: Moses tells the people: 'Fear not, stand firm, and see the salvation of the LORD, which he will work for you today. The Egyptians you see today you shall never see again.' One of the greatest declarations of faith in Scripture.

verses.text: Yahweh will fight for you, and you shall be still.”
verses.explainer: 'The LORD will fight for you, and you have only to be silent.' God does not need Israel's help. He fights alone.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Why do you cry to me? Speak to the children of Israel, that they go forward.
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: 'Why do you cry to me? Tell the Israelites to move forward.' Even faith requires action. Stand firm, but then step into the water.

verses.text: Lift up your rod, and stretch out your hand over the sea, and divide it: and the children of Israel shall go into the middle of the sea on dry ground.
verses.explainer: Lift up your staff and stretch out your hand over the sea and divide it, that the Israelites may go through on dry ground.

verses.text: Behold, I myself will harden the hearts of the Egyptians, and they shall go in after them: and I will get myself honor over Pharaoh, and over all his armies, over his chariots, and over his horsemen.
verses.explainer: I will harden the hearts of the Egyptians so they will follow them. I will get glory over Pharaoh, his chariots, and his horsemen.

verses.text: The Egyptians shall know that I am Yahweh, when I have gotten myself honor over Pharaoh, over his chariots, and over his horsemen.”
verses.explainer: The Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I have gotten glory over Pharaoh. Even the enemies of God come to know who he is through judgment.

verses.text: The angel of God, who went before the camp of Israel, moved and went behind them; and the pillar of cloud moved from before them, and stood behind them.
verses.explainer: The angel of God who was going before the Israelites moves and goes behind them. The pillar of cloud moves from in front to behind them. God places himself between his people and their enemies.

verses.text: It came between the camp of Egypt and the camp of Israel; and there was the cloud and the darkness, yet gave it light by night: and one didn’t come near the other all night.
verses.explainer: The cloud is darkness to the Egyptians but light to the Israelites. The same presence that comforts God's people terrifies their enemies. All night the two camps do not come near each other.

verses.text: Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and Yahweh caused the sea to go back by a strong east wind all night, and made the sea dry land, and the waters were divided.
verses.explainer: Moses stretches out his hand over the sea. The LORD drives the sea back by a strong east wind all night and makes the sea dry land, and the waters are divided. The miracle works through natural means (wind) directed by supernatural power.

verses.text: The children of Israel went into the middle of the sea on the dry ground, and the waters were a wall to them on their right hand, and on their left.
verses.explainer: The Israelites go into the midst of the sea on dry ground, the waters being a wall to them on their right hand and on their left. They walk between walls of water. The image is unforgettable.

verses.text: The Egyptians pursued, and went in after them into the middle of the sea: all of Pharaoh’s horses, his chariots, and his horsemen.
verses.explainer: The Egyptians pursue and go in after them -- all Pharaoh's horses, chariots, and horsemen.

verses.text: In the morning watch, Yahweh looked out on the Egyptian army through the pillar of fire and of cloud, and confused the Egyptian army.
verses.explainer: In the morning watch, the LORD in the pillar of fire and cloud looks down on the Egyptian army and throws them into panic.

verses.text: He took off their chariot wheels, and they drove them heavily; so that the Egyptians said, “Let’s flee from the face of Israel, for Yahweh fights for them against the Egyptians!”
verses.explainer: He clogs their chariot wheels so they drive heavily. The Egyptians say: 'Let us flee from Israel, for the LORD fights for them against Egypt.' Even the Egyptians recognize what is happening -- too late.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Stretch out your hand over the sea, that the waters may come again on the Egyptians, on their chariots, and on their horsemen.”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: Stretch out your hand over the sea, that the water may come back upon the Egyptians, their chariots, and their horsemen.

verses.text: Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and the sea returned to its strength when the morning appeared; and the Egyptians fled against it. Yahweh overthrew the Egyptians in the middle of the sea.
verses.explainer: Moses stretches out his hand over the sea. At dawn, the sea returns to its normal course. The Egyptians flee into it, and the LORD overthrows them in the midst of the sea.

verses.text: The waters returned, and covered the chariots and the horsemen, even all Pharaoh’s army that went in after them into the sea. There remained not so much as one of them.
verses.explainer: The waters return and cover the chariots and horsemen -- all the host of Pharaoh that followed them into the sea. Not one of them remains.

verses.text: But the children of Israel walked on dry land in the middle of the sea, and the waters were a wall to them on their right hand, and on their left.
verses.explainer: But the Israelites walk on dry ground through the sea, the waters being a wall on their right and on their left.

verses.text: Thus Yahweh saved Israel that day out of the hand of the Egyptians; and Israel saw the Egyptians dead on the seashore.
verses.explainer: Thus the LORD saves Israel that day from the hand of the Egyptians. Israel sees the Egyptians dead on the seashore.

verses.text: Israel saw the great work which Yahweh did to the Egyptians, and the people feared Yahweh; and they believed in Yahweh, and in his servant Moses.
verses.explainer: Israel sees the great power the LORD used against the Egyptians. The people fear the LORD and believe in the LORD and in his servant Moses. Seeing leads to fearing leads to believing. The Exodus creates faith.

thenVsNow.then: This moment of collective belief at the seashore is the birth of Israel as a nation of faith. They had been slaves; now they are believers. The sight of God's power transforms them.
thenVsNow.now: The Red Sea crossing is the Old Testament's central image of salvation -- passing through death to life, through chaos to safety. Christian theology sees baptism as the believer's Red Sea crossing (1 Corinthians 10:1-2).

explainer.summary: Israel responds to the Red Sea miracle with one of the oldest and greatest poems in the Bible -- the Song of the Sea (also called the Song of Moses). It celebrates God as a warrior who has thrown horse and rider into the sea. Miriam leads the women in a response. But the mood shifts quickly: three days into the wilderness, the people find only bitter water at Marah. God shows Moses a tree that makes the water sweet. God promises that if they obey, they will not suffer the diseases of Egypt.
themes: Worship as the response to salvation
The abrupt transition from triumph to testing
explainer.contextInBook: The Song of the Sea is the theological high point of the Exodus narrative -- Israel's first great act of worship. But within three days, the people are complaining. This pattern of deliverance followed by complaint will define the wilderness period.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Bitterness
wordStudies.meaning: Bitterness. The place is named for its undrinkable water. The wordplay is intentional: the first experience after the ecstasy of the Red Sea is bitterness. The name captures the reality that faith journeys alternate between triumph and trial.

verses.text:
Then Moses and the children of Israel sang this song to Yahweh, and said,
“I will sing to Yahweh, for he has triumphed gloriously.
The horse and his rider he has thrown into the sea.
verses.explainer: Then Moses and the Israelites sing this song to the LORD: 'I will sing to the LORD, for he has triumphed gloriously; horse and rider he has thrown into the sea.' The first line of the oldest victory hymn in the Bible.

verses.text:
Yah is my strength and song.
He has become my salvation.
This is my God, and I will praise him;
my father’s God, and I will exalt him.
verses.explainer: The LORD is my strength and my song, and he has become my salvation. He is my God, and I will praise him; my father's God, and I will exalt him. Personal and communal faith converge.

verses.text:
Yahweh is a man of war.
Yahweh is his name.
verses.explainer: The LORD is a man of war; the LORD is his name. God is identified as a warrior who fights for the helpless.

verses.text:
He has cast Pharaoh’s chariots and his army into the sea.
His chosen captains are sunk in the Red Sea.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh's chariots and his host he cast into the sea. His chosen officers were sunk in the Red Sea.

verses.text:
The deeps cover them.
They went down into the depths like a stone.
verses.explainer: The floods covered them; they went down into the depths like a stone.

verses.text:
Your right hand, Yahweh, is glorious in power.
Your right hand, Yahweh, dashes the enemy in pieces.
verses.explainer: Your right hand, O LORD, glorious in power, your right hand shatters the enemy.

verses.text:
In the greatness of your excellency, you overthrow those who rise up against you.
You send out your wrath. It consumes them as stubble.
verses.explainer: In the greatness of your majesty you overthrow your adversaries. You send out your fury; it consumes them like stubble.

verses.text:
With the blast of your nostrils, the waters were piled up.
The floods stood upright as a heap.
The deeps were congealed in the heart of the sea.
verses.explainer: At the blast of your nostrils the waters piled up; the floods stood upright in a heap; the deeps congealed in the heart of the sea. Poetic description of the parting.

verses.text:
The enemy said, ‘I will pursue. I will overtake. I will divide the plunder.
My desire shall be satisfied on them.
I will draw my sword, my hand shall destroy them.’
verses.explainer: The enemy said, 'I will pursue, I will overtake, I will divide the spoil, my desire shall have its fill of them.' The enemy's boast is quoted to heighten the reversal.

verses.text:
You blew with your wind.
The sea covered them.
They sank like lead in the mighty waters.
verses.explainer: You blew with your wind; the sea covered them. They sank like lead in the mighty waters.

verses.text:
Who is like you, Yahweh, among the gods?
Who is like you, glorious in holiness,
fearful in praises, doing wonders?
verses.explainer: Who is like you, O LORD, among the gods? Who is like you, majestic in holiness, awesome in glorious deeds, doing wonders? The rhetorical question expects the answer: no one.

verses.text:
You stretched out your right hand.
The earth swallowed them.
verses.explainer: You stretched out your right hand; the earth swallowed them.

verses.text:
“You, in your loving kindness, have led the people that you have redeemed.
You have guided them in your strength to your holy habitation.
verses.explainer: You have led in your steadfast love the people whom you have redeemed. You have guided them by your strength to your holy abode. The song looks forward to the Promised Land.

verses.text:
The peoples have heard.
They tremble.
Pangs have taken hold on the inhabitants of Philistia.
verses.explainer: The peoples have heard; they tremble. Pangs have seized the inhabitants of Philistia.

verses.text:
Then the chiefs of Edom were dismayed.
Trembling takes hold of the mighty men of Moab.
All the inhabitants of Canaan have melted away.
verses.explainer: The chiefs of Edom are dismayed; trembling seizes the leaders of Moab; all the inhabitants of Canaan have melted away.

verses.text:
Terror and dread falls on them.
By the greatness of your arm they are as still as a stone—
until your people pass over, Yahweh,
until the people you have purchased pass over.
verses.explainer: Terror and dread fall upon them. Because of the greatness of your arm, they are still as a stone, till your people pass by, O LORD.

You will bring them in and plant them on your own mountain, the place you have made for your dwelling, the sanctuary the LORD has established with his hands. The song anticipates both the Promised Land and the Temple.

verses.text: Yahweh shall reign forever and ever.”
verses.explainer: The LORD will reign forever and ever. The final declaration: God is the eternal king.

verses.text: For the horses of Pharaoh went in with his chariots and with his horsemen into the sea, and Yahweh brought back the waters of the sea on them; but the children of Israel walked on dry land in the middle of the sea.
verses.explainer: For when Pharaoh's horses with his chariots and horsemen went into the sea, the LORD brought back the waters upon them. But the Israelites walked on dry ground. The narrative summary restating the miracle.

verses.text: Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron, took a tambourine in her hand; and all the women went out after her with tambourines and with dances.
verses.explainer: Miriam the prophetess, Aaron's sister, takes a tambourine, and all the women go out after her with tambourines and dancing. Miriam leads the women's worship.

verses.text:
Miriam answered them,
“Sing to Yahweh, for he has triumphed gloriously.
The horse and his rider he has thrown into the sea.”
verses.explainer: Miriam sings to them: 'Sing to the LORD, for he has triumphed gloriously; horse and rider he has thrown into the sea.' She sings the same words as Moses -- or perhaps Moses expanded her original song.

verses.text: Moses led Israel onward from the Red Sea, and they went out into the wilderness of Shur; and they went three days in the wilderness, and found no water.
verses.explainer: Moses leads Israel from the Red Sea into the wilderness of Shur. They go three days without finding water.

verses.text: When they came to Marah, they couldn’t drink from the waters of Marah, for they were bitter. Therefore its name was called Marah.
verses.explainer: They come to Marah but cannot drink the water because it is bitter. So the place is called Marah.

verses.text: The people murmured against Moses, saying, “What shall we drink?”
verses.explainer: The people grumble against Moses: 'What shall we drink?' Three days after the greatest miracle in history, they complain. Faith is fragile.
