verses.text: Then he cried to Yahweh. Yahweh showed him a tree, and he threw it into the waters, and the waters were made sweet. There he made a statute and an ordinance for them, and there he tested them;
verses.explainer: Moses cries to the LORD, and the LORD shows him a log. He throws it into the water, and the water becomes sweet. There the LORD makes a statute and a rule for them, and there he tests them.

thenVsNow.then: A tree makes bitter water sweet. The provision is unexpected and simple. God does not create a river or transport them to an oasis -- he transforms what is already there.
thenVsNow.now: The image of something bitter being made sweet resonates spiritually. Suffering transformed by God's presence is a central Christian theme. The cross itself is the tree that transforms the bitterness of death into the sweetness of redemption.

verses.text: and he said, “If you will diligently listen to Yahweh your God’s voice, and will do that which is right in his eyes, and will pay attention to his commandments, and keep all his statutes, I will put none of the diseases on you, which I have put on the Egyptians; for I am Yahweh who heals you.”
verses.explainer: God says: 'If you will diligently listen to the voice of the LORD your God, and do what is right in his eyes, and keep his statutes, I will put none of the diseases on you that I put on the Egyptians, for I am the LORD, your healer.' The first conditional promise.

verses.text: They came to Elim, where there were twelve springs of water, and seventy palm trees: and they encamped there by the waters.
verses.explainer: They come to Elim, where there are twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees. They encamp there by the water. Twelve springs for twelve tribes. The abundance after the bitter water is God's provision.

explainer.summary: The whole congregation grumbles: they wish they had died in Egypt where at least they had bread and meat. God responds not with punishment but with provision: quail in the evening and manna -- a mysterious bread-like substance -- every morning. The manna teaches daily dependence on God: take only what you need for the day, because hoarding causes it to rot. On the sixth day, gather double because there will be none on the Sabbath. Some hoard anyway and find worms; some go out on the Sabbath and find nothing. The pattern teaches that God provides, but provision requires trust.
themes: The discipline of trust and daily dependence
The Sabbath as built into creation's rhythm
Manna as the bread of heaven
explainer.contextInBook: Manna is the primary physical provision of the wilderness period, lasting forty years. It teaches Israel that life depends not on human effort alone but on God's word (Deuteronomy 8:3). Jesus later calls himself the bread of life, the true manna from heaven.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The people see it and say 'man hu?' -- 'What is it
wordStudies.meaning: The people see it and say 'man hu?' -- 'What is it?' The food is named for the question it provokes. Nobody knows what it is except that God gives it. Described as fine, flake-like, white, tasting like wafers made with honey.

verses.text: They took their journey from Elim, and all the congregation of the children of Israel came to the wilderness of Sin, which is between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after their departing out of the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: They set out from Elim. The whole congregation comes to the wilderness of Sin, between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after they departed Egypt.

verses.text: The whole congregation of the children of Israel murmured against Moses and against Aaron in the wilderness;
verses.explainer: The whole congregation grumbles against Moses and Aaron in the wilderness.

verses.text: and the children of Israel said to them, “We wish that we had died by Yahweh’s hand in the land of Egypt, when we sat by the meat pots, when we ate our fill of bread, for you have brought us out into this wilderness, to kill this whole assembly with hunger.”
verses.explainer: The Israelites say: 'Would that we had died by the hand of the LORD in Egypt, where we sat by the meat pots and ate bread to the full! You have brought us out to kill this whole assembly with hunger.' Selective memory: they remember Egyptian food but not Egyptian whips.

thenVsNow.then: The nostalgia for slavery is psychologically realistic. Enslaved people often had basic sustenance provided by their masters. Freedom brought the terror of uncertainty.
thenVsNow.now: The pull back toward old patterns -- even harmful ones -- simply because they are familiar is a universal human experience. Freedom is terrifying precisely because it demands responsibility.

verses.text: Then Yahweh said to Moses, “Behold, I will rain bread from the sky for you, and the people shall go out and gather a day’s portion every day, that I may test them, whether they will walk in my law, or not.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: I will rain bread from heaven for you. The people shall go out and gather a day's portion every day, that I may test them, whether they will walk in my law or not. Provision and testing are intertwined.

verses.text: It shall come to pass on the sixth day, that they shall prepare that which they bring in, and it shall be twice as much as they gather daily.”
verses.explainer: On the sixth day, when they prepare what they bring in, it will be twice as much as they gather daily. The Sabbath principle is built into the provision.

verses.text: Moses and Aaron said to all the children of Israel, “At evening, then you shall know that Yahweh has brought you out from the land of Egypt;
verses.explainer: Moses and Aaron tell the people: At evening you shall know that the LORD brought you out of Egypt.

verses.text: and in the morning, then you shall see Yahweh’s glory; because he hears your murmurings against Yahweh. Who are we, that you murmur against us?”
verses.explainer: In the morning you shall see the glory of the LORD, because he has heard your grumbling against the LORD. What are we, that you grumble against us?

verses.text: Moses said, “Now Yahweh shall give you meat to eat in the evening, and in the morning bread to satisfy you; because Yahweh hears your murmurings which you murmur against him. And who are we? Your murmurings are not against us, but against Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: When the LORD gives you meat in the evening and bread in the morning -- it is not against us that you grumble but against the LORD.

verses.text: Moses said to Aaron, “Tell all the congregation of the children of Israel, ‘Come near before Yahweh, for he has heard your murmurings.’”
verses.explainer: Moses tells Aaron: Say to the whole congregation, 'Come near before the LORD, for he has heard your grumbling.'

verses.text: As Aaron spoke to the whole congregation of the children of Israel, they looked toward the wilderness, and behold, Yahweh’s glory appeared in the cloud.
verses.explainer: As Aaron speaks, they look toward the wilderness, and the glory of the LORD appears in the cloud. God shows up in response to complaint -- not to punish but to provide.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to Moses.

verses.text: “I have heard the murmurings of the children of Israel. Speak to them, saying, ‘At evening you shall eat meat, and in the morning you shall be filled with bread: and you shall know that I am Yahweh your God.’”
verses.explainer: I have heard the grumbling of the Israelites. Tell them: 'At twilight you shall eat meat, and in the morning you shall be filled with bread. Then you shall know that I am the LORD your God.'

verses.text: In the evening, quail came up and covered the camp; and in the morning the dew lay around the camp.
verses.explainer: In the evening quail come up and cover the camp. In the morning dew lies around the camp.

verses.text: When the dew that lay had gone, behold, on the surface of the wilderness was a small round thing, small as the frost on the ground.
verses.explainer: When the dew lifts, there is on the surface of the wilderness a fine, flake-like thing, fine as frost on the ground. Something nobody has seen before.

verses.text: When the children of Israel saw it, they said to one another, “What is it?” For they didn’t know what it was. Moses said to them, “It is the bread which Yahweh has given you to eat.”
verses.explainer: The Israelites see it and say to one another, 'What is it?' -- man hu in Hebrew. Moses says: 'It is the bread the LORD has given you to eat.'

verses.text: This is the thing which Yahweh has commanded: “Gather of it everyone according to his eating; an omer a head, according to the number of your persons, you shall take it, every man for those who are in his tent.”
verses.explainer: The LORD commands: Gather as much as each person needs, an omer per person according to the number of people in your tent.

verses.text: The children of Israel did so, and gathered some more, some less.
verses.explainer: The Israelites do so. Some gather more, some less.

verses.text: When they measured it with an omer, he who gathered much had nothing over, and he who gathered little had no lack. They gathered every man according to his eating.
verses.explainer: When they measure it with an omer, whoever gathered much has nothing left over, and whoever gathered little has no lack. Each gathers according to need. A miracle of equitable distribution.

verses.text: Moses said to them, “Let no one leave of it until the morning.”
verses.explainer: Moses tells them: Let no one leave any until morning.

verses.text: Notwithstanding they didn’t listen to Moses, but some of them left of it until the morning, and it bred worms, and became foul: and Moses was angry with them.
verses.explainer: But they do not listen. Some leave part until morning, and it breeds worms and stinks. Moses is angry. Hoarding what God provides daily is an act of distrust.

verses.text: They gathered it morning by morning, everyone according to his eating. When the sun grew hot, it melted.
verses.explainer: They gather it morning by morning, each according to need. When the sun grows hot, it melts. The manna is available only to those who rise and gather it.

verses.text: On the sixth day, they gathered twice as much bread, two omers for each one, and all the rulers of the congregation came and told Moses.
verses.explainer: On the sixth day they gather twice as much, two omers per person. The leaders of the congregation come and tell Moses.

verses.text: He said to them, “This is that which Yahweh has spoken, ‘Tomorrow is a solemn rest, a holy Sabbath to Yahweh. Bake that which you want to bake, and boil that which you want to boil; and all that remains over lay up for yourselves to be kept until the morning.’”
verses.explainer: Moses says: This is what the LORD has commanded. Tomorrow is a day of solemn rest, a holy Sabbath to the LORD. Bake what you will bake, boil what you will boil, and all that is left over set aside until morning. The first Sabbath instruction in the wilderness.

verses.text: They laid it up until the morning, as Moses asked, and it didn’t become foul, and there were no worms in it.
verses.explainer: They set it aside until morning, and it does not stink or have worms. What rots on any other day is preserved for the Sabbath. The Sabbath has its own physics.

verses.text: Moses said, “Eat that today, for today is a Sabbath to Yahweh. Today you shall not find it in the field.
verses.explainer: Moses says: Eat it today, for today is a Sabbath to the LORD. Today you will not find it in the field.

verses.text: Six days you shall gather it, but on the seventh day is the Sabbath. In it there shall be none.”
verses.explainer: Six days you shall gather it, but on the seventh day, the Sabbath, there will be none.

verses.text: On the seventh day, some of the people went out to gather, and they found none.
verses.explainer: On the seventh day some go out to gather and find none.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “How long do you refuse to keep my commandments and my laws?
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: How long will you refuse to keep my commandments and laws?

verses.text: Behold, because Yahweh has given you the Sabbath, therefore he gives you on the sixth day the bread of two days. Everyone stay in his place. Let no one go out of his place on the seventh day.”
verses.explainer: See! The LORD has given you the Sabbath; therefore on the sixth day he gives you bread for two days. Stay in your place on the seventh day. No one is to go out.

verses.text: So the people rested on the seventh day.
verses.explainer: So the people rest on the seventh day.

verses.text: The house of Israel called its name Manna, and it was like coriander seed, white; and its taste was like wafers with honey.
verses.explainer: The house of Israel calls the bread manna. It is like coriander seed, white, and tastes like wafers made with honey. Sweet provision from a barren wilderness.

verses.text: Moses said, “This is the thing which Yahweh has commanded, ‘Let an omer-full of it be kept throughout your generations, that they may see the bread with which I fed you in the wilderness, when I brought you out of the land of Egypt.’”
verses.explainer: Moses says: This is what the LORD commands: Keep an omer of manna throughout your generations, so they may see the bread with which I fed you in the wilderness.

verses.text: Moses said to Aaron, “Take a pot, and put an omer-full of manna in it, and lay it up before Yahweh, to be kept throughout your generations.”
verses.explainer: Moses tells Aaron: Take a jar, put an omer of manna in it, and place it before the LORD to be kept throughout your generations.

verses.text: As Yahweh commanded Moses, so Aaron laid it up before the Testimony, to be kept.
verses.explainer: As the LORD commanded Moses, Aaron places it before the Testimony to be kept.

verses.text: The children of Israel ate the manna forty years, until they came to an inhabited land. They ate the manna until they came to the borders of the land of Canaan.
verses.explainer: The Israelites eat manna forty years, until they come to a habitable land. They eat manna until they reach the border of Canaan. Forty years of daily bread from heaven.

verses.text: Now an omer is one tenth of an ephah.
verses.explainer: An omer is the tenth part of an ephah. The measurement note for future readers.

explainer.summary: Two crises. First, no water: the people quarrel with Moses so fiercely he fears they will stone him. God tells Moses to strike the rock at Horeb, and water flows. Moses names the place Massah ('testing') and Meribah ('quarreling'). Second, the Amalekites attack. Joshua leads the army while Moses holds up his staff on a hilltop. When Moses' arms are raised, Israel prevails; when they drop, Amalek prevails. Aaron and Hur hold up his weary arms until sunset, and Joshua defeats Amalek.
themes: The role of community in sustaining leaders
Joshua's first appearance as a military leader
explainer.contextInBook: The water-from-rock miracle becomes one of the most referenced events in the Old Testament. The battle with Amalek introduces Joshua and the pattern of spiritual warfare: victory depends on more than military strength.

verses.text: All the congregation of the children of Israel traveled from the wilderness of Sin, by their journeys, according to Yahweh’s commandment, and encamped in Rephidim; but there was no water for the people to drink.
verses.explainer: The whole congregation journeys from the wilderness of Sin in stages, as the LORD commands. They camp at Rephidim, but there is no water for the people to drink.

verses.text:
Therefore the people quarreled with Moses, and said, “Give us water to drink.”
Moses said to them, “Why do you quarrel with me? Why do you test Yahweh?”
verses.explainer: The people quarrel with Moses: 'Give us water to drink.' Moses says: 'Why do you quarrel with me? Why do you test the LORD?'

verses.text: The people were thirsty for water there; and the people murmured against Moses, and said, “Why have you brought us up out of Egypt, to kill us, our children, and our livestock with thirst?”
verses.explainer: The people thirst and grumble against Moses: 'Why did you bring us up out of Egypt to kill us and our children and livestock with thirst?'

verses.text: Moses cried to Yahweh, saying, “What shall I do with these people? They are almost ready to stone me.”
verses.explainer: Moses cries to the LORD: 'What shall I do with this people? They are almost ready to stone me.' The leader's desperation is raw.

thenVsNow.then: Moses faces a mob of hundreds of thousands of thirsty, angry people in a waterless desert. His fear of being stoned is not paranoia -- it is a realistic assessment. Leadership of the newly freed is dangerous.
thenVsNow.now: Leaders under pressure from those they serve face an ancient dilemma: the people they are trying to help may turn on them. Moses' prayer -- 'What shall I do?' -- is the prayer of every overwhelmed leader.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Walk on before the people, and take the elders of Israel with you, and take the rod in your hand with which you struck the Nile, and go.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: Go on ahead of the people, take with you some of the elders. Take in your hand the staff with which you struck the Nile.

verses.text: Behold, I will stand before you there on the rock in Horeb. You shall strike the rock, and water will come out of it, that the people may drink.” Moses did so in the sight of the elders of Israel.
verses.explainer: I will stand before you on the rock at Horeb. Strike the rock, and water will come out of it for the people to drink. Moses does so in the sight of the elders of Israel.

verses.text: He called the name of the place Massah, and Meribah, because the children of Israel quarreled, and because they tested Yahweh, saying, “Is Yahweh among us, or not?”
verses.explainer: He calls the place Massah and Meribah -- 'Testing' and 'Quarreling' -- because the Israelites quarreled and tested the LORD, saying, 'Is the LORD among us or not?' The fundamental question of the wilderness.

verses.text: Then Amalek came and fought with Israel in Rephidim.
verses.explainer: Then Amalek comes and fights with Israel at Rephidim. The first military conflict. Amalek attacks without provocation.

verses.text: Moses said to Joshua, “Choose men for us, and go out, fight with Amalek. Tomorrow I will stand on the top of the hill with God’s rod in my hand.”
verses.explainer: Moses says to Joshua: 'Choose some men and go out and fight Amalek. Tomorrow I will stand on the top of the hill with the staff of God in my hand.' Joshua's first mission.

verses.text: So Joshua did as Moses had told him, and fought with Amalek; and Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill.
verses.explainer: Joshua does as Moses tells him and fights Amalek. Moses, Aaron, and Hur go up to the top of the hill.

verses.text: When Moses held up his hand, Israel prevailed. When he let down his hand, Amalek prevailed.
verses.explainer: Whenever Moses holds up his hand, Israel prevails. Whenever he lowers his hand, Amalek prevails. The battle is won not by military might alone but by intercessory prayer.

thenVsNow.then: The upraised staff was a sign of divine authority and intercessory prayer. Moses was not performing magic -- he was interceding. When he stopped, the intercession ceased and Israel's advantage evaporated.
thenVsNow.now: The image of Moses' upraised arms has become the iconic picture of intercession in Jewish and Christian tradition. Prayer sustains the fight; its absence leads to defeat.

verses.text: But Moses’ hands were heavy; and they took a stone, and put it under him, and he sat on it. Aaron and Hur held up his hands, the one on the one side, and the other on the other side. His hands were steady until sunset.
verses.explainer: When Moses' hands grow heavy, they take a stone and put it under him, and he sits on it. Aaron and Hur hold up his hands, one on each side. His hands are steady until sunset. Leaders need support. Intercession is exhausting.

verses.text: Joshua defeated Amalek and his people with the edge of the sword.
verses.explainer: Joshua overwhelms Amalek and his people with the edge of the sword.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Write this for a memorial in a book, and rehearse it in the ears of Joshua: that I will utterly blot out the memory of Amalek from under the sky.”
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: Write this as a memorial in a book and recite it in Joshua's ears: I will utterly blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven. The first command to write something down in the Bible.

verses.text: Moses built an altar, and called its name Yahweh our Banner.
verses.explainer: Moses builds an altar and calls it 'The LORD Is My Banner' (Yahweh-Nissi). God is the banner under which Israel fights.

verses.text: He said, “Yah has sworn: ‘Yahweh will have war with Amalek from generation to generation.’”
verses.explainer: He says: A hand upon the throne of the LORD! The LORD will have war with Amalek from generation to generation. The enmity with Amalek becomes a permanent feature of Israelite history.

explainer.summary: Jethro, Moses' father-in-law, hears about the Exodus and brings Moses' wife Zipporah and two sons to meet him in the wilderness. Moses tells Jethro everything God has done. Jethro worships and declares: 'Now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods.' The next day Jethro watches Moses judge the people from morning to evening and says: 'What you are doing is not good. You will wear yourself out.' He advises Moses to appoint capable leaders over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens, handling minor cases themselves. Moses listens.
themes: A Gentile's worship of the LORD
explainer.contextInBook: Jethro provides the organizational structure that makes governing Israel possible. Remarkably, this crucial advice comes from a non-Israelite priest. The chapter demonstrates that God's wisdom is not limited to Israel.

verses.text: Now Jethro, the priest of Midian, Moses’ father-in-law, heard of all that God had done for Moses, and for Israel his people, how that Yahweh had brought Israel out of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Jethro, the priest of Midian, Moses' father-in-law, hears all that God has done for Moses and Israel -- how the LORD brought Israel out of Egypt.

verses.text: Jethro, Moses’ father-in-law, received Zipporah, Moses’ wife, after he had sent her away,
verses.explainer: Jethro takes Zipporah, Moses' wife, after Moses had sent her home.

verses.text: and her two sons. The name of one son was Gershom, for Moses said, “I have lived as a foreigner in a foreign land”.
verses.explainer: He brings her two sons. The first is named Gershom ('I have been a sojourner in a foreign land').

verses.text: The name of the other was Eliezer, for he said, “My father’s God was my help and delivered me from Pharaoh’s sword.”
verses.explainer: The second is Eliezer ('The God of my father was my help, and delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh').

verses.text: Jethro, Moses’ father-in-law, came with his sons and his wife to Moses into the wilderness where he was encamped, at the Mountain of God.
verses.explainer: Jethro comes with Moses' sons and wife to the wilderness where Moses is camped at the mountain of God.

verses.text: He said to Moses, “I, your father-in-law Jethro, have come to you with your wife, and her two sons with her.”
verses.explainer: He sends word to Moses: I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you, with your wife and her two sons.

verses.text: Moses went out to meet his father-in-law, and bowed and kissed him. They asked each other of their welfare, and they came into the tent.
verses.explainer: Moses goes out to meet his father-in-law, bows down, and kisses him. They ask each other about their welfare and go into the tent.

verses.text: Moses told his father-in-law all that Yahweh had done to Pharaoh and to the Egyptians for Israel’s sake, all the hardships that had come on them on the way, and how Yahweh delivered them.
verses.explainer: Moses tells Jethro all that the LORD has done to Pharaoh and Egypt for Israel's sake, all the hardship they encountered on the way, and how the LORD delivered them.

verses.text: Jethro rejoiced for all the goodness which Yahweh had done to Israel, in that he had delivered them out of the hand of the Egyptians.
verses.explainer: Jethro rejoices over all the good the LORD has done for Israel in delivering them from the Egyptians.

verses.text: Jethro said, “Blessed be Yahweh, who has delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians, and out of the hand of Pharaoh; who has delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians.
verses.explainer: Jethro says: Blessed be the LORD, who has delivered you from the Egyptians and from Pharaoh. He has delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians.

verses.text: Now I know that Yahweh is greater than all gods because of the thing in which they dealt arrogantly against them.”
verses.explainer: Now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods, because in this affair they dealt arrogantly with the people. A Gentile priest confesses that YHWH is supreme.

thenVsNow.then: A Midianite priest declaring YHWH's supremacy over all gods was a remarkable statement in the polytheistic ancient Near East. Jethro does not deny other gods exist -- he declares YHWH greater than all of them.
thenVsNow.now: Jethro's confession represents the journey from general religious awareness to specific faith in the God who liberates. His story encourages the idea that people outside the covenant community can recognize and worship the true God.

verses.text: Jethro, Moses’ father-in-law, took a burnt offering and sacrifices for God. Aaron came with all the elders of Israel, to eat bread with Moses’ father-in-law before God.
verses.explainer: Jethro brings a burnt offering and sacrifices to God. Aaron and all the elders of Israel come to eat bread with him before God. A Gentile leads Israelite elders in worship.

verses.text: On the next day, Moses sat to judge the people, and the people stood around Moses from the morning to the evening.
verses.explainer: The next day Moses sits to judge the people. They stand around him from morning to evening.

verses.text: When Moses’ father-in-law saw all that he did to the people, he said, “What is this thing that you do for the people? Why do you sit alone, and all the people stand around you from morning to evening?”
verses.explainer: Jethro sees all that Moses is doing and asks: What is this you are doing for the people? Why do you sit alone while all the people stand around you from morning to evening?

verses.text: Moses said to his father-in-law, “Because the people come to me to inquire of God.
verses.explainer: Moses answers: Because the people come to me to inquire of God.

verses.text: When they have a matter, they come to me, and I judge between a man and his neighbor, and I make them know the statutes of God, and his laws.”
verses.explainer: When they have a dispute, they come to me. I decide between one person and another, and I make them know the statutes and laws of God.

verses.text: Moses’ father-in-law said to him, “The thing that you do is not good.
verses.explainer: Jethro says: What you are doing is not good. Blunt, practical wisdom.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Jethro's organizational advice", 'explanation': "Jethro's recommended structure -- rulers of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens -- mirrors military organization in the ancient Near East. It creates a judicial hierarchy where most disputes are resolved at the lowest possible level, with only the hardest cases escalating to Moses. This is arguably the first recorded organizational management advice in history."}

verses.text: You will surely wear away, both you, and this people that is with you; for the thing is too heavy for you. You are not able to perform it yourself alone.
verses.explainer: You and the people with you will certainly wear yourselves out. The thing is too heavy for you. You are not able to do it alone.

verses.text: Listen now to my voice. I will give you counsel, and God be with you. You represent the people before God, and bring the causes to God.
verses.explainer: Now listen to me; I will give you advice, and God be with you! You shall represent the people before God and bring their cases to God.

verses.text: You shall teach them the statutes and the laws, and shall show them the way in which they must walk, and the work that they must do.
verses.explainer: Teach them the statutes and laws. Show them the way they must walk and what they must do.

verses.text: Moreover you shall provide out of all the people able men which fear God: men of truth, hating unjust gain; and place such over them, to be rulers of thousands, rulers of hundreds, rulers of fifties, and rulers of tens.
verses.explainer: Look for able men from all the people -- men who fear God, who are trustworthy, who hate a bribe. Set them as chiefs of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.

verses.text: Let them judge the people at all times. It shall be that every great matter they shall bring to you, but every small matter they shall judge themselves. So shall it be easier for you, and they shall share the load with you.
verses.explainer: Let them judge the people at all times. Every major matter they shall bring to you, but every small matter they shall decide themselves. It will be easier for you, and they will share the burden.

verses.text: If you will do this thing, and God commands you so, then you will be able to endure, and all these people also will go to their place in peace.”
verses.explainer: If you do this, God will direct you, you will be able to endure, and all these people will go to their place in peace.

verses.text: So Moses listened to the voice of his father-in-law, and did all that he had said.
verses.explainer: Moses listens to his father-in-law and does everything he says. Wisdom accepted regardless of its source.

verses.text: Moses chose able men out of all Israel, and made them heads over the people, rulers of thousands, rulers of hundreds, rulers of fifties, and rulers of tens.
verses.explainer: Moses chooses able men from all Israel and makes them heads over the people -- chiefs of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.

verses.text: They judged the people at all times. They brought the hard causes to Moses, but every small matter they judged themselves.
verses.explainer: They judge the people at all times. Hard cases they bring to Moses. Every small matter they decide themselves.

verses.text: Moses let his father-in-law depart, and he went his way into his own land.
verses.explainer: Moses lets his father-in-law go. Jethro returns to his own country. His work is done. He came, he worshipped, he advised, and he departed.

explainer.summary: Israel arrives at Mount Sinai, and God proposes a covenant: 'You shall be to me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation.' Moses goes up and down the mountain as mediator. God tells Moses to consecrate the people for two days because on the third day the LORD will come down on Mount Sinai in the sight of all. When the third day arrives, there is thunder, lightning, thick cloud, and the blast of a trumpet so loud the people tremble. The mountain is wrapped in smoke because the LORD descends in fire. God calls Moses to the top.
themes: The covenant proposal: kingdom of priests
The theophany at Sinai -- God's visible descent
The awesome terror of God's presence
explainer.contextInBook: Sinai is the destination promised at the burning bush (3:12). Israel has been delivered from Egypt for this moment -- to meet God and receive their identity as his covenant people. Everything from chapter 20 onward flows from this encounter.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A kingdom where every citizen serves as a priest
wordStudies.meaning: A kingdom where every citizen serves as a priest. Not a kingdom ruled by priests, but a nation where every member has direct access to God and mediates his presence to the world. Israel's calling is to be to the nations what a priest is to a congregation.

verses.text: In the third month after the children of Israel had gone out of the land of Egypt, on that same day they came into the wilderness of Sinai.
verses.explainer: On the third new moon after the Israelites have gone out of Egypt, on that day they come into the wilderness of Sinai.

verses.text: When they had departed from Rephidim, and had come to the wilderness of Sinai, they encamped in the wilderness; and there Israel encamped before the mountain.
verses.explainer: They set out from Rephidim, come into the wilderness of Sinai, and camp before the mountain.

verses.text: Moses went up to God, and Yahweh called to him out of the mountain, saying, “This is what you shall tell the house of Jacob, and tell the children of Israel:
verses.explainer: Moses goes up to God, and the LORD calls to him from the mountain: 'Thus shall you say to the house of Jacob, and tell the Israelites.'

verses.text: ‘You have seen what I did to the Egyptians, and how I bore you on eagles’ wings, and brought you to myself.
verses.explainer: You yourselves have seen what I did to the Egyptians, and how I bore you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself. God's first words at Sinai are not commands but a reminder of grace.

verses.text: Now therefore, if you will indeed obey my voice, and keep my covenant, then you shall be my own possession from among all peoples; for all the earth is mine;
verses.explainer: Now therefore, if you will indeed obey my voice and keep my covenant, you shall be my treasured possession among all peoples, for all the earth is mine.

verses.text: and you shall be to me a kingdom of priests, and a holy nation.’ These are the words which you shall speak to the children of Israel.”
verses.explainer: You shall be to me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation. These are the words you shall speak to the Israelites. This is Israel's vocation: every Israelite is a priest to the nations, mediating God's presence to the world.

verses.text: Moses came and called for the elders of the people, and set before them all these words which Yahweh commanded him.
verses.explainer: Moses comes and calls the elders of the people and sets before them all these words the LORD has commanded.

verses.text:
All the people answered together, and said, “All that Yahweh has spoken we will do.”
Moses reported the words of the people to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: All the people answer together: 'All that the LORD has spoken we will do.' Moses reports the words of the people to the LORD. The covenant is accepted before the terms are fully revealed.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Behold, I come to you in a thick cloud, that the people may hear when I speak with you, and may also believe you forever.” Moses told the words of the people to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: I am coming to you in a thick cloud, so the people may hear when I speak with you and may also believe you forever. Direct audible divine speech.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Go to the people, and sanctify them today and tomorrow, and let them wash their garments,
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: Go to the people and consecrate them today and tomorrow. Let them wash their garments.

verses.text: and be ready against the third day; for on the third day Yahweh will come down in the sight of all the people on Mount Sinai.
verses.explainer: Be ready for the third day. On the third day the LORD will come down on Mount Sinai in the sight of all the people.

verses.text: You shall set bounds to the people all around, saying, ‘Be careful that you don’t go up onto the mountain, or touch its border. Whoever touches the mountain shall be surely put to death.
verses.explainer: Set limits around the mountain. Anyone who touches the mountain shall be put to death.

verses.text: No hand shall touch him, but he shall surely be stoned or shot through; whether it is animal or man, he shall not live.’ When the trumpet sounds long, they shall come up to the mountain.”
verses.explainer: No hand shall touch him; he shall be stoned or shot. Whether beast or man, he shall not live. When the trumpet sounds a long blast, they shall come up to the mountain. Holiness requires boundaries.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Boundaries around the mountain', 'explanation': "The concept of sacred space in the ancient world required physical boundaries between the holy and the common. The mountain becomes the first 'holy of holies' -- God's dwelling space that no unauthorized person may enter. This architecture of holiness will later be built into the Tabernacle and Temple."}

verses.text: Moses went down from the mountain to the people, and sanctified the people; and they washed their clothes.
verses.explainer: Moses goes down from the mountain to the people. He consecrates them, and they wash their garments.

verses.text: He said to the people, “Be ready by the third day. Don’t have sexual relations with a woman.”
verses.explainer: He says to the people: Be ready for the third day. Preparation for meeting God.

verses.text: On the third day, when it was morning, there were thunders and lightnings, and a thick cloud on the mountain, and the sound of an exceedingly loud trumpet; and all the people who were in the camp trembled.
verses.explainer: On the morning of the third day there are thunders and lightnings and a thick cloud on the mountain and a very loud trumpet blast, so that all the people in the camp tremble. The theophany begins.

verses.text: Moses led the people out of the camp to meet God; and they stood at the lower part of the mountain.
verses.explainer: Moses brings the people out of the camp to meet God, and they take their stand at the foot of the mountain.

verses.text: All of Mount Sinai smoked, because Yahweh descended on it in fire; and its smoke ascended like the smoke of a furnace, and the whole mountain quaked greatly.
verses.explainer: Mount Sinai is wrapped in smoke because the LORD descends on it in fire. The smoke goes up like the smoke of a kiln, and the whole mountain trembles greatly.

thenVsNow.then: The volcanic imagery -- fire, smoke, trembling earth -- conveyed to the Israelites that this was no human event. The God who had defeated Egypt now manifests his presence with the full force of nature.
thenVsNow.now: Modern readers sometimes try to explain the Sinai event naturalistically (volcano, thunderstorm). The text is not interested in the mechanism but in the meaning: the Creator of the universe showed up, and it was terrifying.

verses.text: When the sound of the trumpet grew louder and louder, Moses spoke, and God answered him by a voice.
verses.explainer: The sound of the trumpet grows louder and louder. Moses speaks, and God answers him in thunder.

verses.text: Yahweh came down on Mount Sinai, to the top of the mountain. Yahweh called Moses to the top of the mountain, and Moses went up.
verses.explainer: The LORD comes down on Mount Sinai, to the top of the mountain. The LORD calls Moses to the top, and Moses goes up.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Go down, warn the people, lest they break through to Yahweh to gaze, and many of them perish.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: Go down and warn the people not to break through to the LORD to look, lest many of them perish. Even curiosity about the divine can be fatal without proper consecration.

verses.text: Let the priests also, who come near to Yahweh, sanctify themselves, lest Yahweh break out on them.”
verses.explainer: Even the priests who come near the LORD must consecrate themselves, lest the LORD break out against them.

verses.text: Moses said to Yahweh, “The people can’t come up to Mount Sinai, for you warned us, saying, ‘Set bounds around the mountain, and sanctify it.’”
verses.explainer: Moses says to the LORD: The people cannot come up to Mount Sinai, for you yourself warned us, 'Set limits around the mountain and consecrate it.'

verses.text: Yahweh said to him, “Go down! You shall bring Aaron up with you, but don’t let the priests and the people break through to come up to Yahweh, lest he break out against them.”
verses.explainer: The LORD says: Go down, and come up bringing Aaron with you. But do not let the priests and the people break through to come up to the LORD, lest he break out against them.

verses.text: So Moses went down to the people, and told them.
verses.explainer: So Moses goes down to the people and tells them. The stage is set for the greatest speech in history: the Ten Commandments.

explainer.summary: God speaks the Ten Commandments directly to the people -- the only time in the Bible God addresses an entire nation audibly. The commandments begin not with a demand but with an identity statement: 'I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of Egypt.' The first four commandments govern the relationship with God (no other gods, no idols, no misusing God's name, keep the Sabbath). The last six govern relationships with people (honor parents, no murder, no adultery, no stealing, no false witness, no coveting). The people are terrified by God's voice and ask Moses to speak to them instead. Moses reassures them: God has come to test you, so that the fear of him may keep you from sinning.
themes: The covenant stipulations: how the redeemed should live
The fear of God as the beginning of wisdom
explainer.contextInBook: The Ten Commandments are the heart of the Sinai covenant. They are not arbitrary rules but the constitution of a freed people. Every law that follows in Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy is an application of these ten principles.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To desire, to take pleasure in, to set one's heart
wordStudies.meaning: To desire, to take pleasure in, to set one's heart on something that belongs to another. Coveting is the only commandment that addresses internal disposition rather than external action. It recognizes that all outward sins begin with inward desire. You cannot murder, steal, or commit adultery without first coveting.

verses.text: God spoke all these words, saying,
verses.explainer: God speaks all these words. Not Moses -- God. The Ten Commandments are the only words in the Bible spoken directly by God to an assembled nation.

verses.text: “I am Yahweh your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage.
verses.explainer: I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery. Before any command comes identity. God does not say 'I am the creator of the universe.' He says 'I am the one who freed you.' The law flows from liberation.

verses.text: “You shall have no other gods before me.
verses.explainer: You shall have no other gods before me. The first commandment: exclusive allegiance. In a world of many gods, Israel is to worship only YHWH.

thenVsNow.then: Ancient Near Eastern religions were polytheistic -- worshipping multiple gods was normal and expected. A command to worship only one god was radical, even bizarre, to Israel's neighbors.
thenVsNow.now: The 'gods' of the modern world are rarely statues. They are money, success, power, pleasure, nationalism -- anything that commands ultimate allegiance. The first commandment asks: what do you organize your life around?

verses.text: “You shall not make for yourselves an idol, nor any image of anything that is in the heavens above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth:
verses.explainer: You shall not make for yourself a carved image, or any likeness of anything in heaven, earth, or under the earth. The second commandment: no representations of God. God cannot be contained or controlled through images.

verses.text: you shall not bow yourself down to them, nor serve them, for I, Yahweh your God, am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children, on the third and on the fourth generation of those who hate me,
verses.explainer: You shall not bow down to them or serve them, for I the LORD your God am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me. Divine jealousy is not petty -- it is the fierce love of a God who will not share his people with counterfeits.

verses.text: and showing loving kindness to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments.
verses.explainer: But showing steadfast love to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments. The ratio is significant: judgment to the third and fourth generation, but love to thousands. Grace overwhelms judgment by a factor of hundreds.

verses.text: “You shall not take the name of Yahweh your God in vain, for Yahweh will not hold him guiltless who takes his name in vain.
verses.explainer: You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain, for the LORD will not hold him guiltless who takes his name in vain. The third commandment is not primarily about profanity. It is about using God's name -- his character, his authority -- to justify what he has not authorized.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Taking God's name in vain", 'explanation': "In the ancient world, invoking a deity's name meant claiming that deity's endorsement. To take God's name 'in vain' (Hebrew: la-shav, 'for emptiness/falseness') meant swearing false oaths in God's name, claiming divine authority for human agendas, or using God's name for magical manipulation. It is about misrepresentation more than vocabulary."}

verses.text: “Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy.
verses.explainer: Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy. The fourth commandment: time belongs to God. One day in seven is set apart.

verses.text: You shall labor six days, and do all your work,
verses.explainer: Six days you shall labor and do all your work.

verses.text: but the seventh day is a Sabbath to Yahweh your God. You shall not do any work in it, you, nor your son, nor your daughter, your male servant, nor your female servant, nor your livestock, nor your stranger who is within your gates;
verses.explainer: But the seventh day is a Sabbath to the LORD your God. On it you shall not do any work -- you, your son, your daughter, your male servant, your female servant, your livestock, or the sojourner within your gates. Everyone rests -- even slaves, even animals, even immigrants. The Sabbath is radically egalitarian.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, slaves never rested. The Sabbath commandment is the first labor law in history -- it mandates rest for every person regardless of social status, including foreigners and animals.
thenVsNow.now: In a culture that glorifies productivity, the Sabbath is countercultural. It insists that human worth is not defined by output. Rest is not laziness -- it is an act of trust that the world will continue without our constant effort.

verses.text: for in six days Yahweh made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that is in them, and rested the seventh day; therefore Yahweh blessed the Sabbath day, and made it holy.
verses.explainer: For in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that is in them, and rested on the seventh day. Therefore the LORD blessed the Sabbath day and made it holy. The Sabbath is embedded in creation's rhythm.

verses.text: “Honor your father and your mother, that your days may be long in the land which Yahweh your God gives you.
verses.explainer: Honor your father and your mother, that your days may be long in the land the LORD your God is giving you. The fifth commandment bridges the God-ward and human-ward sections. Parents are the conduit through which faith, identity, and covenant knowledge pass.

verses.text: “You shall not murder.
verses.explainer: You shall not murder. The sixth commandment protects human life. The Hebrew ratsach refers specifically to unlawful killing (murder, manslaughter) rather than all forms of killing. The distinction between murder and other forms of killing is developed in the case law that follows.

verses.text: “You shall not commit adultery.
verses.explainer: You shall not commit adultery. The seventh commandment protects the marriage covenant. Adultery in the ancient world was primarily about violation of covenant trust and destruction of family structure.

verses.text: “You shall not steal.
verses.explainer: You shall not steal. The eighth commandment protects property. Some scholars argue the original context may refer specifically to kidnapping -- stealing persons -- which was a capital offense.

verses.text: “You shall not give false testimony against your neighbor.
verses.explainer: You shall not bear false witness against your neighbor. The ninth commandment protects justice. In a world without forensic evidence, testimony was the foundation of the legal system. False witness could mean death for the innocent.

verses.text: “You shall not covet your neighbor’s house. You shall not covet your neighbor’s wife, nor his male servant, nor his female servant, nor his ox, nor his donkey, nor anything that is your neighbor’s.”
verses.explainer: You shall not covet your neighbor's house. You shall not covet your neighbor's wife, or his male servant, or his female servant, or his ox, or his donkey, or anything that belongs to your neighbor. The tenth commandment addresses the interior life -- desire itself. Every external sin begins here.

verses.text: All the people perceived the thunderings, the lightnings, the sound of the trumpet, and the mountain smoking. When the people saw it, they trembled, and stayed at a distance.
verses.explainer: All the people see the thunder and the flashes of lightning, the sound of the trumpet, and the mountain smoking. The people are afraid and tremble and stand far off. The direct voice of God is too much for them.

verses.text: They said to Moses, “Speak with us yourself, and we will listen; but don’t let God speak with us, lest we die.”
verses.explainer: They say to Moses: 'You speak to us, and we will listen; but do not let God speak to us, lest we die.' The people request a mediator. They cannot bear unmediated divine presence.

verses.text: Moses said to the people, “Don’t be afraid, for God has come to test you, and that his fear may be before you, that you won’t sin.”
verses.explainer: Moses says to the people: Do not fear. God has come to test you, that the fear of him may be before you, so that you may not sin. A paradox: do not be afraid, but fear God. Healthy reverence prevents destructive sin.

verses.text: The people stayed at a distance, and Moses came near to the thick darkness where God was.
verses.explainer: The people stand far off while Moses draws near to the thick darkness where God is. Moses enters what the people cannot. He goes into the darkness toward the light.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “This is what you shall tell the children of Israel: ‘You yourselves have seen that I have talked with you from heaven.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: Say to the Israelites, 'You have seen for yourselves that I have talked with you from heaven.'

verses.text: You shall most certainly not make gods of silver or gods of gold for yourselves to be alongside me.
verses.explainer: You shall not make gods of silver or gods of gold to be with me. Do not make such gods for yourselves. The first application of the commandments: no metallic representations of God.

verses.text: You shall make an altar of earth for me, and shall sacrifice on it your burnt offerings and your peace offerings, your sheep and your cattle. In every place where I record my name I will come to you and I will bless you.
verses.explainer: An altar of earth you shall make for me and sacrifice on it your burnt offerings and peace offerings, your sheep and oxen. In every place where I cause my name to be remembered I will come to you and bless you. Worship should be simple.

verses.text: If you make me an altar of stone, you shall not build it of cut stones; for if you lift up your tool on it, you have polluted it.
verses.explainer: If you make an altar of stone, do not build it of hewn stones, for if you use a tool on it you profane it. Human craftsmanship must not embellish God's altar. The altar's beauty is its simplicity.

verses.text: You shall not go up by steps to my altar, that your nakedness may not be exposed to it.’
verses.explainer: You shall not go up by steps to my altar, so that your nakedness not be exposed on it. Practical modesty in worship. Dignity before God.

explainer.summary: The case law begins. After the broad principles of the Ten Commandments, God provides specific applications. Chapter 21 addresses slavery, personal injury, and property damage. Hebrew slaves must be freed after six years. If a master injures a slave, the slave goes free. The famous 'eye for eye' principle (lex talionis) limits retribution: the punishment must fit the crime, no more. An owner whose ox kills someone bears responsibility. These laws were revolutionary in the ancient world for their protection of the vulnerable.
themes: The value of human life over property
explainer.contextInBook: Chapters 21-23 are called the 'Book of the Covenant' or 'Covenant Code' -- the oldest law collection in the Bible. These laws apply the Ten Commandments to daily life in an agricultural society.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The lex talionis (law of retaliation) is not a man
wordStudies.meaning: The lex talionis (law of retaliation) is not a mandate for violence but a limit on it. In a world where a slight against a powerful person could result in death for the offender's entire family, 'an eye for an eye' said: the maximum punishment is equivalent harm. No more. It is a law of proportional justice, not vengeance.

verses.text: “Now these are the ordinances which you shall set before them.
verses.explainer: These are the rules you shall set before them. The specific case law that applies the Ten Commandments.

verses.text: “If you buy a Hebrew servant, he shall serve six years and in the seventh he shall go out free without paying anything.
verses.explainer: When you buy a Hebrew slave, he shall serve six years, and in the seventh year he shall go free, for nothing. Slavery of fellow Hebrews is limited to six years. The Sabbath principle applies even to bondage.

thenVsNow.then: Hebrew servitude was typically debt slavery -- a person worked off what they owed. Six years was the maximum term. This was radically different from the chattel slavery of Egypt (or later American slavery), which was permanent, hereditary, and based on race.
thenVsNow.now: These laws represent the beginning of the abolition of slavery, not its endorsement. They imposed limits, protections, and an expiration date on a universal ancient institution. The trajectory points toward full liberation.

verses.text: If he comes in by himself, he shall go out by himself. If he is married, then his wife shall go out with him.
verses.explainer: If he comes in single, he goes out single. If he comes in married, his wife goes out with him.

verses.text: If his master gives him a wife and she bears him sons or daughters, the wife and her children shall be her master’s, and he shall go out by himself.
verses.explainer: If his master gives him a wife and she bears children, the wife and children belong to the master. He goes out alone. A difficult provision reflecting the economic realities of debt servitude.

verses.text: But if the servant shall plainly say, ‘I love my master, my wife, and my children. I will not go out free;’
verses.explainer: But if the slave plainly says, 'I love my master, my wife, and my children; I will not go out free' --

verses.text: then his master shall bring him to God, and shall bring him to the door or to the doorpost, and his master shall bore his ear through with an awl, and he shall serve him for ever.
verses.explainer: Then his master shall bring him to God and bring him to the door. He shall bore his ear with an awl, and he shall serve him forever. Voluntary permanent service, marked by a pierced ear.

verses.text: “If a man sells his daughter to be a female servant, she shall not go out as the male servants do.
verses.explainer: When a man sells his daughter as a servant, she shall not go out as the male servants do. Female servants had different protections because of their vulnerability.

verses.text: If she doesn’t please her master, who has married her to himself, then he shall let her be redeemed. He shall have no right to sell her to a foreign people, since he has dealt deceitfully with her.
verses.explainer: If she does not please her master who designated her for himself, he shall let her be redeemed. He shall have no right to sell her to a foreign people, since he has broken faith with her. Protection against trafficking.

verses.text: If he marries her to his son, he shall deal with her as a daughter.
verses.explainer: If he designates her for his son, he shall deal with her as with a daughter. She gains family status.

verses.text: If he takes another wife to himself, he shall not diminish her food, her clothing, and her marital rights.
verses.explainer: If he takes another wife to himself, he shall not diminish her food, clothing, or marital rights.

verses.text: If he doesn’t do these three things for her, she may go free without paying any money.
verses.explainer: If he does not do these three things for her, she shall go free, for nothing, without payment of money. Her freedom is the remedy for neglect.

verses.text: “One who strikes a man so that he dies shall surely be put to death,
verses.explainer: Whoever strikes a man so that he dies shall be put to death. The sanctity of human life demands ultimate accountability.

verses.text: but not if it is unintentional, but God allows it to happen: then I will appoint you a place where he shall flee.
verses.explainer: But if he did not lie in wait for him, but God let him fall into his hand, then I will appoint a place to which he may flee. The distinction between murder and manslaughter, with cities of refuge for accidental killing.

verses.text: If a man schemes and comes presumptuously on his neighbor to kill him, you shall take him from my altar, that he may die.
verses.explainer: But if a man willfully attacks another to kill him treacherously, you shall take him from my altar so that he may die. Even the sanctuary cannot protect a murderer.

verses.text: “Anyone who attacks his father or his mother shall be surely put to death.
verses.explainer: Whoever strikes his father or mother shall be put to death.

verses.text: “Anyone who kidnaps someone and sells him, or if he is found in his hand, he shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: Whoever steals a man and sells him, and anyone found in possession of him, shall be put to death. Kidnapping for slavery is a capital offense.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Man-stealing', 'explanation': "This law explicitly criminalizes kidnapping people for sale into slavery. Some scholars argue the eighth commandment ('you shall not steal') originally referred to this -- stealing persons, not property. The death penalty for kidnapping-for-slavery stands in stark contrast to later historical systems that made such kidnapping an industry."}

verses.text: “Anyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: Whoever curses his father or mother shall be put to death.

verses.text: “If men quarrel and one strikes the other with a stone, or with his fist, and he doesn’t die, but is confined to bed;
verses.explainer: When men quarrel and one strikes the other with a stone or fist and he does not die but takes to his bed --

verses.text: if he rises again and walks around with his staff, then he who struck him shall be cleared: only he shall pay for the loss of his time, and shall provide for his healing until he is thoroughly healed.
verses.explainer: If he gets up and walks outdoors with his staff, the one who struck him shall be clear; only he shall pay for the loss of his time and have him thoroughly healed. Early personal injury law: compensation for lost wages and medical care.

verses.text: “If a man strikes his servant or his maid with a rod, and he dies under his hand, he shall surely be punished.
verses.explainer: When a man strikes his slave, male or female, with a rod and the slave dies under his hand, he shall be avenged. A master who kills a slave is punished. This was unprecedented in the ancient world.

verses.text: Notwithstanding, if he gets up after a day or two, he shall not be punished, for he is his property.
verses.explainer: But if the slave survives a day or two, the master is not punished, for the slave is his property. A difficult provision, but still more protective than any contemporary legal system.

verses.text: “If men fight and hurt a pregnant woman so that she gives birth prematurely, and yet no harm follows, he shall be surely fined as much as the woman’s husband demands and the judges allow.
verses.explainer: When men fight and hit a pregnant woman so that her children come out, but there is no serious injury, the offender shall pay a fine as the woman's husband demands and the judges determine.

verses.text: But if any harm follows, then you must take life for life,
verses.explainer: But if there is serious injury, you shall pay life for life.

verses.text: eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot,
verses.explainer: Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot. The lex talionis limits revenge. You may take only what was taken from you -- no more.

verses.text: burning for burning, wound for wound, and bruise for bruise.
verses.explainer: Burn for burn, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.

verses.text: “If a man strikes his servant’s eye, or his maid’s eye, and destroys it, he shall let him go free for his eye’s sake.
verses.explainer: When a man strikes the eye of his slave, male or female, and destroys it, he shall let the slave go free because of his eye. Injuring a slave results in the slave's freedom. The slave's body is protected.

verses.text: If he strikes out his male servant’s tooth, or his female servant’s tooth, he shall let him go free for his tooth’s sake.
verses.explainer: If he knocks out the tooth of his slave, male or female, he shall let the slave go free because of his tooth. Even a knocked-out tooth earns a slave their freedom. The law consistently protects the powerless.

verses.text: “If a bull gores a man or a woman to death, the bull shall surely be stoned, and its meat shall not be eaten; but the owner of the bull shall not be held responsible.
verses.explainer: When an ox gores a man or woman to death, the ox shall be stoned and its flesh shall not be eaten. The owner of the ox shall not be liable.

verses.text: But if the bull had a habit of goring in the past, and it has been testified to its owner, and he has not kept it in, but it has killed a man or a woman, the bull shall be stoned, and its owner shall also be put to death.
verses.explainer: But if the ox has been accustomed to gore in the past, and its owner has been warned but has not kept it in, and it kills a man or woman, the ox shall be stoned, and its owner also shall be put to death. Known danger creates liability.

verses.text: If a ransom is laid on him, then he shall give for the redemption of his life whatever is laid on him.
verses.explainer: If a ransom is imposed on him, he shall pay whatever is imposed to redeem his life.

verses.text: Whether it has gored a son or has gored a daughter, according to this judgment it shall be done to him.
verses.explainer: Whether it gores a son or a daughter, this same rule applies.

verses.text: If the bull gores a male servant or a female servant, thirty shekels of silver shall be given to their master, and the ox shall be stoned.
verses.explainer: If the ox gores a slave, the owner shall give to the master thirty shekels of silver, and the ox shall be stoned.

verses.text: “If a man opens a pit, or if a man digs a pit and doesn’t cover it, and a bull or a donkey falls into it,
verses.explainer: When a man opens a pit, or digs a pit and does not cover it, and an ox or donkey falls into it --

verses.text: the owner of the pit shall make it good. He shall give money to its owner, and the dead animal shall be his.
verses.explainer: The owner of the pit shall make restoration. He shall pay money to the owner of the animal, and the dead beast shall be his.

verses.text: “If one man’s bull injures another’s, so that it dies, then they shall sell the live bull, and divide its price; and they shall also divide the dead animal.
verses.explainer: When one man's ox butts another's ox and it dies, they shall sell the live ox and share the price. They shall also share the dead ox.

verses.text: Or if it is known that the bull was in the habit of goring in the past, and its owner has not kept it in, he shall surely pay bull for bull, and the dead animal shall be his own.
verses.explainer: But if it has been known that the ox was accustomed to gore and its owner has not kept it in, he shall repay ox for ox, and the dead beast shall be his. Negligence increases liability.

explainer.summary: The case law continues with theft, property damage, custody of goods, seduction, sorcery, and social justice. A thief must make restitution -- if he cannot, he is sold to pay the debt. If a man seduces a virgin, he must marry her or pay the bride-price. The chapter culminates in some of the Bible's most powerful social justice commands: do not mistreat a sojourner, do not afflict widows or orphans, do not charge interest to the poor, return a neighbor's cloak before sunset because it may be his only blanket.
themes: God hearing the cry of the oppressed
explainer.contextInBook: These laws reveal God's priorities: property crimes require restitution (not prison); the poor, the immigrant, and the widow receive special protection. God identifies personally with the marginalized.

verses.text: “If a man steals an ox or a sheep, and kills it, or sells it; he shall pay five oxen for an ox, and four sheep for a sheep.
verses.explainer: If a man steals an ox or a sheep and kills it or sells it, he shall repay five oxen for an ox and four sheep for a sheep. Restitution with multiplied repayment -- not imprisonment.

verses.text: If the thief is found breaking in, and is struck so that he dies, there shall be no guilt of bloodshed for him.
verses.explainer: If a thief is caught breaking in and is struck so that he dies, there shall be no bloodguilt for the homeowner.

verses.text: If the sun has risen on him, guilt of bloodshed shall be for him; he shall make restitution. If he has nothing, then he shall be sold for his theft.
verses.explainer: But if the sun has risen on him, there shall be bloodguilt. Self-defense in the dark is justified; killing a thief in daylight is not.

verses.text: If the stolen property is found in his hand alive, whether it is ox, donkey, or sheep, he shall pay double.
verses.explainer: He shall surely pay. If he has nothing, he shall be sold for his theft.

verses.text: “If a man causes a field or vineyard to be eaten, and lets his animal loose, and it grazes in another man’s field, he shall make restitution from the best of his own field, and from the best of his own vineyard.
verses.explainer: If a man causes a field or vineyard to be grazed over, or lets his beast loose and it feeds in another man's field, he shall make restitution from the best in his own field.

verses.text: “If fire breaks out, and catches in thorns so that the shocks of grain, or the standing grain, or the field are consumed; he who kindled the fire shall surely make restitution.
verses.explainer: If fire breaks out and catches in thorns so that stacked grain, standing grain, or the field is consumed, he who started the fire shall make full restitution. Negligence with fire creates liability.

verses.text: “If a man delivers to his neighbor money or stuff to keep, and it is stolen out of the man’s house; if the thief is found, he shall pay double.
verses.explainer: If a man gives his neighbor money or goods to keep safe and it is stolen from the man's house, the thief, if found, shall pay double.

verses.text: If the thief isn’t found, then the master of the house shall come near to God, to find out if he hasn’t put his hand to his neighbor’s goods.
verses.explainer: If the thief is not found, the owner of the house shall come near to God to show whether he has put his hand on his neighbor's property. The oath before God serves as a legal proceeding.

verses.text: For every matter of trespass, whether it is for ox, for donkey, for sheep, for clothing, or for any kind of lost thing, about which one says, ‘This is mine,’ the cause of both parties shall come before God. He whom God condemns shall pay double to his neighbor.
verses.explainer: For every breach of trust, whether over ox, donkey, sheep, clothing, or any lost thing, both parties shall come before God. The one whom God condemns shall pay double.

verses.text: “If a man delivers to his neighbor a donkey, an ox, a sheep, or any animal to keep, and it dies or is injured, or driven away, no man seeing it;
verses.explainer: If a man gives his neighbor a donkey, ox, sheep, or any beast to keep, and it dies or is injured or driven away without anyone seeing --

verses.text: the oath of Yahweh shall be between them both, whether he hasn’t put his hand to his neighbor’s goods; and its owner shall accept it, and he shall not make restitution.
verses.explainer: An oath by the LORD shall be between them that he has not put his hand on his neighbor's property. The owner shall accept it, and he shall not make restitution.

verses.text: But if it is stolen from him, he shall make restitution to its owner.
verses.explainer: But if it is stolen from him, he shall make restitution to its owner.

verses.text: If it is torn in pieces, let him bring it for evidence. He shall not make good that which was torn.
verses.explainer: If it is torn by a beast, let him bring it as evidence. He shall not make restitution for what has been torn.

verses.text: “If a man borrows anything of his neighbor’s, and it is injured, or dies, its owner not being with it, he shall surely make restitution.
verses.explainer: If a man borrows anything from his neighbor and it is injured or dies while the owner is not present, he shall make full restitution.

verses.text: If its owner is with it, he shall not make it good. If it is a leased thing, it came for its lease.
verses.explainer: If the owner was present, he shall not make restitution. If it was hired, it came for its hiring fee.

verses.text: “If a man entices a virgin who isn’t pledged to be married, and lies with her, he shall surely pay a dowry for her to be his wife.
verses.explainer: If a man seduces a virgin who is not betrothed and lies with her, he shall give the bride-price for her and make her his wife.

verses.text: If her father utterly refuses to give her to him, he shall pay money according to the dowry of virgins.
verses.explainer: If her father utterly refuses to give her to him, he shall pay money equal to the bride-price for virgins. The woman and her father retain the decision.

verses.text: “You shall not allow a sorceress to live.
verses.explainer: You shall not permit a sorceress to live.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Sorcery prohibition', 'explanation': "In the ancient Near East, sorcery was primarily about using rituals, spells, and potions to manipulate spiritual forces for personal gain or to harm others. The prohibition is not about herb-gathering or folk medicine but about the attempt to control supernatural power outside of God. Egypt's magicians practiced exactly this kind of power, and Israel is being distinguished from them."}

verses.text: “Whoever has sex with an animal shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: Whoever lies with an animal shall be put to death.

verses.text: “He who sacrifices to any god, except to Yahweh only, shall be utterly destroyed.
verses.explainer: Whoever sacrifices to any god other than the LORD alone shall be devoted to destruction.

verses.text: “You shall not wrong an alien or oppress him, for you were aliens in the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: You shall not wrong a sojourner or oppress him, for you were sojourners in Egypt. The memory of being immigrants grounds the ethic of treating immigrants well.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, foreigners had no legal rights. Israel's law is unique in extending legal protection to the non-citizen. The reason given is experiential: you know what it feels like to be a foreigner in a hostile land.
thenVsNow.now: Immigration remains one of the most contested political issues globally. The biblical ethic is clear: because you were once the outsider, you must protect outsiders. Experience of suffering should create empathy, not hardness.

verses.text: “You shall not take advantage of any widow or fatherless child.
verses.explainer: You shall not afflict any widow or fatherless child. The most vulnerable members of ancient society receive divine protection.

verses.text: If you take advantage of them at all, and they cry at all to me, I will surely hear their cry;
verses.explainer: If you do afflict them, and they cry out to me, I will surely hear their cry. God personally hears the cry of the oppressed -- the same language used for Israel's cry in Egypt.

verses.text: and my wrath will grow hot, and I will kill you with the sword; and your wives shall be widows, and your children fatherless.
verses.explainer: My wrath will burn, and I will kill you with the sword, and your wives shall become widows and your children fatherless. The punishment fits the crime: oppress the vulnerable, and your own family becomes vulnerable.

verses.text: “If you lend money to any of my people with you who is poor, you shall not be to him as a creditor. You shall not charge him interest.
verses.explainer: If you lend money to any of my people who is poor among you, you shall not be like a moneylender to him, and you shall not exact interest from him. Lending to the poor is an act of charity, not a business opportunity.

verses.text: If you take your neighbor’s garment as collateral, you shall restore it to him before the sun goes down,
verses.explainer: If you take your neighbor's cloak as a pledge, you shall return it to him before the sun goes down.

verses.text: for that is his only covering, it is his garment for his skin. What would he sleep in? It will happen, when he cries to me, that I will hear, for I am gracious.
verses.explainer: For that is his only covering. It is his cloak for his body. In what shall he sleep? And if he cries to me, I will hear, for I am compassionate. God cares about whether a poor man has a blanket tonight.

verses.text: “You shall not blaspheme God, nor curse a ruler of your people.
verses.explainer: You shall not revile God, nor curse a ruler of your people.

verses.text:
“You shall not delay to offer from your harvest and from the outflow of your presses.
“You shall give the firstborn of your sons to me.
verses.explainer: You shall not delay to offer from the fullness of your harvest and from the outflow of your presses. The firstborn of your sons you shall give to me.

verses.text: You shall do likewise with your cattle and with your sheep. Seven days it shall be with its mother, then on the eighth day you shall give it to me.
verses.explainer: You shall do the same with your oxen and sheep: seven days it shall be with its mother; on the eighth day you shall give it to me.

verses.text: “You shall be holy men to me, therefore you shall not eat any meat that is torn by animals in the field. You shall cast it to the dogs.
verses.explainer: You shall be consecrated to me. You shall not eat any flesh torn by beasts in the field. You shall throw it to the dogs. Holiness extends to diet.

explainer.summary: The Covenant Code concludes with laws about justice in court, Sabbath rest for land and people, the three annual festivals, and God's promise to send an angel before Israel to guide them into the Promised Land. The chapter contains some of the Bible's most quoted ethical commands: do not spread a false report, do not follow a crowd to do evil, do not pervert justice for the poor, help your enemy's donkey. God promises to drive out the inhabitants of Canaan gradually.
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter closes the Book of the Covenant with a vision of life in the Promised Land. The laws are not abstract -- they describe the kind of society God wants Israel to build when they arrive.

verses.text: “You shall not spread a false report. Don’t join your hand with the wicked to be a malicious witness.
verses.explainer: You shall not spread a false report. Do not join hands with a wicked man to be a malicious witness. Fake news is ancient, and it is forbidden.

verses.text: “You shall not follow a crowd to do evil. You shall not testify in court to side with a multitude to pervert justice.
verses.explainer: You shall not follow a crowd to do evil. When asked to testify, do not go along with the majority to pervert justice. Mob mentality is not an excuse.

thenVsNow.then: Ancient trials could be swayed by public pressure and group testimony. The command to resist crowd-driven injustice was a protection for fair trials.
thenVsNow.now: Social media amplifies mob mentality to a global scale. The command not to follow a crowd to do evil has never been more relevant. Justice requires individual moral courage, not collective outrage.

verses.text: You shall not favor a poor man in his cause.
verses.explainer: Nor shall you be partial to a poor man in his lawsuit. Justice is not reversed partiality. The poor deserve protection, but they do not deserve rigged verdicts.

verses.text: “If you meet your enemy’s ox or his donkey going astray, you shall surely bring it back to him again.
verses.explainer: If you meet your enemy's ox or donkey going astray, you shall bring it back to him. Love your enemy starts with helping his livestock.

verses.text: If you see the donkey of him who hates you fallen down under his burden, don’t leave him, you shall surely help him with it.
verses.explainer: If you see the donkey of one who hates you lying under its burden, you shall not leave him. You shall surely help him with it. Compassion for an enemy's animal is required.

verses.text: “You shall not deny justice to your poor people in their lawsuits.
verses.explainer: You shall not pervert the justice due to your poor in his lawsuit. The courts must not be weaponized against the vulnerable.

verses.text: “Keep far from a false charge, and don’t kill the innocent and righteous: for I will not justify the wicked.
verses.explainer: Keep far from a false charge, and do not kill the innocent and righteous, for I will not acquit the wicked.

verses.text: “You shall take no bribe, for a bribe blinds those who have sight and perverts the words of the righteous.
verses.explainer: You shall take no bribe, for a bribe blinds the officials and subverts the cause of the righteous. Corruption in justice is forbidden absolutely.
