verses.text: You shall overlay it with pure gold, its top, its sides around it, and its horns; and you shall make a gold molding around it.
verses.explainer: Overlay it with pure gold -- its top, its sides all around, and its horns. Make a molding of gold around it. All gold -- this altar stands inside the Holy Place, close to God's presence.

verses.text: You shall make two golden rings for it under its molding; on its two ribs, on its two sides you shall make them; and they shall be for places for poles with which to bear it.
verses.explainer: Make two golden rings for it under its molding on two opposite sides, as holders for the poles to carry it.

verses.text: You shall make the poles of acacia wood, and overlay them with gold.
verses.explainer: Make the poles of acacia wood and overlay them with gold.

verses.text: You shall put it before the veil that is by the ark of the testimony, before the mercy seat that is over the testimony, where I will meet with you.
verses.explainer: Put it in front of the veil that is above the ark of the testimony, in front of the mercy seat that is above the testimony, where I will meet with you. The incense altar stands as close to God's presence as a priest can get without passing behind the veil.

verses.text: Aaron shall burn incense of sweet spices on it every morning. When he tends the lamps, he shall burn it.
verses.explainer: Aaron shall burn fragrant incense on it. Every morning when he tends the lamps, he shall burn it.

verses.text: When Aaron lights the lamps at evening, he shall burn it, a perpetual incense before Yahweh throughout your generations.
verses.explainer: When Aaron sets up the lamps at twilight, he shall burn it. A regular incense offering before the LORD throughout your generations.

verses.text: You shall offer no strange incense on it, nor burnt offering, nor meal offering; and you shall pour no drink offering on it.
verses.explainer: You shall not offer unauthorized incense on it, or a burnt offering, or a grain offering, and you shall not pour a drink offering on it. Only the prescribed incense -- nothing unauthorized. Worship according to God's design, not human improvisation.

verses.text: Aaron shall make atonement on its horns once in the year; with the blood of the sin offering of atonement once in the year he shall make atonement for it throughout your generations. It is most holy to Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: Once a year Aaron shall make atonement on its horns with the blood of the sin offering of atonement. Once a year throughout your generations. It is most holy to the LORD. The Day of Atonement connects even this altar to blood sacrifice.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to Moses.

verses.text: “When you take a census of the children of Israel, according to those who are counted among them, then each man shall give a ransom for his soul to Yahweh, when you count them; that there be no plague among them when you count them.
verses.explainer: When you take a census of the Israelites to count them, each one shall give a ransom for his life to the LORD when you number them, so that no plague come among them. Being counted requires giving -- each life has a cost.

verses.text: They shall give this, everyone who passes over to those who are counted, half a shekel after the shekel of the sanctuary (the shekel is twenty gerahs); half a shekel for an offering to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Each one who is numbered shall give half a shekel according to the shekel of the sanctuary (twenty gerahs is a shekel). This half shekel is a contribution to the LORD.

verses.text: Everyone who passes over to those who are counted, from twenty years old and upward, shall give the offering to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Everyone who is numbered, from twenty years old and upward, shall give the LORD's contribution.

verses.text: The rich shall not give more, and the poor shall not give less, than the half shekel, when they give the offering of Yahweh, to make atonement for your souls.
verses.explainer: The rich shall not give more, and the poor shall not give less than half a shekel when giving the contribution to the LORD to make atonement for your lives. Before God, all lives are of equal value.

thenVsNow.then: In a society with extreme wealth disparities, the half-shekel requirement was radically egalitarian. The richest landowner and the poorest laborer paid exactly the same amount. Before God, every life has the same value.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that every person stands equally before God remains foundational to democratic ideals. The half-shekel did not fund the Tabernacle proportionally but symbolically -- every person matters equally.

verses.text: You shall take the atonement money from the children of Israel, and shall appoint it for the service of the Tent of Meeting; that it may be a memorial for the children of Israel before Yahweh, to make atonement for your souls.”
verses.explainer: Take the atonement money from the Israelites and use it for the service of the tent of meeting. It shall be a memorial for the Israelites before the LORD, making atonement for your lives.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to Moses.

verses.text: “You shall also make a basin of brass, and its base of brass, in which to wash. You shall put it between the Tent of Meeting and the altar, and you shall put water in it.
verses.explainer: Make a bronze basin, with its stand of bronze, for washing. Place it between the tent of meeting and the altar, and put water in it.

verses.text: Aaron and his sons shall wash their hands and their feet in it.
verses.explainer: Aaron and his sons shall wash their hands and their feet with water from it.

verses.text: When they go into the Tent of Meeting, they shall wash with water, that they not die; or when they come near to the altar to minister, to burn an offering made by fire to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: When they go into the tent of meeting, or when they come near the altar to minister, to burn a food offering to the LORD, they shall wash with water, so that they may not die. Cleansing before approaching God is a matter of life and death.

verses.text: So they shall wash their hands and their feet, that they not die: and it shall be a statute forever to them, even to him and to his descendants throughout their generations.”
verses.explainer: They shall wash their hands and their feet, so that they may not die. It shall be a statute forever for them, for Aaron and his offspring throughout their generations.

verses.text: Moreover Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to Moses.

verses.text: “Also take fine spices: of liquid myrrh, five hundred shekels; and of fragrant cinnamon half as much, even two hundred and fifty; and of fragrant cane, two hundred and fifty;
verses.explainer: Take the finest spices: five hundred shekels of liquid myrrh, half as much of sweet-smelling cinnamon (two hundred fifty shekels), two hundred fifty shekels of aromatic cane.

verses.text: and of cassia five hundred, after the shekel of the sanctuary; and a hin of olive oil.
verses.explainer: Five hundred shekels of cassia, according to the shekel of the sanctuary, and a hin of olive oil.

verses.text: You shall make it into a holy anointing oil, a perfume compounded after the art of the perfumer: it shall be a holy anointing oil.
verses.explainer: Make of these a sacred anointing oil, blended as by the perfumer. It shall be a holy anointing oil.

verses.text: You shall use it to anoint the Tent of Meeting, the ark of the testimony,
verses.explainer: With it you shall anoint the tent of meeting and the ark of the testimony.

verses.text: the table and all its articles, the lamp stand and its accessories, the altar of incense,
verses.explainer: The table and all its utensils, the lampstand and its utensils, the altar of incense.

verses.text: the altar of burnt offering with all its utensils, and the basin with its base.
verses.explainer: The altar of burnt offering with all its utensils, and the basin and its stand.

verses.text: You shall sanctify them, that they may be most holy. Whatever touches them shall be holy.
verses.explainer: You shall consecrate them, that they may be most holy. Whatever touches them will become holy.

verses.text: You shall anoint Aaron and his sons, and sanctify them, that they may minister to me in the priest’s office.
verses.explainer: Anoint Aaron and his sons and consecrate them, that they may serve me as priests.

verses.text: You shall speak to the children of Israel, saying, ‘This shall be a holy anointing oil to me throughout your generations.
verses.explainer: Say to the Israelites: This shall be my holy anointing oil throughout your generations.

verses.text: It shall not be poured on man’s flesh, and do not make any like it, according to its composition. It is holy. It shall be holy to you.
verses.explainer: It shall not be poured on the body of an ordinary person, and you shall make no other like it in composition. It is holy, and it shall be holy to you.

verses.text: Whoever compounds any like it, or whoever puts any of it on a stranger, he shall be cut off from his people.’”
verses.explainer: Whoever compounds any like it or puts it on an outsider shall be cut off from his people. The sacred anointing oil is unique and reserved.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Take to yourself sweet spices, gum resin, and onycha, and galbanum; sweet spices with pure frankincense: there shall be an equal weight of each;
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: Take sweet spices -- stacte, onycha, galbanum, sweet spices with pure frankincense, equal parts of each.

verses.text: and you shall make incense of it, a perfume after the art of the perfumer, seasoned with salt, pure and holy:
verses.explainer: Make an incense blended as by the perfumer, seasoned with salt, pure and holy.

verses.text: and you shall beat some of it very small, and put some of it before the testimony in the Tent of Meeting, where I will meet with you. It shall be to you most holy.
verses.explainer: Beat some of it very small and put part of it before the testimony in the tent of meeting where I shall meet with you. It shall be most holy to you.

verses.text: The incense which you shall make, according to its composition you shall not make for yourselves: it shall be to you holy for Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The incense you shall make according to its composition, you shall not make for yourselves. It shall be holy to you for the LORD.

verses.text: Whoever shall make any like that, to smell of it, he shall be cut off from his people.”
verses.explainer: Whoever makes any like it to use as perfume shall be cut off from his people. The sacred incense, like the anointing oil, is reserved for God alone. What belongs to God cannot be appropriated for personal use.

explainer.summary: God names Bezalel and Oholiab as the master craftsmen for the Tabernacle, filling them with his Spirit for artistic skill. Then God gives Moses the stone tablets and reemphasizes the Sabbath as a perpetual sign of the covenant between God and Israel. The Sabbath is placed at the climax of the Tabernacle instructions -- even building God's house does not override the command to rest.
themes: Skilled craftsmanship as a spiritual gift
The stone tablets as God's own writing
Rest as a boundary on even sacred work
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter closes the Tabernacle instructions with two crucial details: who will build it (Spirit-filled artisans) and when to stop building (the Sabbath). The transition to the golden calf crisis in chapter 32 is jarring -- from the height of divine design to the depth of human failure.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Meaning 'in the shadow of God
wordStudies.meaning: Meaning 'in the shadow of God.' Bezalel is the first person in the Bible described as filled with the Spirit of God, and the purpose is art, not prophecy or warfare. God's Spirit empowers beauty, craftsmanship, and creative skill.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to Moses.

verses.text: “Behold, I have called by name Bezalel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah:
verses.explainer: See, I have called by name Bezalel the son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah. God knows the artist by name. The craftsman is called, not merely hired.

verses.text: and I have filled him with the Spirit of God, in wisdom, and in understanding, and in knowledge, and in all kinds of workmanship,
verses.explainer: I have filled him with the Spirit of God, with ability and intelligence, with knowledge and all craftsmanship. The first person described as filled with the Spirit of God is an artist. Artistic skill is a spiritual gift.

verses.text: to devise skillful works, to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass,
verses.explainer: To devise artistic designs, to work in gold, silver, and bronze.

verses.text: and in cutting of stones for setting, and in carving of wood, to work in all kinds of workmanship.
verses.explainer: In cutting stones for setting, in carving wood, to work in every craft.

verses.text: Behold, I myself have appointed with him Oholiab, the son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan; and in the heart of all who are wise-hearted I have put wisdom, that they may make all that I have commanded you:
verses.explainer: And behold, I have appointed with him Oholiab the son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan. I have given ability to all able-minded, that they may make all I have commanded you. God's Spirit does not work through one person alone but through a team of skilled people.

verses.text: the Tent of Meeting, the ark of the testimony, the mercy seat that is on it, all the furniture of the Tent,
verses.explainer: The tent of meeting, the ark of the testimony, the mercy seat, and all the furnishings of the tent.

verses.text: the table and its vessels, the pure lamp stand with all its vessels, the altar of incense,
verses.explainer: The table and its utensils, the pure lampstand with all its utensils, the altar of incense.

verses.text: the altar of burnt offering with all its vessels, the basin and its base,
verses.explainer: The altar of burnt offering with all its utensils, the basin and its stand.

verses.text: the finely worked garments—the holy garments for Aaron the priest—the garments of his sons to minister in the priest’s office,
verses.explainer: The finely worked garments -- the sacred garments for Aaron the priest and the garments of his sons for their service as priests.

verses.text: the anointing oil, and the incense of sweet spices for the holy place: according to all that I have commanded you they shall do.”
verses.explainer: The anointing oil and the fragrant incense for the Holy Place. According to all I have commanded you, they shall do.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to Moses.

verses.text: “Speak also to the children of Israel, saying, ‘Most certainly you shall keep my Sabbaths: for it is a sign between me and you throughout your generations; that you may know that I am Yahweh who sanctifies you.
verses.explainer: Speak to the Israelites: Above all you shall keep my Sabbaths, for this is a sign between me and you throughout your generations, that you may know that I, the LORD, sanctify you. The Sabbath is the covenant sign -- like circumcision, it identifies God's people.

thenVsNow.then: The Sabbath placement here is deliberate: even the sacred work of building God's dwelling place must stop on the seventh day. If God rested from creating the universe, Israel must rest from building the Tabernacle.
thenVsNow.now: The principle challenges modern workaholic culture, even religious workaholic culture. No task -- however holy, urgent, or important -- overrides the need for rest. Rest is not a break from service; it is itself an act of obedience.

verses.text: You shall keep the Sabbath therefore; for it is holy to you. Everyone who profanes it shall surely be put to death; for whoever does any work therein, that soul shall be cut off from among his people.
verses.explainer: You shall keep the Sabbath, because it is holy for you. Everyone who profanes it shall be put to death. Whoever does any work on it, that soul shall be cut off from the people.

verses.text: Six days shall work be done, but on the seventh day is a Sabbath of solemn rest, holy to Yahweh. Whoever does any work on the Sabbath day shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: Six days shall work be done, but the seventh day is a Sabbath of solemn rest, holy to the LORD. Whoever does any work on the Sabbath day shall be put to death.

verses.text: Therefore the children of Israel shall keep the Sabbath, to observe the Sabbath throughout their generations, for a perpetual covenant.
verses.explainer: Therefore the Israelites shall keep the Sabbath, observing the Sabbath throughout their generations, as a covenant forever.

verses.text: It is a sign between me and the children of Israel forever; for in six days Yahweh made heaven and earth, and on the seventh day he rested, and was refreshed.’”
verses.explainer: It is a sign forever between me and the Israelites that in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, and on the seventh day he rested and was refreshed. The Hebrew says God was 'refreshed' (naphash) -- the same word used for catching one's breath. Even God models the restoration that rest brings.

verses.text: He gave to Moses, when he finished speaking with him on Mount Sinai, the two tablets of the testimony, stone tablets, written with God’s finger.
verses.explainer: When he finished speaking with Moses on Mount Sinai, he gave him the two tablets of the testimony, tablets of stone, written with the finger of God. God writes the law himself -- not dictated to Moses but inscribed by the divine hand.

explainer.summary: The greatest crisis in the Exodus story. While Moses is on the mountain receiving God's instructions for the Tabernacle, the people below grow impatient. They pressure Aaron into making a golden calf, declaring: 'These are your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of Egypt.' God tells Moses what has happened and threatens to destroy the people. Moses intercedes, reminding God of his promises to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. God relents. Moses comes down, sees the calf and the revelry, smashes the stone tablets, burns the calf, grinds it to powder, and makes the people drink it. Aaron gives a pathetic excuse. Moses calls for those loyal to the LORD, and the Levites execute about three thousand men.
themes: Idolatry as reducing God to a controllable image
God's mercy in response to intercession
explainer.contextInBook: The golden calf is the mirror image of the Tabernacle. Chapters 25-31 describe how God wants to dwell among his people; chapter 32 shows how the people want to worship God on their own terms. The calf is a human-made dwelling for God; the Tabernacle is a God-designed dwelling. The difference is everything.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Literally 'hard of neck' -- an agricultural metaph
wordStudies.meaning: Literally 'hard of neck' -- an agricultural metaphor for an ox that will not turn when the yoke pulls. A stiff-necked person refuses to be redirected by God. The term becomes God's characteristic description of Israel throughout the Old Testament.

verses.text: When the people saw that Moses delayed to come down from the mountain, the people gathered themselves together to Aaron, and said to him, “Come, make us gods, which shall go before us; for as for this Moses, the man who brought us up out of the land of Egypt, we don’t know what has become of him.”
verses.explainer: When the people see that Moses delays to come down from the mountain, they gather around Aaron and say: 'Up, make us gods who shall go before us. As for this Moses, the man who brought us up out of Egypt, we do not know what has become of him.' Forty days of waiting is too long. They want a visible god they can see and touch.

thenVsNow.then: The demand for a visible god reflects deep conditioning from four centuries in Egypt, where gods were everywhere in visible form. The Israelites could not sustain worship of an invisible God whose mediator had disappeared into a cloud.
thenVsNow.now: The impulse to make God visible, manageable, and under human control persists in every generation. We reduce God to ideologies, political programs, consumer products -- anything that makes the divine controllable and convenient.

verses.text: Aaron said to them, “Take off the golden rings, which are in the ears of your wives, of your sons, and of your daughters, and bring them to me.”
verses.explainer: Aaron says to them: Take off the rings of gold that are in the ears of your wives, sons, and daughters, and bring them to me. Aaron offers no resistance.

verses.text: All the people took off the golden rings which were in their ears, and brought them to Aaron.
verses.explainer: All the people take off their gold earrings and bring them to Aaron.

verses.text: He received what they handed him, fashioned it with an engraving tool, and made it a molten calf. Then they said, “These are your gods, Israel, which brought you up out of the land of Egypt.”
verses.explainer: He receives the gold, fashions it with a tool, and makes a golden calf. They say: 'These are your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt!' The calf was likely modeled on the Egyptian bull god Apis or the Canaanite deity represented by a bull. They credit an idol for what God did.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Golden calf', 'explanation': 'Bulls and calves were symbols of divine power throughout the ancient Near East. In Egypt, the Apis bull was a sacred animal associated with the creator god Ptah. In Canaan, the storm god Baal was often depicted standing on a bull. The golden calf was not an entirely new god but an attempt to worship YHWH through a familiar cultural form -- which is precisely what the second commandment forbids.'}

verses.text: When Aaron saw this, he built an altar before it; and Aaron made a proclamation, and said, “Tomorrow shall be a feast to Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: When Aaron sees this, he builds an altar before it and announces: 'Tomorrow shall be a feast to the LORD.' Aaron tries to redirect the idolatry toward YHWH -- as if putting God's name on an idol makes it acceptable. It does not.

verses.text: They rose up early on the next day, and offered burnt offerings, and brought peace offerings; and the people sat down to eat and to drink, and rose up to play.
verses.explainer: They rise early the next day, offer burnt offerings and peace offerings, and the people sit down to eat and drink and rise up to play. The 'play' (tsachaq) implies sexual revelry. The worship devolves into debauchery.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, “Go, get down; for your people, who you brought up out of the land of Egypt, have corrupted themselves!
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Moses: Go down, for your people, whom you brought up out of Egypt, have corrupted themselves. God distances himself: 'your people' -- not 'my people.'

verses.text: They have turned away quickly out of the way which I commanded them. They have made themselves a molten calf, and have worshiped it, and have sacrificed to it, and said, ‘These are your gods, Israel, which brought you up out of the land of Egypt.’”
verses.explainer: They have turned aside quickly out of the way I commanded them. They have made a golden calf, worshipped it, sacrificed to it, and said, 'These are your gods, O Israel, who brought you up from Egypt.'

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “I have seen these people, and behold, they are a stiff-necked people.
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: I have seen this people, and behold, it is a stiff-necked people.

verses.text: Now therefore leave me alone, that my wrath may burn hot against them, and that I may consume them; and I will make of you a great nation.”
verses.explainer: Now therefore let me alone, that my wrath may burn hot against them and I may consume them, in order that I may make a great nation of you. God offers to start over with Moses as the new Abraham.

verses.text: Moses begged Yahweh his God, and said, “Yahweh, why does your wrath burn hot against your people, that you have brought out of the land of Egypt with great power and with a mighty hand?
verses.explainer: But Moses implores the LORD his God: 'O LORD, why does your wrath burn hot against your people, whom you brought out of Egypt with great power and a mighty hand?' Moses corrects God's distancing language -- they are your people, not mine. You brought them out, not me.

verses.text: Why should the Egyptians speak, saying, ‘He brought them out for evil, to kill them in the mountains, and to consume them from the surface of the earth?’ Turn from your fierce wrath, and repent of this evil against your people.
verses.explainer: Why should the Egyptians say, "With evil intent did he bring them out, to kill them in the mountains?" Turn from your burning anger and relent from this disaster against your people. Moses appeals to God's reputation among the nations.

verses.text: Remember Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, your servants, to whom you swore by your own self, and said to them, ‘I will multiply your offspring as the stars of the sky, and all this land that I have spoken of I will give to your offspring, and they shall inherit it forever.’”
verses.explainer: Remember Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, your servants, to whom you swore by your own self: 'I will multiply your offspring as the stars of heaven, and all this land I will give to your offspring, and they shall inherit it forever.' Moses appeals to God's promises. You swore an oath -- by yourself.

verses.text: Yahweh repented of the evil which he said he would do to his people.
verses.explainer: And the LORD relented from the disaster that he had spoken of bringing on his people. The intercession succeeds. Moses' prayer changes the outcome.

verses.text: Moses turned, and went down from the mountain, with the two tablets of the testimony in his hand; tablets that were written on both their sides; on the one side and on the other they were written.
verses.explainer: Moses turns and goes down from the mountain with the two tablets of the testimony in his hands -- tablets written on both sides, on the front and on the back.

verses.text: The tablets were the work of God, and the writing was the writing of God, engraved on the tablets.
verses.explainer: The tablets were the work of God, and the writing was the writing of God, engraved on the tablets. Divine authorship, divine craftsmanship -- about to be shattered.

verses.text: When Joshua heard the noise of the people as they shouted, he said to Moses, “There is the noise of war in the camp.”
verses.explainer: When Joshua hears the noise of the people as they shout, he says to Moses: 'There is a noise of war in the camp.' Joshua mistakes revelry for battle.

verses.text: He said, “It isn’t the voice of those who shout for victory. It is not the voice of those who cry for being overcome; but the noise of those who sing that I hear.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: It is not the sound of shouting for victory, or the sound of the cry of defeat, but the sound of singing that I hear.

verses.text: As soon as he came near to the camp, he saw the calf and the dancing. Then Moses’ anger grew hot, and he threw the tablets out of his hands, and broke them beneath the mountain.
verses.explainer: As soon as he comes near the camp and sees the calf and the dancing, Moses' anger burns hot. He throws the tablets out of his hands and breaks them at the foot of the mountain. The smashing of the tablets is not a tantrum. The covenant has been broken by the people; the tablets merely make the breach visible.

thenVsNow.then: Breaking a treaty tablet in the ancient Near East was a formal declaration that the covenant was nullified. Moses' act is legal symbolism: Israel has violated the covenant, so the covenant document is destroyed.
thenVsNow.now: The broken tablets have become a powerful image for covenant violation. The gap between what God offers and what humanity does with it is as old as Sinai. But the story does not end with broken tablets -- God writes new ones.

verses.text: He took the calf which they had made, and burned it with fire, ground it to powder, and scattered it on the water, and made the children of Israel drink it.
verses.explainer: He takes the calf they had made, burns it with fire, grinds it to powder, scatters it on the water, and makes the Israelites drink it. Moses destroys the idol utterly and forces the people to consume their own sin.

verses.text: Moses said to Aaron, “What did these people do to you, that you have brought a great sin on them?”
verses.explainer: Moses says to Aaron: 'What did this people do to you that you have brought such a great sin upon them?'

verses.text: Aaron said, “Don’t let the anger of my lord grow hot. You know the people, that they are set on evil.
verses.explainer: Aaron says: 'Let not the anger of my lord burn hot. You know the people, that they are set on evil.'

verses.text: For they said to me, ‘Make us gods, which shall go before us. As for this Moses, the man who brought us up out of the land of Egypt, we don’t know what has become of him.’
verses.explainer: They said to me: 'Make us gods who shall go before us. As for this Moses who brought us up from Egypt, we do not know what has become of him.'

verses.text: I said to them, ‘Whoever has any gold, let them take it off:’ so they gave it to me; and I threw it into the fire, and out came this calf.”
verses.explainer: So I said to them: 'Let any who have gold take it off.' They gave it to me, and I threw it into the fire, and out came this calf. Aaron's excuse is absurd: the calf spontaneously formed itself in the fire. He takes no responsibility for the tool he used or the form he shaped.

verses.text: When Moses saw that the people had broken loose, (for Aaron had let them loose for a derision among their enemies),
verses.explainer: Moses sees that the people have broken loose (for Aaron had let them break loose, to the derision of their enemies).

verses.text:
then Moses stood in the gate of the camp, and said, “Whoever is on Yahweh’s side, come to me!”
All the sons of Levi gathered themselves together to him.
verses.explainer: Moses stands in the gate of the camp and says: 'Who is on the LORD's side? Come to me.' And all the sons of Levi gather around him. The Levites choose loyalty to God over tribal solidarity.

verses.text: He said to them, “Yahweh says, the God of Israel, ‘Every man put his sword on his thigh, and go back and forth from gate to gate throughout the camp, and every man kill his brother, and every man his companion, and every man his neighbor.’”
verses.explainer: He says to them: Thus says the LORD God of Israel: Each of you put his sword on his side and go back and forth from gate to gate throughout the camp, and each of you kill his brother and companion and neighbor.

verses.text: The sons of Levi did according to the word of Moses: and there fell of the people that day about three thousand men.
verses.explainer: The sons of Levi do as Moses commands, and about three thousand men fall that day. The consequences of covenant violation are severe.

verses.text: Moses said, “Consecrate yourselves today to Yahweh, yes, every man against his son, and against his brother; that he may give you a blessing today.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: Today you have ordained yourselves for the service of the LORD, each one at the cost of his son and his brother, so that he might bestow a blessing upon you this day. The Levites' loyalty earns them the priesthood.

verses.text: On the next day, Moses said to the people, “You have sinned a great sin. Now I will go up to Yahweh. Perhaps I shall make atonement for your sin.”
verses.explainer: The next day Moses tells the people: You have sinned a great sin. Now I will go up to the LORD. Perhaps I can make atonement for your sin.

verses.text: Moses returned to Yahweh, and said, “Oh, this people have sinned a great sin, and have made themselves gods of gold.
verses.explainer: Moses returns to the LORD and says: Alas, this people has sinned a great sin. They have made for themselves gods of gold.

verses.text: Yet now, if you will, forgive their sin—and if not, please blot me out of your book which you have written.”
verses.explainer: But now, if you will forgive their sin -- but if not, please blot me out of your book that you have written. Moses offers his own life for the people. He would rather be erased from God's book than see Israel destroyed.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Whoever has sinned against me, him will I blot out of my book.
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: Whoever has sinned against me, I will blot out of my book. God does not accept the substitutionary offer -- each person bears responsibility for their own sin.

verses.text: Now go, lead the people to the place of which I have spoken to you. Behold, my angel shall go before you. Nevertheless in the day when I punish, I will punish them for their sin.”
verses.explainer: But now, go, lead the people to the place about which I have spoken to you. My angel shall go before you. But in the day when I visit, I will visit their sin upon them.

verses.text: Yahweh struck the people, because they made the calf, which Aaron made.
verses.explainer: Then the LORD sent a plague on the people because they made the calf, the one that Aaron made. The consequences continue even after forgiveness. Sin has aftermath.

explainer.summary: God tells Moses to lead the people toward the Promised Land but says he will not go with them -- they are too stiff-necked and he might destroy them on the way. The people mourn. Moses sets up a tent of meeting outside the camp where he speaks with God face to face, 'as a man speaks to his friend.' Moses makes an extraordinary request: 'Show me your glory.' God agrees to pass by Moses, letting him see his back but not his face -- 'for man shall not see my face and live.'
themes: The tent of meeting as an interim sanctuary
The request to see God's glory
The limits of human capacity for the divine
explainer.contextInBook: After the golden calf, everything hangs in the balance. Will God continue with this people or abandon them? Moses' intercession reaches its most intimate and desperate point. The resolution sets up the covenant renewal in chapter 34.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Weight, substance, the visible manifestation of Go
wordStudies.meaning: Weight, substance, the visible manifestation of God's character and presence. When Moses asks to see God's glory, he is asking for the most complete revelation of God that a human being can receive. God responds by revealing his character -- his goodness, grace, mercy, and justice.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, “Depart, go up from here, you and the people that you have brought up out of the land of Egypt, to the land of which I swore to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, saying, ‘I will give it to your offspring.’
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: Depart; go up from here, you and the people whom you have brought up out of Egypt, to the land I swore to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob: 'To your offspring I will give it.'

verses.text: I will send an angel before you; and I will drive out the Canaanite, the Amorite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite:
verses.explainer: I will send an angel before you, and I will drive out the Canaanites, the Amorites, the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites.

verses.text: to a land flowing with milk and honey: for I will not go up among you, for you are a stiff-necked people, lest I consume you on the way.”
verses.explainer: Go to a land flowing with milk and honey; but I will not go up among you, lest I consume you on the way, for you are a stiff-necked people. God offers the land but withdraws his presence. The gift without the giver is empty.

thenVsNow.then: For Israel, God's absence was worse than any other punishment. The land meant nothing without God. Canaan without YHWH was just real estate -- the presence of God was what made any place the Promised Land.
thenVsNow.now: The distinction between God's gifts and God's presence remains critical. Prosperity, success, and comfort without a living relationship with God are empty. Moses understood what many miss: the blessing is the Blesser.

verses.text: When the people heard this evil news, they mourned: and no one put on his jewelry.
verses.explainer: When the people hear this disastrous word, they mourn, and no one puts on his ornaments. The people are genuinely grieved at the threat of God's absence.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Tell the children of Israel, ‘You are a stiff-necked people. If I were to go up among you for one moment, I would consume you. Therefore now take off your jewelry from you, that I may know what to do to you.’”
verses.explainer: The LORD says to the Israelites: You are a stiff-necked people. If for a single moment I should go up among you, I would consume you. So now take off your ornaments, that I may know what to do with you. God is deliberating.

verses.text: The children of Israel stripped themselves of their jewelry from Mount Horeb onward.
verses.explainer: Therefore the Israelites strip themselves of their ornaments, from Mount Horeb onward.

verses.text: Now Moses used to take the tent and pitch it outside the camp, far away from the camp, and he called it “The Tent of Meeting.” Everyone who sought Yahweh went out to the Tent of Meeting, which was outside the camp.
verses.explainer: Moses takes the tent and pitches it outside the camp, far off from the camp, and calls it the tent of meeting. Everyone who seeks the LORD goes out to the tent of meeting outside the camp. With God's presence withdrawn from the camp, Moses creates a meeting place outside it.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Tent of meeting outside the camp', 'explanation': "This tent is not the Tabernacle (which has not been built yet). It is an interim arrangement -- a simple tent where Moses meets with God after the golden calf crisis. Its location outside the camp symbolizes that God's presence has been pushed out by Israel's sin. It functions as a temporary embassy of heaven."}

verses.text: When Moses went out to the Tent, all the people rose up, and stood, everyone at their tent door, and watched Moses, until he had gone into the Tent.
verses.explainer: Whenever Moses goes out to the tent, all the people rise up, and each stands at his tent door and watches Moses until he has gone into the tent. The whole camp watches in silent reverence.

verses.text: When Moses entered into the Tent, the pillar of cloud descended, stood at the door of the Tent, and spoke with Moses.
verses.explainer: When Moses enters the tent, the pillar of cloud descends and stands at the entrance of the tent, and the LORD speaks with Moses.

verses.text: All the people saw the pillar of cloud stand at the door of the Tent, and all the people rose up and worshiped, everyone at their tent door.
verses.explainer: When all the people see the pillar of cloud standing at the entrance of the tent, they rise and worship, each at his tent door. The people worship from a distance.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend. He turned again into the camp, but his servant Joshua, the son of Nun, a young man, didn’t depart from the Tent.
verses.explainer: Thus the LORD used to speak to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend. When Moses turned again to the camp, his assistant Joshua the son of Nun, a young man, would not depart from the tent. The most intimate description of prayer in the Old Testament: face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.

verses.text: Moses said to Yahweh, “Behold, you tell me, ‘Bring up this people:’ and you haven’t let me know whom you will send with me. Yet you have said, ‘I know you by name, and you have also found favor in my sight.’
verses.explainer: Moses says to the LORD: See, you say to me, 'Bring up this people,' but you have not let me know whom you will send with me. Yet you have said, 'I know you by name, and you have found favor in my sight.' Moses presses God for clarity.

verses.text: Now therefore, if I have found favor in your sight, please show me now your way, that I may know you, so that I may find favor in your sight: and consider that this nation is your people.”
verses.explainer: Now therefore, if I have found favor in your sight, please show me now your ways, that I may know you in order to find favor in your sight. Consider too that this nation is your people. Moses insists: they are your people, not just mine.

verses.text: He said, “My presence will go with you, and I will give you rest.”
verses.explainer: The LORD says: My presence will go with you, and I will give you rest. God relents. His presence will accompany them after all.

verses.text: He said to him, “If your presence doesn’t go with me, don’t carry us up from here.
verses.explainer: Moses says: If your presence will not go with me, do not bring us up from here. Moses would rather stay in the wilderness with God than enter the Promised Land without him.

verses.text: For how would people know that I have found favor in your sight, I and your people? Isn’t it that you go with us, so that we are separated, I and your people, from all the people who are on the surface of the earth?”
verses.explainer: For how shall it be known that I have found favor in your sight, I and your people? Is it not in your going with us, so that we are distinct from every other people on the face of the earth? God's presence is what makes Israel different from every other nation.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “I will do this thing also that you have spoken; for you have found favor in my sight, and I know you by name.”
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: This very thing that you have spoken I will do, for you have found favor in my sight, and I know you by name. Full restoration.

verses.text: He said, “Please show me your glory.”
verses.explainer: Moses says: Please show me your glory. Having secured God's presence with the people, Moses asks for the deepest possible revelation of God himself.

verses.text: He said, “I will make all my goodness pass before you, and will proclaim Yahweh’s name before you. I will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and will show mercy on whom I will show mercy.”
verses.explainer: God says: I will make all my goodness pass before you and will proclaim before you my name 'The LORD.' I will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and will show mercy on whom I will show mercy. God reveals his glory not as blinding light but as goodness, grace, and mercy. His glory is his character.

verses.text: He said, “You cannot see my face, for man may not see me and live.”
verses.explainer: But he said: You cannot see my face, for man shall not see my face and live. The full, unmediated vision of God is beyond human capacity.

verses.text: Yahweh also said, “Behold, there is a place by me, and you shall stand on the rock.
verses.explainer: The LORD says: There is a place by me where you shall stand on the rock.

verses.text: It will happen, while my glory passes by, that I will put you in a cleft of the rock, and will cover you with my hand until I have passed by;
verses.explainer: While my glory passes by I will put you in a cleft of the rock, and I will cover you with my hand until I have passed by. God protects Moses from the full force of his glory by hiding him in the rock.

verses.text: then I will take away my hand, and you will see my back; but my face shall not be seen.”
verses.explainer: Then I will take away my hand, and you shall see my back, but my face shall not be seen. Moses sees the afterglow of God's passing -- the trailing edge of glory. Even this partial vision will transform him (34:29).

explainer.summary: God tells Moses to cut two new stone tablets and come up the mountain alone. There God passes before Moses and proclaims his name -- the most complete self-description of God in the Old Testament: 'The LORD, the LORD, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness, keeping steadfast love for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin, but who will by no means clear the guilty.' Moses worships. God renews the covenant with expanded commands. When Moses comes down, his face shines so brightly that the people are afraid, and he must wear a veil.
themes: Grace and justice held together in God's nature
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 34 is the theological heart of the entire Bible's understanding of God's character. The declaration in verses 6-7 is quoted or echoed more than any other passage in the Old Testament. After the golden calf, God does not merely forgive -- he reveals who he fundamentally is.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Covenant faithfulness, loyal love, lovingkindness
wordStudies.meaning: Covenant faithfulness, loyal love, lovingkindness. Chesed is the Hebrew word that best captures God's essential character. It describes a love that is not based on the worthiness of the recipient but on the faithfulness of the giver. God's chesed persists even when his people are faithless.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To send out rays, to shine, to have horns
wordStudies.meaning: To send out rays, to shine, to have horns. Moses' face radiated light after being in God's presence. The same root means 'horn,' which is why some ancient translations (and Michelangelo's famous sculpture) depicted Moses with horns. He was radiant, not horned.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Chisel two stone tablets like the first. I will write on the tablets the words that were on the first tablets, which you broke.
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: Cut two tablets of stone like the first, and I will write on the tablets the words that were on the first tablets, which you broke. New tablets for a new beginning. God does not change his word; he writes it again.

thenVsNow.then: The first tablets were entirely God's work -- he made them and wrote on them (31:18). The second tablets require Moses to cut the stone while God writes. Renewal after failure involves both divine grace and human effort.
thenVsNow.now: The second set of tablets represents the biblical principle of second chances. Failure is not final. The law remains the same, but the relationship includes the scars of the crisis and the healing of restoration.

verses.text: Be ready by the morning, and come up in the morning to Mount Sinai, and present yourself there to me on the top of the mountain.
verses.explainer: Be ready by the morning and come up in the morning to Mount Sinai. Present yourself to me on the top of the mountain.

verses.text: No one shall come up with you or be seen anywhere on the mountain. Do not let the flocks or herds graze in front of that mountain.”
verses.explainer: No one shall come up with you, and let no one be seen throughout all the mountain. Let no flocks or herds graze opposite the mountain. Complete solitude for this encounter.

verses.text: He chiseled two tablets of stone like the first; and Moses rose up early in the morning, and went up to Mount Sinai, as Yahweh had commanded him, and took in his hand two stone tablets.
verses.explainer: Moses cuts two tablets of stone like the first. He rises early in the morning and goes up on Mount Sinai, as the LORD had commanded, with the two tablets of stone in his hand.

verses.text: Yahweh descended in the cloud, and stood with him there, and proclaimed Yahweh’s name.
verses.explainer: The LORD descends in the cloud and stands with him there, and proclaims the name of the LORD. God shows up and speaks his own name -- his character, his identity.

verses.text: Yahweh passed by before him, and proclaimed, “Yahweh! Yahweh, a merciful and gracious God, slow to anger, and abundant in loving kindness and truth,
verses.explainer: The LORD passes before him and proclaims: 'The LORD, the LORD, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness.' This is the single most important theological statement in the Old Testament. God defines himself -- and the definition begins with mercy, not power.

verses.text: keeping loving kindness for thousands, forgiving iniquity and disobedience and sin; and who will by no means clear the guilty, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children, and on the children’s children, on the third and on the fourth generation.”
verses.explainer: Keeping steadfast love for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin, but who will by no means clear the guilty, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children and children's children, to the third and fourth generation. God holds mercy and justice together. He forgives but does not pretend sin has no consequences.

verses.text: Moses hurried and bowed his head toward the earth, and worshiped.
verses.explainer: Moses quickly bows his head toward the earth and worships. The proper response to God's self-revelation is worship.

verses.text: He said, “If now I have found favor in your sight, Lord, please let the Lord go among us; although this is a stiff-necked people; pardon our iniquity and our sin, and take us for your inheritance.”
verses.explainer: He says: If now I have found favor in your sight, O Lord, please let the Lord go in the midst of us, although it is a stiff-necked people, and pardon our iniquity and our sin, and take us for your inheritance. Moses uses God's own self-description as the basis for his request.

verses.text: He said, “Behold, I make a covenant: before all your people I will do marvels, such as have not been worked in all the earth, nor in any nation; and all the people among which you are shall see the work of Yahweh; for it is an awesome thing that I do with you.
verses.explainer: God says: Behold, I am making a covenant. Before all your people I will do marvels, such as have not been created in all the earth or in any nation. All the people among whom you are shall see the work of the LORD, for it is an awesome thing that I will do with you.

verses.text: Observe that which I command you today. Behold, I drive out before you the Amorite, the Canaanite, the Hittite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite.
verses.explainer: Observe what I command you this day. Behold, I will drive out before you the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites.

verses.text: Be careful, lest you make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land where you are going, lest it be for a snare among you:
verses.explainer: Take care, lest you make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land to which you go, lest it become a snare in your midst.

verses.text: but you shall break down their altars, and dash in pieces their pillars, and you shall cut down their Asherah poles;
verses.explainer: You shall tear down their altars, break their pillars, and cut down their Asherim.

verses.text: for you shall worship no other god: for Yahweh, whose name is Jealous, is a jealous God.
verses.explainer: For you shall worship no other god, for the LORD, whose name is Jealous, is a jealous God. God's name is Jealous -- his love for his people is exclusive and fierce.

verses.text: “Don’t make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land, lest they play the prostitute after their gods, and sacrifice to their gods, and one call you and you eat of his sacrifice;
verses.explainer: Lest you make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land, and when they whore after their gods and sacrifice to them, they invite you, and you eat of his sacrifice.

verses.text: and you take of their daughters to your sons, and their daughters play the prostitute after their gods, and make your sons play the prostitute after their gods.
verses.explainer: And you take of their daughters for your sons, and their daughters whore after their gods and make your sons whore after their gods.

verses.text: “You shall make no cast idols for yourselves.
verses.explainer: You shall not make for yourself any gods of cast metal. The golden calf prohibition restated with emphasis.

verses.text: “You shall keep the feast of unleavened bread. Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread, as I commanded you, at the time appointed in the month Abib; for in the month Abib you came out of Egypt.
verses.explainer: You shall keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread. Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread, as I commanded you, at the time appointed in the month Abib, for in the month Abib you came out from Egypt.

verses.text: “All that opens the womb is mine; and all your livestock that is male, the firstborn of cow and sheep.
verses.explainer: All that open the womb are mine, all your male livestock, the firstborn of cow and sheep.

verses.text: You shall redeem the firstborn of a donkey with a lamb. If you will not redeem it, then you shall break its neck. You shall redeem all the firstborn of your sons. No one shall appear before me empty.
verses.explainer: The firstborn of a donkey you shall redeem with a lamb, or if you will not redeem it you shall break its neck. All the firstborn of your sons you shall redeem. And none shall appear before me empty-handed.

verses.text: “Six days you shall work, but on the seventh day you shall rest: in plowing time and in harvest you shall rest.
verses.explainer: Six days you shall work, but on the seventh day you shall rest. In plowing time and in harvest you shall rest. Even during the busiest agricultural seasons, rest is required.

verses.text: “You shall observe the feast of weeks with the first fruits of wheat harvest, and the feast of harvest at the year’s end.
verses.explainer: You shall observe the Feast of Weeks, the firstfruits of wheat harvest, and the Feast of Ingathering at the turn of the year.

verses.text: Three times in the year all your males shall appear before the Lord Yahweh, the God of Israel.
verses.explainer: Three times in the year shall all your males appear before the LORD God, the God of Israel.

verses.text: For I will drive out nations before you and enlarge your borders; neither shall any man desire your land when you go up to appear before Yahweh, your God, three times in the year.
verses.explainer: For I will cast out nations before you and enlarge your borders. No one shall covet your land when you go up to appear before the LORD your God three times in the year. God promises to protect their land during the pilgrimages.

verses.text: “You shall not offer the blood of my sacrifice with leavened bread. The sacrifice of the feast of the Passover shall not be left to the morning.
verses.explainer: You shall not offer the blood of my sacrifice with anything leavened, or let the sacrifice of the Feast of the Passover remain until the morning.

verses.text:
“You shall bring the first of the first fruits of your ground to the house of Yahweh your God.
“You shall not boil a young goat in its mother’s milk.”
verses.explainer: The best of the firstfruits of your ground you shall bring to the house of the LORD your God. You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Write you these words: for in accordance with these words I have made a covenant with you and with Israel.”
verses.explainer: The LORD says to Moses: Write these words, for in accordance with these words I have made a covenant with you and with Israel. The covenant is documented.

verses.text: He was there with Yahweh forty days and forty nights; he neither ate bread, nor drank water. He wrote on the tablets the words of the covenant, the ten commandments.
verses.explainer: Moses was there with the LORD forty days and forty nights. He neither ate bread nor drank water. And he wrote on the tablets the words of the covenant, the Ten Commandments. Another forty days on the mountain. Sustained by divine presence alone.

verses.text: When Moses came down from Mount Sinai with the two tablets of the testimony in Moses’ hand, when he came down from the mountain, Moses didn’t know that the skin of his face shone by reason of his speaking with him.
verses.explainer: When Moses comes down from Mount Sinai with the two tablets of the testimony in his hand, he does not know that the skin of his face shines because he has been talking with God. Prolonged exposure to God's presence transforms the person. Moses glows and does not know it.

verses.text: When Aaron and all the children of Israel saw Moses, behold, the skin of his face shone; and they were afraid to come near him.
verses.explainer: When Aaron and all the Israelites see Moses, the skin of his face is shining, and they are afraid to come near him.

verses.text: Moses called to them, and Aaron and all the rulers of the congregation returned to him; and Moses spoke to them.
verses.explainer: Moses calls to them. Aaron and all the leaders come back to him, and Moses talks with them.

verses.text: Afterward all the children of Israel came near, and he gave them all the commandments that Yahweh had spoken with him on Mount Sinai.
verses.explainer: Afterward all the Israelites come near, and he commands them all that the LORD has spoken with him on Mount Sinai.

verses.text: When Moses was done speaking with them, he put a veil on his face.
verses.explainer: When Moses finishes speaking with them, he puts a veil over his face.

verses.text: But when Moses went in before Yahweh to speak with him, he took the veil off, until he came out; and he came out, and spoke to the children of Israel that which he was commanded.
verses.explainer: Whenever Moses goes in before the LORD to speak with him, he removes the veil until he comes out. He comes out and tells the Israelites what he is commanded.

verses.text: The children of Israel saw Moses’ face, that the skin of Moses’ face shone: and Moses put the veil on his face again, until he went in to speak with him.
verses.explainer: The Israelites see that the skin of Moses' face is shining. Moses puts the veil back on his face until he goes in to speak with the LORD. The veil becomes a permanent feature of Moses' ministry -- the people cannot bear unfiltered glory.

explainer.summary: Moses assembles the entire congregation and repeats the Sabbath command, then invites voluntary contributions for building the Tabernacle. The people respond with overwhelming generosity -- everyone whose heart stirs brings gold, silver, bronze, yarns, linen, skins, wood, oil, spices, and precious stones. The willing-hearted bring so much that Moses will eventually have to tell them to stop (36:6). Bezalel and Oholiab are confirmed as the master craftsmen.
themes: Sabbath rest restated after the golden calf
The community participating in building God's dwelling
explainer.contextInBook: After the crisis of the golden calf and the renewal of the covenant, the building of the Tabernacle begins. The golden calf was funded by the people's gold given reluctantly (or demanded by Aaron). The Tabernacle is funded by gold given voluntarily and joyfully. Same material, opposite spirit.

verses.text: Moses assembled all the congregation of the children of Israel, and said to them, “These are the words which Yahweh has commanded, that you should do them.
verses.explainer: Moses assembles all the congregation of the Israelites and says to them: These are the things the LORD has commanded you to do.

verses.text: ‘Six days shall work be done, but on the seventh day there shall be a holy day for you, a Sabbath of solemn rest to Yahweh: whoever does any work in it shall be put to death.
verses.explainer: Six days work shall be done, but on the seventh day you shall have a Sabbath of solemn rest, holy to the LORD. Whoever does any work on it shall be put to death. The Sabbath is restated first, before any construction begins.

verses.text: You shall kindle no fire throughout your habitations on the Sabbath day.’”
verses.explainer: You shall kindle no fire in all your dwelling places on the Sabbath day.

verses.text: Moses spoke to all the congregation of the children of Israel, saying, “This is the thing which Yahweh commanded, saying,
verses.explainer: Moses says to all the congregation: This is the thing the LORD has commanded.

verses.text: ‘Take from among you an offering to Yahweh. Whoever is of a willing heart, let him bring it, Yahweh’s offering: gold, silver, brass,
verses.explainer: Take from among you a contribution to the LORD. Whoever is of a generous heart, let him bring the LORD's contribution: gold, silver, and bronze. Generosity is voluntary, not compulsory. The heart must be willing.

thenVsNow.then: The Tabernacle is funded entirely by voluntary donations. No tax, no requirement, no minimum. God wants willing hearts, not grudging compliance.
thenVsNow.now: The principle of voluntary giving remains central to Jewish and Christian tradition. Paul echoes this in 2 Corinthians 9:7: 'Each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver.'

verses.text: blue, purple, scarlet, fine linen, goats’ hair,
verses.explainer: Blue, purple, and scarlet yarns and fine linen, goats' hair.

verses.text: rams’ skins dyed red, sea cow hides, acacia wood,
verses.explainer: Tanned rams' skins, goatskins, acacia wood.

verses.text: oil for the light, spices for the anointing oil and for the sweet incense,
verses.explainer: Oil for the light, spices for the anointing oil and for the fragrant incense.

verses.text: onyx stones, and stones to be set for the ephod and for the breastplate.
verses.explainer: Onyx stones and stones for setting, for the ephod and for the breastpiece.

verses.text: “‘Let every wise-hearted man among you come, and make all that Yahweh has commanded:
verses.explainer: Let every skillful craftsman among you come and make all that the LORD has commanded.

verses.text: the tabernacle, its outer covering, its roof, its clasps, its boards, its bars, its pillars, and its sockets;
verses.explainer: The tabernacle, its tent and its covering, its clasps and frames, its bars, pillars, and bases.

verses.text: the ark, and its poles, the mercy seat, the veil of the screen;
verses.explainer: The ark with its poles, the mercy seat, and the veil of the screen.

verses.text: the table with its poles and all its vessels, and the show bread;
verses.explainer: The table with its poles and all its utensils, and the bread of the Presence.

verses.text: the lamp stand also for the light, with its vessels, its lamps, and the oil for the light;
verses.explainer: The lampstand for the light, with its utensils and its lamps, and the oil for the light.

verses.text: and the altar of incense with its poles, the anointing oil, the sweet incense, the screen for the door, at the door of the tabernacle;
verses.explainer: The altar of incense with its poles, the anointing oil and the fragrant incense, and the screen for the entrance at the door of the tabernacle.

verses.text: the altar of burnt offering, with its grating of brass, it poles, and all its vessels, the basin and its base;
verses.explainer: The altar of burnt offering with its grating of bronze, its poles, and all its utensils, the basin and its stand.

verses.text: the hangings of the court, its pillars, their sockets, and the screen for the gate of the court;
verses.explainer: The hangings of the court, its pillars and its bases, and the screen for the gate of the court.

verses.text: the pins of the tabernacle, the pins of the court, and their cords;
verses.explainer: The pegs of the tabernacle and the pegs of the court, and their cords.

verses.text: the finely worked garments, for ministering in the holy place, the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons, to minister in the priest’s office.’”
verses.explainer: The finely worked garments for ministering in the Holy Place -- the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons for their service as priests.

verses.text: All the congregation of the children of Israel departed from the presence of Moses.
verses.explainer: Then all the congregation of the Israelites depart from the presence of Moses.

verses.text: They came, everyone whose heart stirred him up, and everyone whom his spirit made willing, and brought Yahweh’s offering, for the work of the Tent of Meeting, and for all of its service, and for the holy garments.
verses.explainer: They come, everyone whose heart stirs him, and everyone whose spirit moves him, and bring the LORD's contribution for the work of the tent of meeting. The movement is from the inside out -- the heart stirs, the spirit moves, the hands bring.

verses.text: They came, both men and women, as many as were willing-hearted, and brought brooches, earrings, signet rings, and armlets, all jewels of gold; even every man who offered an offering of gold to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: They come, both men and women. All who are willing-hearted bring brooches, earrings, rings, and ornaments, all sorts of gold objects.

verses.text: Everyone, with whom was found blue, purple, scarlet, fine linen, goats’ hair, rams’ skins dyed red, and sea cow hides, brought them.
verses.explainer: Everyone who has blue, purple, or scarlet yarns, fine linen, goats' hair, tanned rams' skins, or goatskins brings them.

verses.text: Everyone who offered an offering of silver and brass brought Yahweh’s offering; and everyone, with whom was found acacia wood for any work of the service, brought it.
verses.explainer: Everyone who can make a contribution of silver or bronze brings it as the LORD's contribution. Everyone who has acacia wood for any work brings it.

verses.text: All the women who were wise-hearted spun with their hands, and brought that which they had spun, the blue, the purple, the scarlet, and the fine linen.
verses.explainer: All the skillful women spin with their hands and bring what they have spun in blue, purple, and scarlet yarns and fine linen.

verses.text: All the women whose heart stirred them up in wisdom spun the goats’ hair.
verses.explainer: All the women whose hearts stir them with skill spin the goats' hair.

verses.text: The rulers brought the onyx stones and the stones to be set for the ephod and for the breastplate;
verses.explainer: The leaders bring onyx stones and stones for setting, for the ephod and breastpiece.

verses.text: with the spice and the oil for the light, for the anointing oil, and for the sweet incense.
verses.explainer: And spices and oil for the light, for the anointing oil, and for the fragrant incense.

verses.text: The children of Israel brought a freewill offering to Yahweh; every man and woman, whose heart made them willing to bring for all the work, which Yahweh had commanded to be made by Moses.
verses.explainer: All the men and women of the Israelites whose heart moves them bring a freewill offering to the LORD for the work the LORD has commanded by Moses.

verses.text: Moses said to the children of Israel, “Behold, Yahweh has called by name Bezalel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah.
verses.explainer: Moses says to the Israelites: See, the LORD has called by name Bezalel the son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah.

verses.text: He has filled him with the Spirit of God, in wisdom, in understanding, in knowledge, and in all kinds of workmanship;
verses.explainer: He has filled him with the Spirit of God, with skill, intelligence, knowledge, and all craftsmanship.

verses.text: and to make skillful works, to work in gold, in silver, in brass,
verses.explainer: To devise artistic designs, to work in gold, silver, and bronze.

verses.text: in cutting of stones for setting, and in carving of wood, to work in all kinds of skillful workmanship.
verses.explainer: In cutting stones for setting and in carving wood, for work in every skilled craft.

verses.text: He has put in his heart that he may teach, both he, and Oholiab, the son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan.
verses.explainer: And he has inspired him to teach, both him and Oholiab the son of Ahisamach of the tribe of Dan. The Spirit-filled artist is also a teacher -- passing on skill to others.

verses.text: He has filled them with wisdom of heart, to work all kinds of workmanship, of the engraver, of the skillful workman, and of the embroiderer, in blue, in purple, in scarlet, and in fine linen, and of the weaver, even of those who do any workmanship, and of those who make skillful works.
verses.explainer: He has filled them with skill to do every sort of work done by an engraver, a designer, an embroiderer in blue, purple, and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen, and by a weaver -- by any sort of workman or skilled designer.

explainer.summary: Bezalel, Oholiab, and every craftsman whose heart God has stirred begin the work. The people continue bringing freewill offerings every morning until the craftsmen report to Moses: the people are bringing much more than enough. Moses issues a command to stop giving -- they have too much material. The construction of the Tabernacle curtains, frames, and coverings begins, following exactly the pattern God showed Moses on the mountain.
themes: Faithful execution of the divine plan
explainer.contextInBook: The surplus of offerings is the antithesis of the golden calf. Where the calf represented Israel at its worst, the Tabernacle offerings represent Israel at its best -- so generous that they must be told to stop. The construction follows the pattern of chapters 25-30 in reverse order.

verses.text: “Bezalel and Oholiab shall work with every wise-hearted man, in whom Yahweh has put wisdom and understanding to know how to do all the work for the service of the sanctuary, according to all that Yahweh has commanded.”
verses.explainer: Bezalel and Oholiab and every craftsman in whom the LORD has put skill and intelligence to know how to do any work in the construction of the sanctuary shall work in accordance with all that the LORD has commanded.

verses.text: Moses called Bezalel and Oholiab, and every wise-hearted man, in whose heart Yahweh had put wisdom, even everyone whose heart stirred him up to come to the work to do it:
verses.explainer: Moses calls Bezalel and Oholiab and every craftsman in whose mind the LORD has put skill, everyone whose heart stirs him up to come and do the work.

verses.text: and they received from Moses all the offering which the children of Israel had brought for the work of the service of the sanctuary, with which to make it. They brought yet to him freewill offerings every morning.
verses.explainer: They receive from Moses all the contribution the Israelites have brought for doing the work on the sanctuary. They still bring freewill offerings every morning.

verses.text: All the wise men, who performed all the work of the sanctuary, each came from his work which they did.
verses.explainer: So all the craftsmen who are doing every sort of task on the sanctuary come, each from the task he is doing.

verses.text: They spoke to Moses, saying, “The people bring much more than enough for the service of the work which Yahweh commanded to make.”
verses.explainer: They say to Moses: The people bring much more than enough for doing the work the LORD has commanded us to do. The workers tell Moses the impossible: we have too much.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, construction projects were funded by taxation and forced labor. A project funded entirely by voluntary donations that exceed what is needed was extraordinary. It demonstrated the genuine transformation of Israel's heart after the golden calf crisis.
thenVsNow.now: The idea of a congregation giving so generously that leadership must tell them to stop remains the dream of every faith community. It happens when hearts are genuinely stirred by God rather than pressured by human systems.

verses.text: Moses gave commandment, and they caused it to be proclaimed throughout the camp, saying, “Let neither man nor woman make anything else for the offering for the sanctuary.” So the people were restrained from bringing.
verses.explainer: Moses gives a command and word is sent throughout the camp: Let no man or woman do anything more for the contribution for the sanctuary. So the people are restrained from bringing. The only fundraising campaign in history that had to be shut down for excess.

verses.text: For the stuff they had was sufficient for all the work to make it, and too much.
verses.explainer: For the material they had was sufficient to do all the work, and more. Sufficiency and surplus -- God's provision through human generosity.

verses.text: All the wise-hearted men among those who did the work made the tabernacle with ten curtains; of fine twined linen, blue, purple, and scarlet, with cherubim, the work of the skillful workman, they made them.
verses.explainer: All the craftsmen among the workers make the tabernacle with ten curtains of fine twined linen and blue, purple, and scarlet yarns, with cherubim skillfully worked.

verses.text: The length of each curtain was twenty-eight cubits, and the width of each curtain four cubits. All the curtains had one measure.
verses.explainer: The length of each curtain is twenty-eight cubits, and the breadth four cubits. All the curtains are the same size.

verses.text: He coupled five curtains to one another, and the other five curtains he coupled to one another.
verses.explainer: He couples five curtains to one another, and the other five curtains to one another.

verses.text: He made loops of blue on the edge of the one curtain from the edge in the coupling. Likewise he made in the edge of the curtain that was outermost in the second coupling.
verses.explainer: He makes loops of blue on the edge of the outermost curtain of the first set. Likewise on the edge of the outermost curtain of the second set.

verses.text: He made fifty loops in the one curtain, and he made fifty loops in the edge of the curtain that was in the second coupling. The loops were opposite to one another.
verses.explainer: He makes fifty loops on one curtain and fifty on the second, with the loops opposite one another.

verses.text: He made fifty clasps of gold, and coupled the curtains to one another with the clasps: so the tabernacle was a unit.
verses.explainer: He makes fifty clasps of gold and couples the curtains one to the other with clasps, so the tabernacle becomes a single whole.

verses.text: He made curtains of goats’ hair for a covering over the tabernacle. He made them eleven curtains.
verses.explainer: He makes curtains of goats' hair for a tent over the tabernacle. He makes eleven curtains.

verses.text: The length of each curtain was thirty cubits, and four cubits the width of each curtain. The eleven curtains had one measure.
verses.explainer: The length of each curtain is thirty cubits and the breadth four cubits. The eleven curtains are the same size.

verses.text: He coupled five curtains by themselves, and six curtains by themselves.
verses.explainer: He couples five curtains by themselves and six by themselves.

verses.text: He made fifty loops on the edge of the curtain that was outermost in the coupling, and he made fifty loops on the edge of the curtain which was outermost in the second coupling.
verses.explainer: He makes fifty loops on the edge of the outermost curtain of the one set and fifty on the edge of the other.

verses.text: He made fifty clasps of brass to couple the tent together, that it might be a unit.
verses.explainer: He makes fifty clasps of bronze to couple the tent together as one whole.

verses.text: He made a covering for the tent of rams’ skins dyed red, and a covering of sea cow hides above.
verses.explainer: He makes a covering for the tent of tanned rams' skins and goatskins.

verses.text: He made the boards for the tabernacle of acacia wood, standing up.
verses.explainer: He makes the upright frames for the tabernacle of acacia wood.

verses.text: Ten cubits was the length of a board, and a cubit and a half the width of each board.
verses.explainer: Ten cubits the length of a frame, and a cubit and a half the breadth of each frame.

verses.text: Each board had two tenons, joined to one another. He made all the boards of the tabernacle this way.
verses.explainer: Each frame has two tenons for fitting together. He does this for all the frames.

verses.text: He made the boards for the tabernacle: twenty boards for the south side southward.
verses.explainer: He makes the frames for the tabernacle: twenty frames for the south side.

verses.text: He made forty sockets of silver under the twenty boards; two sockets under one board for its two tenons, and two sockets under another board for its two tenons.
verses.explainer: He makes forty bases of silver under the twenty frames -- two bases under each frame.

verses.text: For the second side of the tabernacle, on the north side, he made twenty boards,
verses.explainer: For the second side of the tabernacle, on the north side, he makes twenty frames.

verses.text: and their forty sockets of silver; two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board.
verses.explainer: And their forty bases of silver -- two bases under each frame.

verses.text: For the far part of the tabernacle westward he made six boards.
verses.explainer: For the rear of the tabernacle westward he makes six frames.

verses.text: He made two boards for the corners of the tabernacle in the far part.
verses.explainer: He makes two frames for the corners of the rear.

verses.text: They were double beneath, and in the same way they were all the way to its top to one ring. He did this to both of them in the two corners.
verses.explainer: They are separate beneath but joined at the top at the first ring. He does this for both corners.

verses.text: There were eight boards, and their sockets of silver, sixteen sockets; under every board two sockets.
verses.explainer: There are eight frames with their bases of silver -- sixteen bases, two under each frame.

verses.text: He made bars of acacia wood; five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle,
verses.explainer: He makes bars of acacia wood: five for the frames of one side of the tabernacle.

verses.text: and five bars for the boards of the other side of the tabernacle, and five bars for the boards of the tabernacle for the hinder part westward.
verses.explainer: Five bars for the frames of the other side, and five bars for the rear westward.

verses.text: He made the middle bar to pass through in the middle of the boards from the one end to the other.
verses.explainer: He makes the middle bar to run from end to end halfway up the frames.

verses.text: He overlaid the boards with gold, and made their rings of gold as places for the bars, and overlaid the bars with gold.
verses.explainer: He overlays the frames with gold and makes their rings of gold as holders for the bars. He overlays the bars with gold.

verses.text: He made the veil of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen: with cherubim. He made it the work of a skillful workman.
verses.explainer: He makes the veil of blue, purple, and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen, with cherubim skillfully worked into it. The veil separating God's inner dwelling from the Holy Place.
